places.name: A Coruna

visits.text: The Tower of Hercules—the world's only Roman lighthouse still in operation—rises 180 feet above the Atlantic. Its 112-foot Roman masonry core dates to the 1st century AD.
Climb the 234 steps to the summit. The UNESCO site features a sculpture park and ancient petroglyphs carved into the Monte dos Bicos rocks.
The Paseo Marítimo stretches eight miles along the Atlantic coast, linking Riazor and Orzán beaches to the Tower of Hercules.
The Aquarium Finisterrae showcases Atlantic sharks and seals in the Nautilus Room—a 1.2-million-gallon pool submerged in the ocean itself.
The glass-enclosed balconies of the Avenida de la Marina, built between 1870 and 1884, earned A Coruña its nickname: the City of Glass.
The Domus, designed by Arata Isozaki, offers interactive exhibits on the human body perched dramatically above the crashing Atlantic waves.

activities.text: Swim at Playa de Orzán, a popular urban surf spot with nearly a mile of protected sand and consistent Atlantic swells.
The seafood stalls on Rúa Pescadería sell percebes, razor clams, and scallops pulled fresh from the ocean. Pair with crisp Albariño.
Rent a bike and cruise the seafront promenade from Riazor all the way to the sculpture park at the Tower of Hercules.

places.name: Aguilas

visits.text: The Castillo de San Juan de las Águilas has watched over the bay since 1579. The 18th-century fortress offers 360° panoramas of the harbor and the Costa Cálida coastline.

28 kilometers of coastline carve out urban beaches and wild coves. The Cuatro Calas—La Carolina, Los Cocedores, La Higuerica, La Calarreona—display dramatic geological formations and crystal-clear waters.

The Carnival Museum traces 200 years of celebration. Costumes from the comparsas (dance troupes), miniature floats, and the story of the Mussona mascot. Águilas' carnival is recognized as an event of international tourist interest.
The 2nd-century Roman baths preserve mosaics, a caldarium, and frigidarium. Remnants of the ancient port of Urci.
The Embarcadero del Hornillo, an 1890 iron pier, once shipped mineral ore to England across the Mediterranean.

activities.text: Kayak along volcanic cliffs at dawn. Paddle to Cala Amarilla, Isla del Fraile, and sea caves carved by millennia of waves.
Snorkel in the coves. Basalt rocks, parrotfish, octopuses. Calm waters, rocky seabed teeming with life.
Climb to the hermitage for sunset. Low light gilds the sierras, the Mediterranean turns molten gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida

map_keywords: Castillo San Juan de las Águilas

places.name: Alba De Tormes

visits.text: Alba de Tormes is inseparable from Saint Teresa of Ávila. She died here on October 4, 1582, exhausted from a final journey from Burgos, in the Carmelite convent she had founded in 1571. Her body—declared incorruptible at the three tomb openings in 1750, 1914, and 2024—rests in the Church of the Annunciation, in a carved marble urn above the main altar. You can see two reliquaries displaying her heart and left arm. John Paul II came on pilgrimage here November 1, 1982, for the fourth centenary of the saint's death.

The Theresian Basilica, built in 1896 on orders of the Bishop of Salamanca to accommodate the influx of pilgrims, is Alba de Tormes's most visible religious building from the road. Its tower measures 34.6 meters. It's the heart of Marian pilgrimage in Castile and León. The combined visit with the Carmelite Museum (CARMUS)—installed in the convent's renovated cloister—offers the most complete historical and spiritual context on Teresa and John of the Cross.

The Castle of Alba, whose watchtower has guarded the town since the 12th century, symbolizes the Duchy of Alba de Tormes—one of Spain's great noble houses. In 1472, García Álvarez de Toledo obtained the title of Duke of Alba and made Alba a major cultural center of the Golden Age, attracting poets and thinkers. The El Espolón park laid out around the old fortress offers fine views over the Tormes.

The Church of San Juan, built in the 12th-13th centuries, is classified as a National Monument. Its Romanesque-Mudéjar style and the coexistence of brick and stone make it an interesting monument. It preserves the three apses of the original chevet. It's also the first church in the world dedicated to Saint John of the Cross—friend of Teresa of Ávila and co-reformer of the Carmelites.

activities.text: Walk along the banks of the Tormes below the castle—this is where Teresa loved to pray, drawn by the view of the river from her convent. The medieval bridge and old ruined mills on the Tormes compose a calm, photogenic landscape.

At the table, look for rosquillas de Ledesma—sweet doughnuts—and bollo maimón, a soft egg cake found in bakeries throughout Salamanca province. Alba restaurants also serve cabrito (kid goat) and roast lamb.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

Musée thérésien CARMUS Alba de Tormes

visits.text: Albarracín appears around a bend in the Guadalaviar, clinging to a rose sandstone cliff at 1,100 meters elevation. Declared a National Monument in 1961, this village of 1,000 inhabitants is considered one of Spain's most beautiful. Its facades of reddish rodeno sandstone and wooden balconies create a fairytale medieval atmosphere.

Albarracín's ramparts extend across an area far larger than the village itself. Three construction phases can be read in the stone: the 10th-century bastion and Torre del Andador, the 11th-century walls of the Berber taifa of Banu Razin, then Christian fortifications after the Reconquest. From atop the Torre del Andador, the panorama embraces the entire valley.

The Cathedral of El Salvador, built in the 16th century on a medieval base, surprises with the austerity of its facade and the richness of its interior. The adjoining Diocesan Museum displays Flemish tapestries, a 14th-century processional cross, and a 16th-century rock crystal fish.

Get lost in the sloping alleys climbing toward the castle. The Casa de la Julianeta, crooked and lopsided near the Portal de Molina, has become the village's photographic symbol. The absence of vertical lines in its structure gives it an unreal air, as if lifted from an illustrated storybook.

The Plaza Mayor, surrounded by arcaded galleries, is dominated by the 16th-century Town Hall and its fine wooden balconies. From the upper floor, the view plunges onto the Guadalaviar meander that encircles the village like a natural moat.

activities.text: Follow the paseo fluvial, a trail along the Guadalaviar, to admire the ramparts from the valley. The contrast between the red sandstone of the walls, the green of the pines, and the blue of the river composes one of Aragon's most photogenic landscapes.

A few kilometers from the village, the red rocks of Pinar del Rodeno shelter UNESCO-listed rock paintings dating from 6,000 to 1,000 BC. These white figures of animals and archers stand out against reddish rock, unique in the entire Levantine rock art arc.
The Sierra de Albarracín offers exceptional night skies for astrotourism. Far from any light pollution, starry nights reveal the Milky Way to the naked eye.

days_plan.itin_title: Valencia's Art & Paella: City of Arts & Sciences & Albufera Wetlands

visits.text: Begin your journey 30 km from Madrid. Alcalá de Henares, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998, was the world's first purpose-built university town. Cardinal Cisneros founded the university in 1499 with the ambition of creating the City of God (Civitas Dei)—a blueprint later exported to the Americas. Head to the Colegio Mayor de San Ildefonso, the founding building: its Plateresque façade, completed in 1553 by Rodrigo Gil de Hontañón, ranks among Spain's finest. Inside, the Paraninfo (ceremonial hall) hosts the annual Cervantes Prize ceremony every 23 April—the Nobel of Hispanic letters, presented by the King of Spain.

Walk down Calle Mayor, one of Europe's longest arcaded streets, to the Casa Natal de Cervantes. Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra was born here in 1547—his baptismal certificate (9 October 1547) is preserved in the town hall. The house, reconstructed as a 16th-century bourgeois residence, is now a free museum: period furniture, the father's medical office (he was a barber-surgeon), and early editions of Don Quixote. Right next door, don't miss the Corral de Comedias (1601), Spain's oldest documented theatre, restored and still in use.

The Magistral Cathedral of Santos Niños Justo y Pastor is one of only two in the world to bear the title 'Magistral' (the other being Leuven)—meaning all its canons had to be doctors of theology from the university. Built in the 15th–16th centuries in Isabelline Gothic style, it houses Cardinal Cisneros' tomb. Climb the bell tower for views over the town's rooftops and the storks nesting on the spires—Alcalá has one of Spain's largest white stork populations.

activities.text: Spend the late afternoon wandering around Plaza de Cervantes (the old Plaza Mayor), the town's beating heart, lined with cafés and terraces. The central bandstand and colourful façades create a wonderfully lively atmosphere. The university quarter around the Colegio Mayor buzzes with the energy of 25,000 students. Sample the costrada (puff pastry cake filled with cream) and rosquillas (anise doughnuts)—Alcalá's signature sweets.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

visits.text: Alcossebre unfolds 10 kilometers of unspoiled coastline. Its beaches—Las Fuentes, Cargador, Tres Playas—and watchtowers dot the Blue Flag-certified shore.
The Sierra d'Irta plunges into the Mediterranean for 12 kilometers. The last undeveloped coastal range in the region, it shelters cliffs, wild coves, and protected wildlife.
The castle of Xivert dominates the sierra at 500 meters. An 11th–12th-century Templar fortress of Moorish origin, recently restored, it still stands watch over the Alcalà plain.
The Torre Ebrí rises 8.5 meters on a clifftop. Built in the 16th century, this watchtower alerted the castle of Xivert whenever pirate ships appeared.

The Church of San Juan Bautista boasts the tallest octagonal bell tower in the Valencia Region (58 meters). Its classical baroque interior preserves a painting by Traver Calzada depicting the martyrdom of Saint John.
The Hermitage of Santa Lucía y San Benito crowns Monte San Benito. This lookout offers one of the finest panoramas in Castellón province, sweeping across the coast and hinterland.
Cala Mundina and Cala Ribamar nestle within the Sierra d'Irta Natural Park. These wild coves surrounded by Mediterranean scrub invite quiet contemplation.

activities.text: Coastal bike paths wind between sea and sierra. The gentle route lets you discover viewpoints and hidden coves at your own pace.
Rent a kayak to explore the sheltered calas. The crystal-clear water reveals the rocky seabed and marine life of the reserve.
The port of Alcossebre serves fish and seafood straight from the day's catch. Marina restaurants offer grilled sea bream, zarzuela, and black rice with cuttlefish.
Hiking trails cross the Sierra d'Irta all the way to where the Columbretes Islands are visible on the horizon. The marked network offers several difficulty levels.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves

visits.text: Alcúdia's ramparts date to the 14th century. Stretching 1.5 kilometers with 26 towers, they once guarded the town from pirates. Access begins at Porta de Vila Roja.
The monumental Porta de Xara opens onto the old town. Its golden stones and rounded arch exemplify Majorcan medieval architecture.
The Roman site of Pollentia was founded in 123 BC. Remains include a theater, forum, and patrician houses from the ancient Balearic Roman capital.
The Monographic Museum displays artifacts unearthed at Pollentia. Pottery, jewelry, coins, and sculptures trace 2,000 years of history.
Sant Jaume Church rises at the heart of the old town. Its Baroque interior contrasts with the medieval cobbled lanes surrounding it.
Alcúdia Beach stretches for 3.5 kilometers. Its fine sand, shallow waters, and Blue Flag status make it one of Majorca's most family-friendly.
Port d'Alcúdia is the modern resort zone. Its seafront promenade, restaurants, and marina animate the waterfront.

activities.text: The coastal bike path traces the north shore for 15 kilometers, running through beaches, pine forests, and S'Albufera Natural Park wetlands.
Kayaks set out toward Cap de Formentor. The rocky peninsula reveals wild coves and plunging cliffs.
Alcúdia Market convenes Tuesday and Sunday mornings. Fruit, vegetables, crafts, and local products spread out before the ramparts.
S'Albufera Natural Park shelters over 200 bird species. Flamingos, herons, and ducks populate the marshes and reedbeds.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Santa Bárbara Castle — views over the bay.
Esplanade of Spain — undulating mosaics.
Santa Cruz Quarter — flowered alleyways.

activities.text: Swimming and paddleboarding on Postiguet beach.
Funicular to the castle then sunset.
Evening seafood tapas at the port.

days_plan.itin_title: Valencia's Art & Paella: City of Arts & Sciences & Albufera Wetlands

visits.text: Two hours' drive south from Madrid and you enter the heart of La Mancha—Don Quixote's plains. Almagro, declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble, is a complete surprise in this landscape of windmills and fields. The Plaza Mayor is its jewel: a rectangular square bordered by two stone-arcaded galleries with 81 columns, and characteristic green-painted glazed balconies—a legacy of the German Fugger bankers (los Fúcares in Spanish) who settled here in the 16th century to manage the Almadén mercury mines on behalf of Charles V.

The Corral de Comedias is the reason for your visit to Almagro. Built in 1628 by Leonardo de Oviedo in the former Mesón del Toro, it's the only Spanish Golden Age theatre preserved intact and still active in the world. Its 300 m² rectangular courtyard, its two-storey wooden galleries, its cazuela (balcony reserved for women), and its tablado (stage) transport you directly to the era of Lope de Vega, Calderón de la Barca, and Tirso de Molina. Closed in the 18th century by royal decree, it was rediscovered by chance in 1954 beneath the partitions of a boarding house. Every summer, the International Festival of Classical Theatre revives Golden Age plays there.

The Museo Nacional del Teatro, housed in the 13th-century Maestral Palace of the Knights of Calatrava, is Spain's only national museum devoted to theatre. It traces the history of performing arts from the Middle Ages: set models, period costumes, manuscripts, puppets, and posters. An entire section is dedicated to the corrales de comedias and theatrical life during the Golden Age.

The Almacén de los Fúcares—the Fuggers' warehouse—is an imposing 16th-century building that served as a grain store and mercury warehouse for the Augsburg bankers. These financiers were so powerful they lent money to Charles V in exchange for the Almadén mercury monopoly. The building, sometimes called a palace because its construction is so refined, now hosts exhibitions. Just next door, the Church of San Agustín, declared of Cultural Interest, deserves a look for its Renaissance cloister.

activities.text: The gastronomy of Almagro and the Campo de Calatrava belongs to the heart of La Mancha. Try the berenjenas de Almagro—small round aubergines pickled in vinegar with cumin and chilli, a local speciality with its own protected designation. Add to that migas manchegas (fried breadcrumbs with chorizo), pisto manchego (a local ratatouille), and a glass of Valdepeñas wine from the neighbouring appellation.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains

visits.text: The Alcazaba of Almería towers over the city from its hilltop perch. Built in 955 by Caliph Abd al-Rahman III, this citadel—Spain's largest after the Alhambra—unfolds across three walled enclosures: the gardens of the first, the five-nave caliphal cistern of the second, and the Christian castle erected by the Catholic Monarchs in 1489.

Step inside the Cathedral of the Incarnation, a unique fortress-temple built after the 1522 earthquake. Its thick walls, battlements, and arrow slits bear witness to attacks by Barbary pirates. Inside, the neoclassical cloister and ambulatory chapels deserve close attention.

The Civil War Shelters burrow 9 meters beneath Paseo de Almería. These 4.5-kilometer underground galleries, designed by architect Guillermo Langle Rubio in 1937, could shelter 40,000 residents. The reconstructed operating room is particularly striking.

The Cable Inglés, an ore-loading pier built in 1904 in Eiffel-style architecture, juts into the sea from Almadrabillas beach. Declared a Cultural Heritage Site in 1998, it offers beautiful perspectives over the port at sunset.

Make your way to Plaza Vieja, the city's administrative heart. The 19th-century neoclassical town hall borders this shaded square. The Monument to the Martyrs of Liberty at its center commemorates victims of absolutist repression in 1824.

Paseo de Almería connects Puerta de Purchena to the port. This shopping avenue, lined with century-old ficus trees, cuts through downtown and passes above the underground shelters. Café terraces come alive as evening falls.

activities.text: Walk the waterfront promenade of Parque Nicolás Salmerón to the marina. Palm trees line the path, with the illuminated Alcazaba glowing in the backdrop.
Head to Mercado Central for a tapas crawl. Almería upholds the tradition of free tapas served with every drink—the bars around the market compete in generosity.
Late afternoon swim at El Zapillo beach, Almería's urban beach, with views of the Cable Inglés and the Sierra de Gádor mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert

visits.text: Twenty minutes west of Córdoba, the castle of Almodóvar del Río appears from afar, perched on a 252-meter hill above the Guadalquivir. This fortress of Umayyad origin, built around 760, is one of the best-preserved in Spain. Its crenellated towers—the Cuadrada, the Redonda, and the Keep—stand out against the sky like an illustration from a medieval book. The interior can be visited: courtyards of arms, furnished halls, armor, dungeons, and oubliettes where Peter I the Cruel supposedly imprisoned his enemies in the 14th century. The building was restored between 1901 and 1936 by the Count of Torralva, which explains its remarkable condition. If the place seems familiar, that's normal—scenes from Game of Thrones (Season 7, Highgarden and Casterly Rock) were filmed here. Allow 1.5 hours for the visit.

In the afternoon, push toward the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park, 30 km northwest. It's one of the least-visited natural areas in Andalusia—and one of the wildest. The Mediterranean forest of holm oaks and cork oaks shelters one of the last populations of Iberian lynx, black vultures, Spanish imperial eagles, and deer. The village of Hornachuelos, listed among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages, overlooks a spectacular gorge of the Río Bembézar. From the village's mirador, the view over the gorges and the Sierra Morena is breathtaking.

The Monastery of San Jerónimo de Valparaíso, on the edge of the park, deserves a stop if timing permits. Founded in the 15th century, this is the monastery where Gonzalo Fernández de Córdoba—the Gran Capitán, the most famous Spanish general of the Renaissance—spent his final years. The cloister and church preserve Gothic and Mudéjar elements.

activities.text: Operators in the Hornachuelos park offer guided hikes with wildlife observation—the Fuente del Valle trail (8 km, 3 hours) crosses a dense cork oak forest with good chances of seeing deer and raptors. In season (autumn-winter), the deer rut is spectacular in these forests. Kayak excursions on the Bembézar reservoir are also available.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

visits.text: In the morning, stop at Aínsa, former capital of the Kingdom of Sobrarbe and one of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. Perched at the confluence of the Ara and Cinca rivers, beneath the gaze of Peña Montañesa, the medieval village seems frozen in the 12th century. The Plaza Mayor, trapezoidal and bordered by Romanesque arcades, is one of the world's best-preserved medieval squares. Climb the square tower of Santa María church (11th-12th centuries) for a panoramic view of the Pyrenees. The restored castle houses an ecomuseum and hosts a music festival in its courtyard each July.

In the afternoon, drive to Alquézar (1h from Aínsa). The name comes from Arabic al-qasr (the fortress) - it was a 9th-century Muslim stronghold conquered by Sancho Ramírez in 1064. The village, clinging to a promontory above the Río Vero canyon, is classified as a Historic Site and ranks among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. Climb up to the Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor, which preserves a Romanesque cloister with carved capitals and a major altarpiece. From the square, the view plunges into the limestone gorges - a dizzying spectacle.

activities.text: Don't miss the Pasarelas del Vero, Aragón's most popular aerial trail. This 3 km route (approximately 2h loop) descends into the Río Vero canyon via metal walkways attached to limestone walls above turquoise water. The trail is paid and well-secured - no technical difficulty, but spectacular views over the gorges and natural pools. In warm weather, bring a swimsuit for a dip.

Near Alquézar, the Río Vero Cultural Park contains over 70 shelters with rock paintings listed as UNESCO World Heritage (1998). The most accessible are the Quizans shelters (schematic art, 5000-1500 BC) and Chimiachas (a deer in Levantine art of striking realism, 6000-3000 BC). Allow 45 minutes' walk from Alquézar to reach the shelters. Access is free but a guide significantly enriches the visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

visits.text: The valleys of Ansó and Hecho are the hidden jewels of the western Aragonese Pyrenees. Ansó, at 860 m altitude, is a museum-village of stone and slate, listed among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. Its traditional ashlar stone houses, with their cylindrical chimneys typical of Pyrenean architecture, have remained virtually unchanged for centuries. The parish church of San Pedro (16th century), in late Gothic style, dominates the village. Wander through the sloping streets and don't miss the Museo del Traje Ansotano, which displays the valley's traditional costumes - worn until the mid-20th century.

Then head to the Hecho Valley (30 min drive), parallel to Ansó. The village of Hecho is another magnificent example of Pyrenean stone architecture, with narrow streets and a Romanesque church. Three kilometers north, the Monastery of San Pedro de Siresa (9th-11th centuries), one of Aragón's oldest, was founded in the Carolingian era and is said to have housed the Holy Grail before its transfer to San Juan de la Peña. The church, massive and austere, is a gem of Aragonese pre-Romanesque art.

activities.text: Drive up the Hecho Valley to the Selva de Oza, a beech and fir forest occupying the floor of an ancient glacial valley - one of the most pristine landscapes in the Pyrenees. The Camino de la Boca del Infierno (Mouth of Hell) follows a spectacular trail along limestone gorges. Allow 2 hours round trip for this easy walk that follows the Aragón Subordán river between 200-meter vertical walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

Discovery of Aracena center and its castle

activities.text: Walk through the rolling landscapes of the Sierra de Aracena

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva Province, Spain: Mining Heritage and Untouched Atlantic Beaches

visits.text: The Royal Palace of Aranjuez (16th–18th centuries) served as the Bourbon monarchs' spring residence. Rococo salons, the porcelain-clad Chamber, and the gilded Throne Room unfold in opulent succession.
The Jardín del Príncipe (18th century) stretches 150 hectares along the Tagus. Shaded avenues, neoclassical fountains, and the Casa del Labrador pavilion create an Enlightenment paradise.
The Jardín de la Isla embraces a Tagus island in geometric Renaissance-Baroque harmony. Parterres, canals, mythological statues, and water jets compose a verdant symphony.
The Royal Barges Museum displays 18th-century royal boats. These ornate vessels carried monarchs on leisurely river excursions—floating thrones on the Tagus.
Plaza de San Antonio (1750) arrays Baroque façades around the town church. This regular square forms Aranjuez's urban heart—elegant geometry meets everyday life.
The Casa del Labrador (1804) showcases neoclassical salons and Empire décor. This pavilion glittering with marble and bronze epitomizes Bourbon refinement at its peak.

activities.text: Embark on a mini-cruise along the Tagus. Narrated tours glide past royal gardens, historic bridges, and tree-lined banks—Aranjuez from the water.
Picnic with Aranjuez strawberries. These famed fresas are harvested April through June and sold at local markets—sweet, sun-warmed perfection.
Cycle along the Tagus. Bike paths wind through royal gardens, orchards, and the huertas (market gardens) that made Aranjuez agriculturally legendary—easy riverside riding.
Ride the Tren de la Fresa (Strawberry Train). This heritage train links Madrid to Aranjuez with strawberry tastings and period-costumed entertainment—nostalgia on rails.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums

visits.text: The Charco de San Ginés defines Arrecife: a natural seawater lagoon in the heart of the capital, lined with whitewashed fishermen's houses. This natural harbor has existed for over six centuries—the city was born here after the Franco-Norman conquest in the 15th century. César Manrique redesigned it as a serene promenade. Walk its perimeter in early morning when the light is soft and the terraces still quiet.

Castillo de San Gabriel deserves a full hour. Built in the 16th century on a small island to defend against Berber pirates—it was first burned by the corsair Morato in 1586 before being rebuilt in stone—it now houses the Museum of the History of Lanzarote with over 3,000 artifacts. You reach it via the Puente de las Bolas, a drawbridge crowned by two stone cannonballs: this is Arrecife's postcard image. The castle was declared a national historic monument in 1972.

The Castillo de San José, perched on the bay of Los Mármoles port, houses the International Museum of Contemporary Art, founded in 1975 on César Manrique's initiative. The building itself dates to the 18th century, and its construction traces back to a legend: in 1630, a French merchant named Francisco García Santaella supposedly founded a hermitage here. The interior blends Baroque, Mudéjar, and Neoclassical styles—a typical Canarian architectural fusion. The castle's panoramic restaurant offers harbor views worth the visit alone.

End with Calle Real—officially called León y Castillo—Arrecife's main artery for centuries. You'll find the Plazuela (Constitution Square) and San Ginés Church, whose white dome dominates the square where an artisan market sets up every Saturday. The church became a parish in 1798 and was dedicated to San Ginés, the city's patron saint. The complete walk takes 2 to 3 hours.

activities.text: Lunch around Charco de San Ginés: the area brims with restaurants serving Canarian classics—papas arrugadas with mojo verde or mojo rojo, fresh fish from the port, and malvasía wines from the island. It's also the best spot to watch fishing boats maneuver through the lagoon.

Swimming and relaxation at Playa del Reducto, Arrecife's main beach. It stretches over half a kilometer with golden sand and calm waters, perfect for a mid-afternoon break. The promenade bordering it is the island's busiest.

Late afternoon, return to stroll around Charco de San Ginés at sunset. Locals gather here for terrace drinks. If you're there on a Wednesday or Thursday, a popular market sets up around the lagoon—a chance to pick up local crafts.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

places.name: Arribes Del Duero

visits.text: The Arribes del Duero Natural Park stretches for nearly 100 kilometers of canyons carved by the Duero River through granite, forming the natural border between Spain and Portugal. The cliffs plunge up to 500 meters—the deepest gorges in the entire Iberian Peninsula. The Mirador del Fraile, at Aldeadávila de la Ribera, offers the most vertiginous viewpoint: a walkway overhangs the canyon directly, not recommended if you have a fear of heights. From there, the Duero's waters seem motionless, they're so far below. Bring water as the trails are exposed.

The Pozo de los Humos is one of the peninsula's most spectacular waterfalls: the Uces River plunges into the canyon from over 50 meters, creating a permanent cloud of spray that gave it its name ('well of smoke'). The trail from Masueco is short (2.3 km round trip) and accessible to all, but stones are slippery in wet season. In summer, flow can be low depending on the year's rainfall—choose spring or autumn to see the falls at full force.

Make a detour through Fermoselle, a town declared a historic and artistic site, perched on the heights with views over the confluence of the Duero and Esla. The town is nicknamed 'the town of a thousand cellars'—caves carved into the rock beneath houses to store wine from the Arribes terroir, a developing denomination of origin with eleven vineyards planted on terraces. The viewpoints at the castle and Las Escaleras are worth the stop.

activities.text: Walk sections of the GR-14, the long-distance trail that follows the Duero from its source to Porto. In the Arribes sector, several sections above the gorges are accessible in 1 to 3 hours. The microclimate warmer than the rest of Castile—due to the deep gorges trapping heat—allows olive trees, almonds and vines to grow at this latitude. Look up: golden eagles, griffon vultures and black storks nest on the rock walls.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Mirador del Fraile Aldeadávila de la Ribera

places.name: Astorga

visits.text: The Episcopal Palace of Astorga is one of Gaudí's rare works built outside Catalonia. After the old palace burned in 1886, Bishop Joan Grau i Vallespinós—a Catalan and Gaudí's personal friend—commissioned him to rebuild. The project began in 1887: Gaudí designed a neo-Gothic building in white granite that blends castle, temple, and palace, with turrets, battlements, and multicolored stained glass inspired by León Cathedral. The bishop's death in 1893 caused a rupture between Gaudí and the church authorities—he walked off the job, leaving the top floor unfinished, which another architect completed more soberly. Today the palace houses the Museum of the Ways, dedicated to pilgrimage routes. Allow at least 90 minutes.

Astorga's Cathedral of Santa María, built from the 15th to 18th centuries, is one of Spain's most composite monuments—Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque, and Plateresque succeed each other on its façades and interior. On one of its apsidal pinnacles, look for the statue of Pedro Mato, a Maragato about whom nothing is certain but local legends are numerous. The cathedral museum houses a magnificently chiseled silver casket by Alfonso III the Great.

Astorga was the Roman capital of Asturica Augusta, crossroads of several Roman roads. The Roman walls preserved in several sections throughout the city are among the best preserved on the peninsula. Don't miss the Roman Museum to understand the city's importance in imperial times—and the Chocolate Museum, since Astorga was historically one of the first European cities to import and process cacao from the Americas via Maragato muleteers.

Castrillo de los Polvazares is La Maragatería's most perfectly preserved village. Its cobbled streets, red-stone houses with large round-arched doorways and windows painted blue or green form a homogeneous 17th-century ensemble declared a Historic-Artistic Site in 1980. Those oversized doorways weren't architectural whimsy: they allowed carts and mules to enter interior courtyards. For Castrillo was from the 16th to 19th centuries the heart of the Maragato muleteer network—those semi-nomadic transporters who linked Galicia and Madrid with their mule caravans. English writer Richard Ford described them in 1846: 'as nomadic as Bedouins, but with mules instead of camels; they cost dearly, but their honesty compensates.' The arrival of the railroad in Astorga in 1866 spelled their end. The village had 1,200 inhabitants in 1870; it has about a hundred today.

Santa Colomba de Somoza, 13 km away, is another preserved Maragato village, inhabited since the 11th century and linked to the muleteering trade since the 16th. The Casa Maragata—an ethnographic museum set in a real muleteer house—recreates the domestic and professional spaces of Maragato life: kitchen with large hearth, cart shed, agricultural tools, and traces of the chocolate trade. Each August, residents open their courtyards for a crafts fair.

activities.text: Bar and restaurant tour around Plaza Mayor to dine on Maragatería specialties: cocido maragato (the Spanish stew eaten backwards—meat first, soup last), mantecadas de Astorga (butter and cinnamon cakes), and artisanal chocolate that's made the city's reputation since the 18th century. Book a restaurant for the cocido—it's a meal lasting several hours.

Lunch at Castrillo de los Polvazares with cocido maragato: the legend about the inverted eating order (meat before soup) is tied to the War of Independence—Spanish soldiers ate the most nourishing parts first before imminent battle against the French. Book ahead, especially weekends—the restaurant is often full.

Hiking the 'Senda Maragata' from Santa Colomba de Somoza—several marked circuits let you discover remnants of Roman mines and the Maragatería landscapes between oak groves and heathland. The Mirador de la Laguna Cernea offers views over the worn hills and Roman gold workings of the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Ibiza and Formentera Guide: Beyond the Clubs to Turquoise Coves and Beaches

visits.text: The ramparts of Ávila encircle the old town for 2,516 meters since the 11th century. Rising 12 meters high and 3 meters thick, they count 88 semicircular towers and 9 fortified gates—the best-preserved in Spain.
The Puerta de San Vicente and Puerta del Alcázar, the oldest, raise two 20-meter towers linked by a semicircular arch. The ramparts define Ávila's silhouette like no other city.
The Gothic cathedral was begun in 1091 in Romanesque style then completed in the 14th century as Gothic. Its fortified apse forms a defensive turret integrated into the ramparts, called the Cimorro.
The interior blends Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance with polychrome stained glass and carved altarpieces. The walls display local red sandstone tinted with iron oxide conferring a unique color.
Saint Teresa of Ávila was born here in 1515. Carmelite mystic, reformer, and writer, she became one of the most important female saints of the Catholic Church—a spiritual giant.
The convent of Santa Teresa was built in 1636 on her birthplace. The Baroque church preserves relics and mementos of the saint who founded 17 convents across Spain.

The Romanesque basilica of San Vicente rises outside the walls in the 12th century on the site of Saint Vincent's martyrdom and his sisters in the 4th century. Its elegant proportions make it a masterpiece of Hispanic Romanesque.

The monastery of Santo Tomás possesses three cloisters and houses the tomb of Prince Juan, son of the Catholic Monarchs. The Grand Inquisitor Torquemada also rests here—a darker chapter of Spanish history.

activities.text: Walk the rampart walkway for panoramic views over the city and mountains. Access is near the cathedral at the information post—one of Europe's most spectacular medieval walks.

Climb to the Cuatro Postes, a monument of four columns erected in 1566. This promontory to the west offers the most spectacular view of the ramparts and old town, particularly at twilight when the stones glow golden.
Try chuletón de Ávila, enormous grilled ribeye served rare, and yemas de Santa Teresa, ultra-sweet egg yolk cookies created in honor of the saint.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva Province, Spain: Mining Heritage and Untouched Atlantic Beaches

visits.text: Baeza is one of the two towns inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2003 for their Renaissance ensembles. But before becoming a 16th-century jewel, it was a stronghold of the Reconquest: Ferdinand III conquered it in 1227, and it became Castile's forward base facing the Kingdom of Granada for two centuries. Begin at Plaza del Pópulo, the civic heart of the town. The Fuente de los Leones (Lion Fountain), which resembles the one in the Alhambra, is actually an Ibero-Roman remnant from the ancient city of Cástulo. Around the square, the old slaughterhouse (Antigua Carnicería) and the Villalar Arch complete a harmonious ensemble.

Climb to Plaza de Santa María, the religious and educational heart of Baeza. The Cathedral of the Nativity, rebuilt in the 16th century on a former mosque, blends Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque. Its main façade is sober, but the interior surprises with the brightness of its vaults and the richness of its chapels. Just opposite, the Palacio de Jabalquinto is Baeza's most photographed monument: its Isabelline façade, covered with carved stone diamond points, is a masterpiece of stone lacework from the 15th century. It now houses the headquarters of the International University of Andalusia.

The Old University of Baeza, founded in 1538, is an austere Renaissance building with a beautiful arcaded courtyard. The poet Antonio Machado taught French there between 1912 and 1919—his classroom has remained intact, with his desk, blackboard, and student benches. It was here that he wrote part of his Campos de Castilla. On the wall, a plaque quotes his verses: 'En estos campos de la tierra mía...' Entry is free and the emotion is real.

Finish with a walk along the Paseo de la Constitución and the ramparts, from where the view embraces the olive fields stretching to the horizon. The sunset over this green and silver sea is a daily spectacle that Baeza's inhabitants never tire of watching.

activities.text: Baeza is a town of tapas and olive oil. The bars around Plaza de la Constitución and Calle San Pablo serve generous portions drizzled with Sierra de Cazorla PDO olive oil. Try the ochíos (olive oil bread typical of Jaén), pipirrana (cold salad of tomato, pepper, and cucumber), and revuelto de ajetes (scrambled eggs with tender garlic). Olive oil here isn't a condiment—it's a main ingredient.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: Balouta is the last village in León province before the borders with Galicia and Asturias converge. With about twenty inhabitants, this Ancares hamlet preserves the highest concentration of pallozas in the entire region—those circular stone houses with thatched rye roofs that look like giant haystacks. Pallozas date back at least to the Middle Ages; their round form helped them withstand the winds and snows of these mountains over 1,000 meters up. Some once sheltered both people and animals under the same roof, the livestock's body heat warming the dwelling. One restored palloza now serves as a bar—make sure to go inside to see the interior. Book your casa rural well in advance; accommodations are extremely scarce.

The neighboring village of Suarbol, 4 km away, has only seven permanent residents (about thirty in summer) and also preserves pallozas. The road between the two villages follows the valley floor of the Ancares, flanked by slopes thick with oaks and chestnuts. You'll also find a small church dedicated to San Justo, with a noble coat of arms on one of the neighboring façades—one of the rare witnesses to the valley's former prosperity.

activities.text: Hiking in the Ancares Biosphere Reserve. Marked trails from Balouta lead to the brañas—high-altitude pastures—where views sweep across multiple valleys simultaneously. Wildlife is remarkable in this seldom-visited area: brown bears (presence confirmed), wolves, deer, and remarkable bird density. Bring proper hiking boots and water—there are no services outside the village.

Watch the sunset from the heights above Balouta. The late-day light on the rye-thatched palloza roofs and green slopes creates one of the Ancares' most striking images. Bring a flashlight for the return.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

places.name: Banos De La Encina

visits.text: The road south from Almagro crosses the Sierra Morena through the Despeñaperros gorge—the natural frontier between Castile and Andalusia. This narrow pass between quartzite cliffs has been the gateway to Andalusia for centuries: Roman armies, Visigoths, Moors, and Christian armies all passed through. Stop at the viewpoint for a view over the gorges, then detour to the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa Interpretation Centre in Santa Elena. On 16 July 1212, the Christian coalition led by Alfonso VIII of Castile crushed the Almohad army here—a battle that reversed the course of the Reconquest and definitively opened Andalusia to the Christian kingdoms.

Baños de la Encina appears among the olive groves, dominated by the spectacular silhouette of Castillo de Burgalimar. This Caliphate fortress, built in 968 on the orders of Caliph Al-Hakam II of Córdoba, is one of the best-preserved Muslim fortresses in Europe. Its 14 towers and its wall of tapial (rammed earth, lime, and stone) still stand at their original height—a millennium later. A 15th tower, the Keep, was added by the Christians after the reconquest. It's called 'the fortress of seven kings' because seven monarchs passed through, from Alfonso VII to Ferdinand the Catholic. Climb the 18-metre keep: the panorama over the endless sea of olive trees is one of the most striking images in the province of Jaén.

Coming down from the castle, stroll through the streets of Baños de la Encina. Unlike Andalusia's white villages, here the façades are ashlar stone—16th and 17th-century manor houses with carved coats of arms line the streets. The Church of San Mateo (15th century), in late Gothic style, dominates the main square. Don't miss the anthropomorphic tombs carved into the rock near the Molino del Santo, vestiges of a Visigothic past.

activities.text: Baños de la Encina sits at the heart of the world's largest olive grove—the province of Jaén alone produces about 20% of the world's olive oil. Local operators offer visits to olive groves and oil mills with tastings. You'll learn to distinguish the Picual variety (the most planted here, intense and fruity) from Arbequina, and to recognise a 'virgen extra' from a simple 'virgen'. It's an experience that permanently changes your relationship with olive oil.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance

map_keywords: Castillo de Burgalimar Baños de la Encina
Iglesia San Mateo Baños de la Encina

places.name: Barcelona

visits.text: The Sagrada Família tells the story of a construction begun in 1882 and still unfinished - making it the world's largest incomplete Catholic church. Gaudí took over the project in 1883 and devoted the final 43 years of his life to it. In October 2025, the central tower reached 162 meters, finally surpassing Montjuïc mountain by just one meter - Gaudí believed no human creation should exceed nature. Book your tickets online well in advance, even off-season: 4.5 million visitors flock here annually.

Just steps away, Passeig de Gràcia showcases Gaudí's two wildest houses. Casa Batlló, renovated between 1904 and 1906, is nicknamed the 'house of bones' - its limestone pillars evoke skeletons and its balconies, skulls. Casa Milà (La Pedrera), built between 1906 and 1912, was the last house Gaudí designed before dedicating himself entirely to the Sagrada Família. Its rooftop, with twisted chimneys, offers one of the city's finest vantage points - the classic spot for photographing the Sagrada Família through the arches.

The Gothic Quarter (Barri Gòtic) stands on the ruins of the Roman colony of Barcino, founded around 15 BC. The Cathedral of Saint Eulalia, begun in 1298, houses 13 white geese in its cloister - a tradition linked to the legend of Saint Eulalia, co-patron of Barcelona, who reportedly suffered 13 tortures in Roman times. Get lost in the narrow streets: the Pont del Bisbe, a neo-Gothic footbridge built in 1928 between the Palau de la Generalitat and Casa dels Canonges, is one of the city's most photographed corners.

Park Güell is a 17-hectare park that Gaudí designed from 1900 as a private garden-city for 60 families - a project that never materialized for lack of buyers. The main terrace, covered in multicolored trencadís mosaics and shaped like a winding serpent, offers panoramic views across the entire city to the sea. Arrive early in the morning: the monumental zone is ticketed and subject to visitor quotas.

The Palau de la Música Catalana, built between 1905 and 1908 by Lluís Domènech i Montaner, is one of the world's most beautiful concert halls and a UNESCO World Heritage site. Its red-brick facade and interior skylight of multicolored stained glass filter unique light during concerts. If you're not attending a performance, the guided tour of the hall comes highly recommended.

The El Born neighborhood houses the Picasso Museum, installed in five medieval palaces from the 13th and 15th centuries connected to one another. The collection traces the painter's formative years in Barcelona, notably featuring the complete Las Meninas series (58 variations on Velázquez, created in 1957). The visit requires at least 90 minutes - book your tickets online.

The Basilica of Santa Maria del Mar, built between 1329 and 1383 in Catalan Gothic style, is often preferred to the cathedral by Barcelonans themselves. Its nave is taller, brighter, more austere. It was collectively financed by the port's stevedores - each neighborhood resident brought a stone. It's 'the cathedral of the sea' described by Ildefonso Falcones in his novel of the same name.

If you missed sites during days 1 and 2, this final day in Barcelona is your chance to catch up. Palau Güell, in the El Raval neighborhood, is the first major commission Barcelona gave to Gaudí (1886-1890) - much less known than the Sagrada Família or Casa Batlló, but fascinating for its dark interiors and sculptural chimneys on the roof. The El Raval neighborhood has changed dramatically since the 1990s: it now mixes museums, galleries, and restaurants from around the world.

La Boqueria market, on La Rambla, is one of Europe's most photographed markets. Arrive before 9 am to avoid tour group crowds and find the freshest stalls. Freshly pressed fruit juices are worth the stop. However, the tapas in the front rows are often prepared for tourists - go eat a bit further into El Raval for a more local address.

activities.text: Start the day with coffee and a coca de recapte (Catalan flatbread with toppings) in the Eixample, the modernist district built according to Cerdà's 1860 plan - its perfect grid with chamfered corners is visible from the air. The markets at Sant Antoni or La Boqueria are ideal for a standing breakfast.

In late afternoon, take the cable car or funicular up to Montjuïc to catch the sunset over the port and Barcelona's skyline. The castle and Laribal gardens are worth exploring - the view embraces the entire bay.

In the evening, head to the Gràcia neighborhood for an aperitif vermouth in one of its old-school bars. This neighborhood, formerly an independent village annexed to Barcelona in 1897, has preserved its lively little squares and village atmosphere. Continue with creative tapas in the Eixample or El Born.

In the morning, stroll down La Rambla to the port, then follow the waterfront promenade to Barceloneta beach. This neighborhood, built in the 18th century on a grid plan to house families expropriated from the Ciutadella, retains its characteristic narrow streets. The beach stretches nearly a kilometer - perfect for a morning swim before the crowds arrive.

In the afternoon, explore the markets and boutiques of the El Born neighborhood, one of the city's most creative areas. Don't miss the medieval archaeological remains of the Born market, visible beneath the 1876 iron hall - an entire neighborhood preserved under glass after its destruction in 1714.

Finish with dinner in the Poble-sec neighborhood, at the foot of Montjuïc, where Carrer de Blai avenue is famous for its pintxos bars. It's the local alternative to tourist tapas: cheaper, more authentic, and the portions are generous.

Final swim at Barceloneta or stroll along the waterfront to Port Olímpic, built for the 1992 Olympic Games. The transformation of Barcelona's waterfront after 1992 is one of Europe's most studied urban projects: 4 km of beaches created from scratch where there had been only warehouses and railway tracks.

To finish the trip on a high note, climb to Tibidabo by blue tram or funicular: the hill at 512 meters dominates the entire bay of Barcelona. On clear days, you can see all the way to Majorca. The amusement park at the summit, opened in 1901, is one of Europe's oldest still operating.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages
Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages
Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

Palau de la Música Catalana Barcelona
Santa Maria del Mar El Born Barcelona

visits.text: Baza sits on the Altiplano of Granada, a high plateau at 850 meters altitude whose arid landscapes sometimes evoke Bolivia or New Mexico. The town of 20,000 inhabitants is best known for the Lady of Baza (Dama de Baza), a 4th-century BC Iberian sculpture discovered on July 22, 1971, in the necropolis of Cerro del Santuario. This seated female figure on a throne, originally polychrome (red, blue, white, black), served as a funerary urn - a cavity in her side contained the ashes of a high-ranking woman. The original is in Madrid's National Archaeological Museum, but the local archaeological museum displays a replica and contextualizes the discovery.

Baza's center preserves traces of the Al-Andalus period: the Arab baths (baños árabes), the ruined Alcazaba and the San Juan quarter, the former morería (Moorish quarter). The Iglesia Mayor, in Gothic and Renaissance styles, dominates the Plaza Mayor. Stroll through the narrow streets of the center - Baza has the slow rhythm of a provincial Andalusian town where people still live outdoors, on the squares, between tapas bars and churches.

activities.text: Like Guadix, Baza also has a cave-house quarter, smaller but equally authentic. The Altiplano around the town offers spectacular badlands landscapes - ravines of ochre and red clay sculpted by erosion, reminiscent of the American desert. Several hiking trails cross these lunar landscapes.

In the evening, taste the Altiplano specialties: migas (sautéed semolina crumbs with chorizo and peppers), cordero segureño (local lamb), and goat cheeses. Baza is a tapas town - here, they give you one free with each drink, in the best Granada tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras

visits.text: The route northward crosses the first dehesa landscapes—those pastures dotted with centuries-old holm oaks that are the birthplace of the Iberian pig. You're entering the Valle de los Pedroches, the largest continuous dehesa in Europe, extending over 3,600 km² in northern Córdoba province. First stop: Belalcázar, whose Sotomayor y Zúñiga castle can be seen from kilometers away. Built in the 15th century, it has the tallest Torre del Homenaje (Keep) in the entire Iberian Peninsula—47 meters of granite flanked by seven massive towers. The castle's interior unfortunately cannot be visited (under restoration), but its silhouette seen from below is one of the most impressive in Spain. Walk around via the path that circles the hill for the best perspectives.

The Convento de Santa Clara de la Columna, in the village of Belalcázar, is the second most important religious monumental complex in Córdoba province after the Mezquita. Founded in the 15th century, it's a cloistered convent still inhabited by nuns. Guided tours (often dramatized) allow you to discover the cloister, the artesonados (coffered wooden ceilings in Mudéjar style), and the chapel. The nuns sell artisanal pastries through a rotating hatch—a typically Spanish tradition.

Continue 9 km east to Hinojosa del Duque. Its church of San Juan Bautista is nicknamed 'the Cathedral of the Sierra'—and rightly so. Built in Pedroches granite between the 15th and 16th centuries, its 56-meter tower is the work of Hernán Ruiz II, the same architect who transformed the Mezquita's minaret into a bell tower in Córdoba and remodeled the Giralda's campanile in Seville. The interior houses a remarkable baptistery and Baroque altarpieces. The ethnological museum, in the village center, traces rural life in Los Pedroches with agricultural tools, antique toys, and even an old cinema projector.

activities.text: The gastronomy of Los Pedroches revolves around the Iberian pig. The jamón ibérico de bellota DOP Los Pedroches is one of the most renowned in Spain—the black pigs graze freely in the dehesa and feed on holm oak acorns during the montanera (October to February). Regional operators offer visits to ham-curing facilities with tastings and explanations of the aging process. It's a first-rate gastronomic experience. But Los Pedroches isn't just about ham: also try the goat cheeses, rejones (local pastries), and salmorejo serrano.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

places.name: Benicassim

visits.text: The Paseo Pilar Coloma runs along the Mediterranean shore. Fifty-one Belle Époque villas built between 1887 and 1930 line the route—Villa Elisa, Villa Ana, Villa del Mar—recalling the era when Benicàssim was dubbed the "Valencian Biarritz."
Villa Elisa opens for cultural exhibitions. This modernist palace showcases Nolla-style ceramics and salons preserved in their original state.
The Torre de San Vicente dates from the 16th century. This Renaissance watchtower guarded the coast against Barbary pirates. Its square plan and machicolations are characteristic of the period.
The Church of Santo Tomás de Villanueva is an 18th-century neoclassical gem. Its sober facade contrasts with the richness of its interior.
The Desert de les Palmes Natural Park extends over 3,200 hectares. Its peaks—Bartolo (729m) and Mola del Morico (694m)—offer views all the way to the Columbretes Islands.
The Monastery of the Discalced Carmelites dates from the 17th century. The Sacred Art Museum displays antique books and the instruments the monks still use to make their carmelitano liqueur.
The castle of Montornés crowns a promontory at 500 meters altitude. This 10th-century Arab fortress, expanded by Christians after 1233, overlooks the Castellón plain.

activities.text: The trails of Desert de les Palmes wind through pine, holm oak, and aromatic plants. The route to Bartolo from the convent takes about 4 hours.
Benicàssim's beaches total 6 kilometers of fine sand. Voramar, Almadraba, Torre de San Vicente, Els Terrers, and Heliópolis all fly the Blue Flag.
Benicàssim hosts several international festivals—FIB, Rototom Sunsplash, Blues Festival. Check the calendar to catch the summer lineup.
The coastal bike path runs for 15 kilometers along the shore. The panoramic route passes through beaches and the town center with breathtaking Mediterranean views.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves

map_keywords: Parc Natural Desert de les Palmes

places.name: Betancuria

visits.text: The Iglesia Santa María de Betancuria, founded in 1410 by Jean de Béthencourt in French Gothic style, was Fuerteventura's first cathedral. Rebuilt after pirates destroyed it in 1593, it now blends Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque elements into a harmonious whole.

The Sacred Art Museum displays paintings, sculptures, and religious artifacts from the 15th and 16th centuries. Ornate chalices, ancient missals, and Béthencourt's banner carried during the conquest reveal the island's spiritual heritage.

The Archaeological Museum showcases pottery, bone tools, and fishing equipment of the Mahos, the indigenous people. Discover how they survived with limited resources before Norman arrival, adapting to one of the Canaries' harshest environments.

Wander among the ruins of the San Buenaventura Convent, founded in 1414 by Franciscan monks. As the island's oldest convent, it bears witness to Betancuria's central religious role in the archipelago.
The Mirador de Morro Velosa offers sweeping panoramas across the Betancuria massif. Two bronze statues represent Guise and Ayose, the legendary Maho kings who ruled before conquest.

Lose yourself in the cobbled streets of the historic center. Traditional whitewashed houses, carved wooden doors, and flower-filled balconies create an atmosphere where time seems to have stopped centuries ago.

activities.text: Hike through the Betancuria Rural Park, which covers 90% of the municipality. Ravines, hills, and a small pine forest—Fuerteventura's only one—shelter endemic flora and fauna found nowhere else on Earth.

Descend to Vega del Río Palmas to visit the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Peña. This mountain chapel houses the Virgin of the Peña, the island's patron saint, honored during a colorful procession each September.
Savor authentic Canarian cuisine in the village restaurants. PDO Majorero cheese, roasted goat, and traditional Canarian stew feature on every menu, paired with local wines.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

map_keywords: Ermita Virgen de la Peña Vega Río Palmas

visits.text: Guggenheim Museum — titanium and Gehry's curves.
Casco Viejo — Siete Calles and cathedral.
Zubizuri Bridge and banks of the Nervión.

activities.text: Pintxos route in the center.
Artxanda cable car for the panorama.
Short river cruise on the Nervión.

days_plan.itin_title: Bay of Biscay Coastal Drive: Fishing Villages & Basque Culinary Soul in France

visits.text: Binibeca Vell, a village rebuilt between 1964 and 1972, unfolds 165 whitewashed houses connected by a labyrinth of winding lanes.
Arched passageways and dark wooden balconies create striking contrasts against the brilliant white walls. A charming faux chapel punctuates the winding route.
The paseo marítimo encircles the village from the small harbor, offering sweeping views over white rooftops meeting the Mediterranean.
Playa de Binibeca stretches 200 meters of fine sand just one kilometer from the village. Shallow, crystalline waters and shady pine groves make it ideal for families.
Cala Torret, at Binibeca's eastern edge, houses a tourist information center and supermarket for practical needs.

activities.text: Visit Binibeca at dawn or early morning to catch the golden light painting the white walls and avoid the midday crowds that flood the narrow lanes.
The crystal-clear waters are perfect for snorkeling, with exceptional visibility revealing rich marine life along the rocky seabed.
Watch sunset from the harbor as the white houses glow pink and gold, creating that quintessential Mediterranean atmosphere in perfect tranquility.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

places.name: Brihuega

visits.text: Brihuega is nicknamed the "Garden of Alcarria"—and in July, "Spanish Provence." This fortified medieval village, declared a historic ensemble in 1973, overlooks the Tajuña valley from its hill. Begin with the 12th-century ramparts, nearly two kilometers long, pierced by monumental gates: the Puerta de la Cadena, Puerta del Juego de Pelota, and Arco de Cozagón. The Castillo de la Piedra Bermeja, south of the village, dates from the same era and offers a panoramic view over the surrounding fields.

Descend into the Cuevas Árabes, a network of underground galleries carved beneath the village. These cellars once served as water reserves and refuges. The cool atmosphere and stone vaults contrast with the Meseta's summer heat. The guided tour lasts about 30 minutes.

The Real Fábrica de Paños is Brihuega's emblematic civil monument. This royal cloth factory, founded by Ferdinand VI in 1750, was given to the inhabitants as reward for their support during the War of Succession. The 18th-century building, with its characteristic rotunda, is a rare example of industrial architecture from this period in Spain. The gardens surrounding it, laid out in 1813 in a geometric style inspired by Versailles, with cypresses, clipped boxwood, and fountains, justify the town's nickname. Writer Camilo José Cela described them as "a romantic garden, a garden to die of adolescent love."

activities.text: If visiting in July, the lavender fields surrounding Brihuega are in full bloom—over 1,000 hectares of plantations representing 10% of global lavender essential oil production. The spectacle of violet expanses stretching to the horizon is breathtaking. Operators offer guided tours of the fields and local distillery. The rest of the year, Alcarria's hills remain beautiful with their fields of cereals, olive trees, and aromatic plants.

Sample Alcarria honey, one of Spain's most renowned—its production is protected by a designation of origin. Village shops also sell lavender-derived products: soaps, essential oils, liqueurs, and even lavender ice cream. For dinner, order cabrito asado (roast kid), the region's specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Castillo de la Piedra Bermeja Brihuega

places.name: Buitrago

visits.text: Buitrago's medieval walls, built between the 11th and 12th centuries, rank among the best preserved in Spain. 800 meters of stone ramparts encircle the old town. The río Lozoya curves around in a natural moat.

Buitrago Castle (Castillo de los Mendoza), a 15th-century Gothic and Mudéjar fortress, dominates the river bend. Property of the powerful Mendoza family. An imposing silhouette with square tower and curtain wall.

The Picasso Museum - Eugenio Arias Collection displays 75 works gifted by Picasso to his barber and friend over 26 years (1948-1972). Drawings, lithographs, ceramics, posters. A moving collection testament to an extraordinary friendship.

The Church of Santa María del Castillo blends Gothic and Mudéjar styles (14th-15th centuries). Commissioned by the Marquis of Santillana. Remarkable Mudéjar ceiling, interior restored after Civil War fire. Adjacent medieval cemetery.
The 14th-century Puente del Arrabal, built on the Cañada Real Segoviana (royal drove road), offers magnificent views of the fortified town. Ancient stone gracefully spanning the Lozoya.
Pass through the Torre del Reloj, a 16-meter-high clock tower standing apart from the main wall. The principal gateway into the medieval citadel.

activities.text: Walk the rampart walkways (Adarve). The Adarve Bajo follows the Lozoya at 6 meters high and 2 meters thick. The Adarve Alto to the south features defensive towers and bastions. Exceptional panoramas at every turn.
Wander the cobbled lanes of the historic center. Traditional whitewashed houses, the lively Plaza de la Constitución. The Arco de las Nieves and fortified gates punctuate your route.
Riosequillo Reservoir, 2 km away, boasts one of the largest swimming pools in the Community of Madrid. Play areas, gardens and full services for a perfect summer day by the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María del Castillo Buitrago

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santa María de Burgos—the only cathedral in Spain individually designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site—unfolds behind a facade crowned by two 84-meter towers with 15th-century openwork spires. The Sarmental Door features a Christ in Majesty surrounded by apostles, one of the finest sculptural ensembles in Spanish Gothic art.

Inside, the Chapel of the Constable, designed by Simon of Cologne between 1482 and 1494, forms a cathedral within the cathedral. Beneath its eight-pointed star vault lie the recumbent effigies in Carrara marble of the Constable and his wife. The sacristy houses a Magdalene attributed to the school of Leonardo da Vinci.

Climb the Golden Staircase, a Renaissance masterpiece with a double stairway designed by Diego de Siloé in 1519. Its gilded railing inspired Charles Garnier for the grand staircase of the Paris Opera. Beneath the neighboring lantern tower, a Mudéjar star vault shelters the tombs of El Cid Campeador and his wife Doña Jimena.

The Arco de Santa María, a 14th-century fortified gate remodeled in the 16th century, welcomes visitors with its six monumental statues including Charles V and El Cid. The interior, open to visitors, displays Mudéjar plasterwork from the castle.

The Museum of Human Evolution, a contemporary building designed by Juan Navarro Baldeweg, presents over 200 original fossils from Atapuerca, including remains of Homo Antecessor (the oldest in Europe), the Miguelón skull, and the Excalibur hand axe. Four floors trace 1.5 million years of evolution.

The ruins of Burgos Castle, on the hill overlooking the city, offer a 360° panorama over the rooftops, cathedral spires, and the Castilian meseta. A network of restored underground galleries can be toured beneath the fortress.

The Charterhouse of Miraflores, a Gothic monastery east of the city, houses a polychrome altarpiece said to have been gilded with the first gold brought back from the Americas. The royal tombs of John II and Isabella of Portugal, sculpted by Gil de Siloé, rank among the finest in Spain.

activities.text: The neighborhood around Plaza Mayor is packed with traditional mesones. Sample morcilla de Burgos, black pudding with rice and spices, and lechazo asado, milk-fed lamb roasted in a wood-fired oven—two pillars of Castilian gastronomy.

The Paseo del Espolón follows the Arlanzón River beneath a double row of plane trees. This shaded promenade, the city's most popular, connects the Arco de Santa María to the San Pablo bridge where an equestrian statue of El Cid stands.

The Monastery of Las Huelgas, founded in the 12th century by Alfonso VIII, served as both royal pantheon and coronation site. Its Gothic cloister, the Museum of Rich Fabrics, and the banner captured from the Moors at Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212 are all worth the detour.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages
The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

places.name: Cabo De Gata

visits.text: Mónsul beach is the natural park's most famous stretch. Enormous black lava tongues frame its golden sand—this is where a scene from Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade was filmed. At the beach's center, a massive wind-sculpted rock serves as a landmark.

Want a kilometer of pristine sand? Los Genoveses beach stretches across a bay bordered by dunes, without a single building in sight. The shallow water slopes gently, perfect for swimming. In summer, vehicle access is limited to a few cars per day.

The Arrecife de las Sirenas (Mermaid Reef) at the cape's tip owes its name to the monk seals sailors once mistook for mermaids. Dark volcanic chimneys emerge from turquoise waters, right at the foot of the Cabo de Gata lighthouse built in 1863.
The village of San José serves as the park's main gateway. Its white houses and small fishing port live by the rhythm of the seasons. Terraces facing the sea serve fresh fish cuisine.

The Cabo de Gata salt flats extend across 400 hectares between the road and the sea. Over 80 bird species frequent these shallow basins, including flamingos visible from observation posts along the site.
Climb up to Mirador de La Amatista for a plunging view over the volcanic cliffs and Pico de los Frailes, the park's highest point. The violet-hued rocks give the viewpoint its name.

activities.text: Coastal hike between Genoveses and Mónsul along the ridges. The trail follows the cliffs and crosses several deserted little coves with colorful geological formations—white, orange, gray.
Snorkeling in the clear waters around San José. Posidonia seagrass meadows shelter wrasses, white seabreams, and octopuses among the volcanic rocks.
Flamingo and black-winged stilt watching at the salt flats, ideally late in the day when the low light colors the basins pink and gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert

map_keywords: Playa de Mónsul Cabo de Gata
Playa de los Genoveses San José
Arrecife de las Sirenas Cabo de Gata
Las Salinas de Cabo de Gata
Mirador de La Amatista Cabo de Gata

places.name: Cabo De Palos

visits.text: The Cabo de Palos lighthouse rises 81 meters above the sea, built in 1865. Climb 300 steps for views to Alicante on clear days and the distant Algerian coast. Spain's second-highest lighthouse.
Calblanque Regional Park protects fossil dunes, virgin beaches, and salt lagoons. Coastal trails wind from Cala Flores to Calarreona through untouched wilderness.
The Islas Hormigas rise 2.5 nautical miles offshore. Volcanic seabeds, giant groupers, and posidonia meadows—voted Europe's best dive site in 2016 and 2018.
The fishing village retains whitewashed houses, a harbor lined with brightly painted boats, and grilled-fish restaurants that open onto the sea.

activities.text: Hike from Cabo de Palos to Calblanque. Start at the harbor, climb to the viewpoint, then descend to Cala Marina. 4 km one way, 1 hour 15 minutes.
The Marchamalo salt pans host flamingos, black-winged stilts, and avocets. Birdwatching hide, best from September to April.
Lunch at the harbor: grilled sea bream, red mullet, octopus. Terraces overlook the bobbing fishing boats at anchor.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida

map_keywords: Playa de Levante Cabo de Palos

visits.text: The Torre de Bujaco (12th century) towers over Plaza Mayor. This 25-meter Almohad tower, built on Roman foundations, once guarded the northwestern flank of the walled city.
Step through the Arco de la Estrella, main gateway to the walled city since the 15th century. The Catholic Monarchs swore their oath to honor Cáceres' privileges here in 1477 and 1478.
The Museo de Cáceres occupies two 16th-century palaces. In the basement, a perfectly preserved Almohad cistern reveals the medieval city's ingenious water system.
Plaza de Santa María brings together the Co-Cathedral (13th century) and the palaces of noble families. The transition from Romanesque to Gothic is visible in the sculpted portals.
Wander through the Jewish quarter's whitewashed alleyways. The Ermita de San Antonio rises on the ruins of an ancient synagogue.
The Almohad ramparts stretch for 1,174 meters with some thirty towers. Climb to the Baluarte de los Pozos for sweeping views across the city.
Make your way to Plaza de San Jorge to see the Iglesia de San Francisco Javier. This 18th-century Jesuit baroque church conceals an ancient underground cistern.

activities.text: Climb the ramparts at sunset. The golden stone blazes with light as the towers stand silhouetted against the Extremadura plains.
Sample Torta del Casar, a creamy sheep's milk cheese with an oozing, spreadable texture. Local cheesemakers offer guided tastings.
Ibérico tapas on Plaza Mayor. The pata negra ham comes from pigs fattened on acorns in the surrounding oak forests.
After nightfall, the palaces light up. Walk through medieval lanes where not a single modern sign breaks the spell.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas
Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

places.name: Cadaques

visits.text: Cadaqués is accessible only by a winding road from Roses - which long preserved it from mass tourism and attracted Dalí. The village of white houses by the water has changed very little since the early 20th century. Start with the 16th-century Church of Santa Maria, whose gilded baroque altarpiece was painted by local artists. The port with its colorful boats moored facing the white facades is one of the most copied views of the Costa Brava.

The Salvador Dalí House-Museum in Portlligat, 2 km north of the village, is the only permanent residence the artist ever had. In 1930, he bought a 22 m² fisherman's hut - penniless, estranged from his family - and expanded it over 40 years by buying neighboring cottages, piece by piece. He lived and worked there until Gala's death in 1982, then never returned. Reservations are mandatory and groups are limited to 8 people - book at least 2 to 3 weeks in advance in summer.

Cap de Creus, 15 km from Cadaqués, is the easternmost point of the Iberian Peninsula and one of Catalonia's wildest natural parks. The granite rocks sculpted by the tramontana - the north wind that blows here up to 150 km/h - directly inspired Dalí's lunar landscapes. The lighthouse restaurant offers a 180-degree view of the sea. Bring a jacket even in summer: the tramontana can pick up without warning.

activities.text: Hike to Cap Norfeu, between Roses and Cadaqués: from the viewpoint, the view plunges over a succession of wild coves accessible only on foot or by boat. The well-marked 6 km round-trip trail from the parking lot takes about 3 hours.

In late afternoon, settle into one of the tapas bars on Cadaqués' waterfront. The low light on the white facades and boats at this hour is extraordinary - Dalí himself said that Cadaqués had taught him aesthetics. Grilled cuttlefish and Empordà anchovies are the local specialties to order.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

map_keywords: Salvador Dalí House Museum Portlligat Cadaqués

visits.text: Cádiz Cathedral blends Baroque grandeur with neoclassical restraint. Its construction spanned 116 years—from 1722 to 1838—under five successive architects. The dome, sheathed in gleaming golden tiles, once served as a landmark for sailors navigating the Atlantic. In the crypt lies composer Manuel de Falla, a native son of the city.

Climb the 170 steps of Torre del Poniente for a sweeping 360-degree panorama of Cádiz Bay, whitewashed rooftops, and the endless Atlantic. This vantage point makes it clear why the Phoenicians chose this peninsula in 1100 BC, making Cádiz the oldest continuously inhabited city in Western Europe.

The Roman Theatre of Gades was discovered by chance in 1980 beneath the houses of El Pópulo quarter. Built in the 1st century BC by Balbus, it seated 20,000 spectators. With a diameter of 120 meters, it ranks among the largest Roman theatres in the empire, a testament to Cádiz's ancient prominence.

Enter the Barrio del Pópulo through one of its three 13th-century medieval arches: Arco del Pópulo, Arco de la Rosa, or Arco de los Blancos. This oldest neighborhood in Cádiz preserves its labyrinth of narrow lanes and shaded squares, straddling Roman ruins and medieval foundations.

Torre Tavira stands tallest among the 160 watchtowers that Cádiz merchants built to spot their ships arriving from the Americas. At 45 meters high, its camera obscura projects live images of the city onto a concave screen—a mesmerizing 18th-century technology still captivating visitors today.

Walk along the Paseo Campo del Sur facing the ocean to reach Parque Genovés. This clifftop botanical garden showcases century-old palms, artificial waterfalls, and exotic species brought back from the Americas by Cádiz navigators during the golden age of transatlantic trade.

activities.text: La Caleta beach, the only beach in the historic center, nestles between the castles of Santa Catalina and San Sebastián. It featured in the James Bond film "Die Another Day." At sunset, colorful fishing boats and the castle silhouette create a picture-perfect scene against the amber sky.

Step into a freiduría and order a paper cone of pescaíto frito: crispy-fried shrimp, baby squid, hake strips, and bienmesabe in golden batter. This is Cádiz's signature street food, best enjoyed standing in the lively La Viña neighborhood with a cold beer in hand.

The Gran Teatro Falla, built in neo-Mudéjar style, hosts Cádiz Carnival every February—one of Spain's most celebrated festivals. Outside carnival season, catch a performance in this 19th-century architectural gem that seamlessly blends Moorish and Spanish influences.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages

activities.text: Water sports and swimming in the shallow waters of Cala Bassa

activities.text: End of day to the rhythm of percussion during sunset at Benirras
