visits.text: Swimming and snorkeling in the turquoise waters of Cala Comte
Descent to the small cove of Cala Escondida (Racó d'en Xic)

activities.text: Sunset watching over the islets of s'Espartar, des Bosc and sa Conillera

days_plan.itin_title: Ibiza and Formentera Guide: Beyond the Clubs to Turquoise Coves and Beaches

visits.text: Cala Galdana curves in a perfect horseshoe bay spanning 300 meters of powdery sand. Calm, shallow waters and Blue Flag status make it Menorca's most family-friendly beach.
Cala Macarella, reachable on foot from Cala Galdana, dazzles with brilliant white sand embraced by 40-meter cliffs. Dense pine forests shade the approach to this postcard-perfect cove.
Cala Macarelleta, a tiny adjacent cove, lies just 15 minutes beyond Macarella. Turquoise waters lap against pristine shores in this wonderfully unspoiled hideaway.
Viewpoints punctuate the coastal trail between Galdana and Macarelleta, offering vertiginous views down limestone cliffs to hidden coves gleaming impossibly blue below.
Cala Mitjana, accessible via the Camí de Cavalls, reveals an isolated beach surrounded by lush vegetation. Far fewer visitors means rare tranquility even in summer.

activities.text: The Camí de Cavalls GR223, a historic 185-kilometer trail, encircles the entire island along the coast. The Galdana-Macarella section covers an easy, scenic three kilometers.
The hike to Macarelleta winds through fragrant pine forests and rocky ravines. Well-marked trails lead over dramatic terrain with spectacular coastal vistas at every turn.
Shaded picnic areas beneath umbrella pines offer perfect lunch spots. Bring provisions from Cala Galdana to enjoy lazy afternoon meals in dappled Mediterranean light.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Discovery of Cala Salada cove surrounded by pine trees
Swimming in the clear waters of Cala Saladeta

activities.text: Walk to the caves of Ses Fontanelles above the coves

days_plan.itin_title: Ibiza and Formentera Guide: Beyond the Clubs to Turquoise Coves and Beaches

places.name: Calatanazor

visits.text: To optimize the day before heading to Segovia in the afternoon, start at opening time with La Fuentona de Muriel: a karst spring that surges from a rocky amphitheater as if water appears from nowhere. An accessible trail follows the Abión River to the spring, then a fork leads to a waterfall (with water in wet season). Start from the Muriel de la Fuente parking lot, about 7 km round trip, 3 hours with breaks.

Two kilometers from La Fuentona, the Sabinar de Calatañazor Nature Reserve is Europe's densest forest of Spanish junipers (Juniperus thurifera). Some specimens reach 15 meters in height and 5 meters in diameter, with ages potentially exceeding 400 years. The resinous, slightly incense-like scent emanating from these twisted trees is unforgettable. Allow about an hour's walk.

Finish with the village of Calatañazor—'Qal'at al-Nusur' in Arabic, the 'castle of eagles.' Declared a historic and artistic ensemble in 1962, it preserves its arcaded medieval main street, adobe and juniper-beam houses, and castle ruins. History says that here in July 1002 Christian armies inflicted on Arab general Almanzor the wound from which he would die a month later, giving birth to the Spanish saying 'At Calatañazor, Almanzor lost his drum.' Head to Segovia around 1pm.

activities.text: Walk through the juniper forest where centenarian trees create a mystical atmosphere. Thrushes, magpies and crows feed on their berries in winter.
Push on to the natural monument of La Fuentona, a karst rock spring giving birth to the Abión River. Underwater caves open in its depths.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Casa del Parque Sabinar Muriel de la Fuente

visits.text: Twenty-five minutes' drive east from Medinaceli brings you to the Monastery of Santa María de Huerta, one of Spain's most beautiful Cistercian monasteries. Founded in 1162, expropriated in 1835 during the Mendizábal disentailment, then returned to the order in 1930, it's still home to some twenty monks. Push through the door and you enter eight centuries of silence. The Gothic cloister (13th century), with its pointed arcades and austere garden, prepares you for the shock of the refectory—an immense hall covered with sexpartite rib vaults, pierced by tall windows that flood the space with light. It's one of Europe's most beautiful monastic refectories.

Continue 1h15 west to enter Aragon and reach Calatayud, second city of Zaragoza province. The ancient Bilbilis (birthplace of the poet Martial) is dominated by the ruins of the Castillo de Ayub, the Arab citadel that gave the town its name (Qal'at Ayyub—Ayyub's castle). But it's Aragonese Mudéjar that makes Calatayud rich: the Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor boasts a unique open-air cloister and a Mudéjar brick portal decorated with ceramics. Calatayud's Mudéjar art forms part of the UNESCO inscription of Mudéjar Architecture of Aragon (2001).

Stroll through Calatayud's historic centre, between Plaza de España (with its fountain and arcades) and the lanes climbing towards the castle ruins. The church of San Pedro de los Francos (14th century), with its leaning Mudéjar tower (yes, it leans—about 2 degrees), is the building where the Aragonese Cortes proclaimed Ferdinand the Catholic heir to the throne in 1461. The medieval Jewish cemetery, discovered in 1980, testifies to the significant Sephardic community that lived here before the 1492 expulsion.

activities.text: If you're a wine lover, Calatayud sits at the heart of the DO Calatayud, an appellation producing Garnacha from old vines growing on slate soils at 700–1,000 m altitude. Several cellars offer tastings. Pair your glass with Aragonese tapas: migas, roast ternasco lamb, or frutas de Aragón (candied fruits coated in chocolate).

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

places.name: Campo De Criptana

visits.text: Ten white windmills stand atop the Sierra de los Molinos, remnants of the 32 that dominated the town in the 16th century. Three—Infante, Sardinero and Burleta—retain their period machinery intact and are designated national monuments.
The Poyatos mill serves as both tourist office and interpretation center. Inside, models and maps explain the workings of Manchegan windmills and how the blades were oriented to face the wind.

From the Cerro de la Paz, the view sweeps across the white rooftops of the village and the immensity of the Castilian plain. This is where Cervantes set Don Quixote's battle against the 'thirty outrageous giants.'

Descend toward the Pósito Real, a 16th-century grain warehouse expanded under Charles III. Its façade bears two coats of arms of the Order of Santiago. It regulated the price of grain during periods of scarcity.
Cave houses carved into the rock line the windmill route. The Casa Cueva de Mambrino houses a museum that recreates troglodyte life with agricultural tools and 16th-century utensils.

The hermitage of the Virgen de Criptana, in Renaissance style, has dominated the hill since the 16th century. Below, the hermitage of Santa Ana and that of the Vera Cruz complete the religious heritage.

activities.text: Arrive at dawn on the Sierra de los Molinos. The absence of vegetation, arid soil and low morning light create an austere, photogenic landscape—the windmills stand out one by one against the horizon.
The Bodega Cooperativa Vinícola del Carmen, founded in 1897, is considered the oldest wine cooperative in Spain. Taste DO La Mancha wines made from Tempranillo and Airén grapes.
Sample gachas manchegas, a flour porridge accompanied by chorizo and fried lard, and Manchegan ratatouille (pisto), typical regional dishes.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains

map_keywords: Molinos de Viento Campo de Criptana
Sierra de los Molinos Campo de Criptana
Cerro de la Paz Campo de Criptana
Ermita de la Virgen de Criptana

places.name: Cangas De Onis

visits.text: The so-called 'Roman' bridge of Cangas de Onís actually dates from the 13th century, built on ancient foundations. Its raised central arch, flanked by two smaller asymmetric arches, spans the Sella River. A replica of the Cross of Victory has hung from it since 1939.
Steps from the bridge, the Hermitage of Santa Cruz conceals a megalithic dolmen beneath its altar. This small edifice founded in 737 covers one of the oldest funerary monuments in Asturias.

Take the winding road up to the Sanctuary of Covadonga, birthplace of the Reconquest. The neo-Romanesque basilica in pink limestone, built between 1877 and 1901, raises its twin spires above the narrow valley.

Descend into the Santa Cueva, a cave carved into the cliff face that houses the statue of Our Lady of Covadonga and the tomb of King Pelayo. Beneath the rocky vault, a waterfall plunges into a pool of green water. Below, the Fuente de los Siete Caños invites the superstitious: drinking from all seven spouts supposedly promises marriage within the year.

Cangas de Onís was the first capital of the Kingdom of Asturias until 774. On the central square, a bronze statue of Pelayo the Conqueror commemorates the victory of 722 that launched eight centuries of Reconquest.

activities.text: Embark on a canoe descent of the Sella, a popular route between Cangas de Onís and Ribadesella. The river flows through landscapes of green hills, dense forests, and small Asturian villages.
Try the fabada asturiana in one of the town's restaurants. This hearty stew of white beans, chorizo, morcilla, and pork is Asturias' signature dish.
On Sunday mornings, Cangas de Onís market takes over the square with stalls of Cabrales cheese, heather honey, artisan cider, and smoked mountain charcuterie.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

map_keywords: Ermita Santa Cruz Cangas de Onís

visits.text: From Granada, expect about an hour's drive to reach Lanjarón, the gateway to the Alpujarras. This small spa town is famous for its mineral water (Spain's best-selling brand) and its ruined Arab castle, the last Moorish stronghold to fall in 1500. Make a quick stop to refill your water bottle at one of the public fountains and grab a coffee on the plaza before continuing.

Pass through Órgiva (the valley's main town, market day on Thursdays) and climb towards the Barranco de Poqueira, the spectacular gorge where the three most famous villages of the Alpujarras cascade down the mountainside. First stop: Pampaneira (1,058 m), the lower village. Its 340 inhabitants live among whitewashed houses with flat roofs (the "terraos"), sloping cobbled lanes, covered passages (the "tinaos") and spring-fed fountains. On the main square, taste the water from the San Antonio fountain - legend says it helps you find love. Pampaneira is also the Alpujarras' artisan village: the "jarapas", those colorful rugs woven from recycled fibers, are the local specialty. Step into a weaving workshop to watch the artisans at work.

Climb to Bubión (1,296 m), 2 km higher up - on foot via the old mule track (30 minutes) or by car. This is the quietest of the three villages, a member of Spain's "slow tourism" network. The Berber architecture is best preserved here: cubic houses with flat slate roofs, cylindrical chimneys topped with a stone cap. At the Museo Casa Alpujarreña, you'll see how the inhabitants lived in the past - kitchen, bedroom and workshop recreated in a traditional house. Don't miss the Artesa de los Moros, a large 2-meter stone whose original purpose remains mysterious.

Capileira (1,436 m) is the highest of the trio, at the gates of Sierra Nevada National Park. From the Mirador de Perchel, the view is vertiginous: to the north, the peak of Veleta (3,396 m); to the south, the rooftops of Bubión and Pampaneira cascading down the valley; and on clear days, the Mediterranean and even the Rif mountains of Morocco on the horizon. This is where the trails begin towards the highest peaks of the Iberian Peninsula, including Mulhacén (3,479 m). The water from the public fountains comes directly from the Sierra Nevada's snowmelt - it's ice-cold even in midsummer.

activities.text: If you have the time and energy, the circular hike linking the three villages via the old mule tracks is the best way to discover the valley. Allow 3 to 4 hours for the complete loop, through chestnut groves, walnut trees, terraced olive groves and irrigation channels inherited from the Moorish era. You might spot Iberian ibex on the slopes.

In the evening, dine in Capileira or Bubión on a "plato alpujarreño" - the local dish: potatoes, fried egg, blood sausage (morcilla), ham and peppers. It's hearty and perfect after a day in the mountains. Pair it with a local Alpujarras wine.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras

visits.text: You're leaving Andalusia for the Murcia region. Caravaca de la Cruz is the fifth holy city of Christianity, along with Jerusalem, Rome, Santiago de Compostela and Santo Toribio de Liébana. This privilege, granted by Pope John Paul II in 1998, allows a perpetual Jubilee to be celebrated every seven years. Everything revolves around the Vera Cruz, a reliquary containing fragments of Christ's cross. According to legend, in 1231, the Moorish king Abou Zeïd asked a captive priest to celebrate mass in the castle. Since a cross was missing, two angels brought one from heaven - and the Moorish king converted to Christianity.

Climb to the Castillo via the steep paved lane that runs through the medieval quarter. The fortress, of Moorish origin (10th-11th century), was enlarged in the 15th century by the Templars then taken over by the Order of Santiago. Fourteen towers guard the walls. Inside stands the Sanctuary of the Santísima y Vera Cruz, built from 1617 onwards - its baroque facade in red marble from Cehegín is spectacular. The interior museum displays the chasuble of the priest Chirinos at the moment of the legendary apparition, as well as paintings dedicated to the miracle. From the ramparts, the view embraces the entire town and the mountains of northwestern Murcia.

Descend into the center to see the El Salvador church, considered the pinnacle of Murcian Renaissance art. The Carmelite convent, founded by Saint John of the Cross in 1586, is a place of calm and contemplation. On calle Mayor and calle Rafael Tegeo, admire the manor houses with wrought-iron balconies. The Templete (or Bañadero) is a small hexagonal baroque building where, every May 3rd, the relic of the Vera Cruz is bathed in water - a ritual celebrated since 1384.

activities.text: Late in the day, go relax at the Fuentes del Marqués, a natural area just outside town: an abundant spring surrounded by poplars and plane trees, with shaded paths, caves carved by Arab settlers (once used for tanning hides) and the Torre de los Templarios. It's the locals' favorite walking spot.

If you visit between May 1st and 5th, you'll witness the fiestas of the Santísima y Vera Cruz, declared of International Tourist Interest. On May 2nd, the Caballos del Vino (Horses of Wine) gallop up the castle slope, sumptuously harnessed - a unique spectacle. The Moors and Christians reenactments in the following days are equally colorful.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras

visits.text: The Roman Theater was built between 5 and 1 BC under Emperor Augustus. Its 7,000-seat cavea is carved into living rock, the stage spans 43 meters. Rafael Moneo's award-winning museum tunnels through the Riquelme Palace and the ruins of Santa María Church before opening onto the ancient tiers.
The Muralla del Mar follows the ancient harbor walls. The 18th-century naval Arsenal preserves dry docks and launching basins still in use.
A panoramic elevator whisks you up to the Castillo de la Concepción. Gaze across the harbor, explore Punic and Roman remains, and walk medieval battlements.
The Roman Forum beneath Plaza del Ayuntamiento displays columns, mosaics, and thermal baths from the ancient port quarter—one of Spain's most extensive urban archaeological sites.
The Palacio Consistorial lines modernist balconies and a neo-baroque tower along Calle Mayor, a belle-époque landmark.

activities.text: Take a narrated harbor cruise departing from the port. Views of the fortifications, naval history, 45 minutes on the water.
The Cabo de Palos marine reserve, 30 km east, protects 19 km² of posidonia meadows and coral reefs. Dive centers offer guided dives to the wreck of the Sirio and volcanic underwater cliffs.
Caldero is fisherman's rice cooked in fish broth and served in two courses: first the saffron-scented soup, then the rice topped with garlicky alioli.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida

places.name: Castillo De Loarre

visits.text: The Castle of Loarre, built from 1020 by King Sancho III of Navarre on a rocky spur at 1,071 meters elevation, is the best-preserved Romanesque fortress in Europe. Its 172-meter perimeter wall, flanked by eleven towers, dominates the plain of the Hoya de Huesca. The site served as a filming location for "Kingdom of Heaven" and "The Name of the Rose."

Pass through the Romanesque portal adorned with a checkerboard pattern in the Jaca style, then climb the barrel-vaulted staircase. The Crypt of Santa Quiteria, to the left, served as a burial site. Two narrow staircases lead to the Church of San Pedro, a marvel of Romanesque art: its 26-meter-high dome crowns a semicircular apse with capitals carved with biblical and fantastical motifs.

The keep, 22 meters high and spanning five floors, was connected to the castle by a drawbridge. The Torre de la Reina, above the entrance to the military section, offers a vertiginous panorama over the Pyrenean foothills and agricultural plains below.

In 1071, King Sancho Ramírez transformed the fortress into an Augustinian monastery. The Church of Santa María, predating the monastery, preserves alabaster stained-glass windows whose white, milky light bathes the nave with an unexpected softness in this military setting.

From the parking lot, before entering the castle, take time to admire the fortress silhouette that seems to spring from the rock itself. The winding road leading up crosses a landscape of scrubland and limestone cliffs where griffon vultures nest.

activities.text: Walk the trail that circles the castle's outer ramparts, added in the 13th century. The path offers varied perspectives on the towers, walls, and cliffs that naturally protect the fortress's southern side.

The limestone cliffs around Loarre shelter a colony of griffon vultures. From the castle's surroundings, look up: these raptors with nearly 3-meter wingspans circle above the rock faces, carried by thermal currents.

The Collegiate Church of Santa María de Bolea, about twenty kilometers away, deserves a detour for its monumental 15th-century Hispano-Flemish altarpiece. This 16-meter-high altarpiece, with panels painted in extraordinary finesse, is considered one of Aragon's most important.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragon Circuit: Zaragoza & Mudéjar Villages

map_keywords: Iglesia de San Pedro Castillo de Loarre
Torre del Homenaje Castillo de Loarre

places.name: Castro Urdiales

visits.text: The Church of Santa María de la Asunción sits enthroned on the rocky promontory above the harbor. Built from the 13th to 15th centuries in rose-colored stone, it is the finest example of Cantabrian Gothic. Its flying buttresses and soaring naves rival French cathedrals.
The Castle of Santa Ana, a 12th-century fortress, houses a lighthouse installed in 1853 in its southeast tower. In the courtyard, a Roman milestone bears witness to the ancient road to Flaviobriga.
Cross the medieval bridge—locally nicknamed the 'Roman bridge'—to reach the hermitage of Santa Ana, perched on a rock battered by the waves. At high tide, the sea surges beneath the arches.
Plaza del Ayuntamiento is ringed by arcades and dominated by the town hall with its crenellated tower. Café terraces come alive from aperitif hour onward.
The lanes of the Puebla Vieja, declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble in 1978, preserve their wooden-balconied houses and medieval facades.
The Palacio de Ocharan, with its Greek-inspired columned portico, illustrates 19th-century bourgeois splendor. Its gardens open onto the sea.

activities.text: Castro Urdiales is a working fishing port. Grilled sardines, preserved anchovies and bonito stew are the specialties to savor beneath the harbor arcades.
Walk along Avenida de la Constitución, parallel to the sea, then descend to Brazomar beach. The golden sand stretches at the harbor's foot, facing the cliffs.
The pintxo bars in the center show Basque Country influence from nearby. Order at the counter and pair with a chilled txakoli overlooking the harbor.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María de la Asunción Castro Urdiales
Castillo-Faro de Santa Ana Castro Urdiales
Ermita de Santa Ana Castro Urdiales

visits.text: Explore the archaeological site of Cástulo, 5 kilometers from Linares, one of the most important Ibero-Roman settlements on the peninsula. This ancient city was the capital of Oretania and a strategic point in the wars between Rome and Carthage, with remains spanning from the Bronze Age to medieval times.

activities.text: Walk through the archaeological excavations where exceptional Roman mosaics, walls, necropolises, and public buildings have been discovered. The site continues to reveal treasures and offers a unique glimpse into life in ancient Hispania.

days_plan.itin_title: Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: The road from Úbeda to Cazorla crosses a sea of olive trees stretching as far as the eye can see—the province of Jaén has over 66 million olive trees. Cazorla is a white village clinging to a hillside at the foot of the natural park. Plaza de Santa María, one of the two main squares, is built around the spectacular ruins of the Iglesia de Santa María—a 16th-century Renaissance church bombed during the Civil War and never rebuilt. Its open-air arches, with the mountain backdrop, create a unique theatrical setting.

The Castillo de la Yedra (Ivy Castle), of Arab origin then enlarged by the Christians, dominates Cazorla from its rocky spur. It houses the Museo de Artes y Costumbres Populares del Alto Guadalquivir—an ethnographic museum tracing rural life in the sierras. From the ramparts, the view plunges over Cazorla's rooftops and the first forests of the natural park. It's also an excellent spot to watch griffon vultures soaring above the gorges.

Two kilometres from Cazorla, the village of La Iruela has one of the most spectacular sites in the province: a castle perched on a rocky pinnacle so narrow it seems to defy gravity. The adjoining ruined church, clinging to the same rock, reinforces the vertiginous impression. The climb is short but steep—the view from the summit embraces the entire Cerezuelo valley and the distant sierras.

Full day in the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park—at 210,000 hectares, it's Spain's largest protected area and Europe's second largest. Declared a Biosphere Reserve by UNESCO in 1983, it's home to over 2,170 plant species including 34 endemics (Cazorla violet, the world's smallest narcissus, a carnivorous plant). Wildlife includes ibex, deer, wild boar, mouflon, griffon vultures, golden eagles, and the rare bearded vulture.

The Río Borosa hike is the park's flagship trail—and one of Andalusia's most beautiful. From the Torre del Vinagre visitor centre, the path follows the Borosa, the Guadalquivir's first major tributary, for about 10 km one way. You first cross the Cerrada de Elías, a narrow gorge where the path is carved into the rock above the turquoise water—wooden walkways span the torrent. Higher up, the path passes through forests of centuries-old pines before reaching two mountain lakes, Laguna de Aguas Negras and Laguna de Valdeazores. Allow 6 to 7 hours return. Bring water, food, and good shoes—there's no refreshment along the trail.

activities.text: Cazorla is the ideal starting point for exploring the natural park, but it's also a town where you eat well. The restaurants around Plaza de la Constitución serve game (deer, wild boar), Segureño lamb, seasonal mushrooms, and of course Sierra de Cazorla PDO olive oil on everything. Rin-ran (cod, tomato, and pepper salad) is the local speciality. Dine early—mountain restaurants close earlier than in the city.

If you prefer a less physical option, operators offer 4x4 excursions through the park, with stops at viewpoints and wildlife observation points. The Tranco route follows the large dam and crosses pine forests with spectacular views. You can also take a boat trip on the Embalse del Tranco—the artificial lake at the heart of the park. It's from the lake that you have the best chances of seeing deer coming to drink at dusk.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: The Fuenfría Valley Interpretation Center unveils the natural and cultural history of the valley. Permanent exhibitions showcase flora, fauna, and human heritage. Try the "Treasure of La Tuerta" scavenger hunt to discover the park's riches.
The Calzada Romana (Antonine Way XXIV) once linked Toledo to Segovia. Restored in 2009, this ancient section reveals the original Roman route. Informative panels line the path, marked by metal posts.

The 18th-century Puente del Descalzo spans the Arroyo de la Fuenfría. Three granite arches with stone benches built into the pillars. Commissioned by Philip V to connect the Royal Palace of La Granja.

Dense forests of Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris)—the best preserved in the Central System—rise on all sides. Oak, cherry, maple and ash mix near the Arroyo de la Venta. Clearings open to vast panoramas.
Puerto de la Fuenfría at 1,796 meters marks the boundary between Madrid and Segovia. A crossroads of historic paths where Calzada Romana, Calzada Borbónica and modern roads converge.

activities.text: Follow the Calzada Romana up to Puerto de la Fuenfría. A gentle ascent through pine forests, 641 meters of elevation over 7.9 km. Track the green circles painted on trees to stay on course.
The Camino Schmidt, laid out in 1926 by mountaineer Eduardo Schmidt, traces the base of Siete Picos. Mature Scots pine forests alternate with meadows. Follow white circles marking the way.
Return via the scenic mountain train (Cercanías C-9). This historic line snakes through the Sierra with spectacular views of valleys and peaks. Departs from Cercedilla station.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

visits.text: Santa Maria Cathedral, built between 1300 and 1365 in Catalan Gothic style, rises where a mosque once stood. The baroque altarpiece gleams, and you can climb the bell tower for rooftop views.
Plaça des Born assembles magnificent 17th-century aristocratic palaces around a central obelisk commemorating the town's brave resistance against Turkish invaders in 1558.
Ciutadella's harbor cuts inland like a dramatic fjord between limestone cliffs. Colorful facades and bustling terraces line the low quays where boats bob gently.
Palau Salort occasionally opens for guided tours, revealing aristocratic interiors, period furniture, and shaded patios that whisper of centuries past.
Museu Palacio Can Saura displays ethnographic and archaeological collections, including the haunting remains of an Andalusian necropolis discovered beneath the building.
Carrer Ses Voltes, lined with graceful arcades, concentrates shops and restaurants beneath medieval arches. These cobbled lanes form the atmospheric heart of the old town.

activities.text: The Sant Joan festival on June 23-24 erupts in spectacular tradition as sleek black Menorcan horses rear and dance through crowds in an ancient ritual unlike anything else.
Old town bakeries tempt with chocolate-filled ensaimadas and formatjades—traditional pastries that embody island flavors in every flaky, sweet bite.
Plaça des Born's terraces come alive at dusk. Sip drinks facing illuminated palaces and the town hall as the golden hour transforms the square into pure magic.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

visits.text: El Capricho de Gaudí, built between 1883 and 1885, is one of the architect's rare works outside Catalonia. Its watchtower, sunflower ceramics and columned portico draw on oriental art with Gaudí's unmistakable flair.
The neo-Gothic Sobrellano Palace was commissioned by the Marquis of Comillas. Beside it, the chapel-pantheon in matching style houses the family tombs.

Climb toward the Pontifical University. This imposing neo-Gothic edifice with Mudéjar influences crowns the hill of La Cardosa. Its red-orange brick stands out against the green of the surrounding hills.
Comillas cemetery, enclosed by medieval walls, is crowned by the Ángel Exterminador, a modernist sculpture keeping watch over the graves facing the sea.
Descend to Plaza del Corro de Campios, the old town's heart. Mansions built by 'Indianos'—émigrés who returned wealthy from the Americas—line the cobbled streets.
The small fishing harbor and sandy beach nestle at the village's foot. The ocean horizon opens onto the dunes of Oyambre Natural Park.

activities.text: Savor octopus, baby squid and shellfish at the harbor restaurants. Cantabrian cuisine celebrates raw ingredients, simply cooked.
Follow the coast toward Oyambre Natural Park. The trail crosses dunes, cliffs and marshes rich in birdlife, with views over wild beaches.
Head to Oyambre beach in the late afternoon. The dunes gilded by the setting sun offer one of the most beautiful panoramas on the Cantabrian coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa

visits.text: Twelve white windmills line the ridge of Cerro Calderico. Built in the late 16th century by the Order of Saint John of Jerusalem, each bears the name of a character from Don Quixote: Sancho, Rocinante, Clavileño…

Push open the door of the Sancho mill, the best preserved of the dozen. Its original mechanism still functions and grinds grain during the Fiesta de la Rosa del Azafrán on the last weekend of October.

The castle of La Muela dominates the hill alongside the windmills. This 10th-century fortress of Arab origin became the seat of the Knights of the Order of Saint John. Its restored halls and ramparts offer a 360° panorama over the plains of the Meseta.

Descend into the village of Consuegra. Plaza de España, a former Roman forum, hosts a Renaissance town hall and the 17th-century Los Corredores building, typical of Manchegan architecture with its wooden galleries.
The church of San Juan Bautista and the cobbled lanes of the historic center reveal several Mudéjar hermitages and whitewashed houses with ochre tile roofs.

activities.text: Head to Cerro Calderico in late afternoon. The golden light illuminates the white windmills against a backdrop of endless plains—the perfect moment to photograph this icon of La Mancha.
The Consuegra region is one of the main terroirs of Spanish saffron. Local cooperatives offer tastings of olive oil and Manchego saffron.
Walk up from the parking lot at the base of the hill (20 min). The gradual ascent reveals the windmills and castle one by one, adding suspense to the panorama.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains

visits.text: The Mezquita-Cathedral of Córdoba is, without exaggeration, one of the most extraordinary monuments on the planet. Begun in 785 by Abd al-Rahman I on the site of a Visigothic basilica, enlarged three times over two centuries, then transformed into a cathedral after the Reconquest of 1236, it layers civilizations within a single 23,400 m² space. Upon entering, you're seized by the forest of more than 850 columns of marble, jasper, and granite—some salvaged from Roman and Visigothic ruins—connected by red-and-white bicolor arches that unfold as far as the eye can see. Push on to Al-Hakam II's mihrab: its golden and blue mosaics, created by artisans sent by the Byzantine emperor, rank among the finest in Islamic art. Then discover the Renaissance cathedral planted in the middle of the mosque—Charles V, who had authorized it, reportedly regretted upon visiting the works: 'You have destroyed what was unique to build what can be seen everywhere.' Arrive at 8:30 AM for free entry (until 9:30 AM)—after that, the queue grows quickly.

Leaving the Mezquita, lose yourself in the Judería, the medieval Jewish quarter that's part of Córdoba's UNESCO listing. Its whitewashed lanes so narrow you can touch both walls with outstretched arms, its patios glimpsed through half-open doors, its pots of geraniums clinging to facades—this is the image of Córdoba you'll take home. Don't miss the Calleja de las Flores, a tiny dead-end alley that opens onto a perfect perspective of the Mezquita's bell tower framed by flowers. The synagogue (14th century), one of only three preserved in Spain along with those in Toledo, can be visited in 15 minutes—it's small, but the Mudéjar stuccowork is remarkable.

The Alcázar de los Reyes Cristianos, built in 1328 by Alfonso XI on the ruins of a Moorish palace, is especially famous for its terraced gardens—rectangular pools bordered by clipped hedges, cypresses, and orange trees that descend toward the Guadalquivir. This is where Ferdinand and Isabella planned the conquest of Granada and where Christopher Columbus presented his project for a voyage to the Indies in 1486. The interior is sober—vaulted halls, Roman mosaics, and Arab baths—but the gardens at sunset are worth the visit.

Cross the Puente Romano, Córdoba's Roman bridge—331 meters, 16 arches, built in the 1st century BC under Augustus, rebuilt several times since. In the middle, pause before the statue of the Archangel Raphael, patron of the city. On the other side, the Torre de la Calahorra (12th century), a former Arab fortified gate, houses a small museum on the Córdoba of three cultures (Muslim, Jewish, Christian). But above all, turn around: the view of the Mezquita and the historic center from the bridge, especially in late afternoon, is the finest in Córdoba.

Devote the morning to Medina Azahara (Madinat al-Zahra), the caliphal city that's been a UNESCO site since 2018. Eight kilometers west of Córdoba, at the foot of the Sierra Morena, Abd al-Rahman III ordered in 936 the construction of an entire palatine city to serve as the seat of the Caliphate of Córdoba. Over 75 years, 10,000 workers built a city of 112 hectares on three terraces—royal palaces at the top, gardens and administration in the middle, mosque and dwellings at the bottom. Then, in 1010, the civil war that ended the caliphate reduced it to ashes. It remained buried for a millennium, until its rediscovery in 1910. Today, only 10% has been excavated, but the Salón Rico (official reception hall), with its arches and carved stone panels of insane delicacy, conveys the measure of what this 'Andalusian Versailles' once was. The museum at the base of the site is excellent—start there to understand what you're about to see. Allow at least 2.5 hours.

Back in the city in the afternoon, visit the Palacio de Viana, nicknamed the 'Museum of Patios.' This aristocratic palace built between the 14th and 18th centuries has 12 patios and a garden—each with its own style, fountains, columns, and vegetation. It's the best way to understand Córdoba's patio culture, inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list since 2012 (Festival of the Patios, in May). If you're there in May, the private patios of the San Basilio and Alcázar Viejo neighborhoods open to the public—it's a spectacle of flowers, colors, and popular pride.

Push on to the Roman Temple on Calle Claudio Marcelo—the eleven Corinthian columns of this 1st-century temple, dedicated to imperial cult, recall that Córdoba was the capital of Roman Baetica and the birthplace of Seneca and Lucan. Just beside it, the remains of the Roman theatre (the largest in Spain in its time) are visible in the cellars of the Archaeological Museum.

Return to Córdoba for the final day, devoted to the less touristy neighborhoods. Start with the Plaza del Potro, mentioned by Cervantes in Don Quixote—the inn bordering it is where Sancho Panza supposedly lodged. The square houses the Museo de Bellas Artes (in the former Hospital de la Caridad, 15th century) and the Museo Julio Romero de Torres, dedicated to the most popular Córdoban painter of the early 20th century, famous for his portraits of Andalusian women with intense gazes.

Head up toward the San Lorenzo neighborhood, one of the most authentic in Córdoba. The church of San Lorenzo (13th century), with its Gothic rose window and arcaded portico, is one of the 'Fernandine churches' built after the Reconquest of 1236—Ferdinand III had 14 parishes constructed in the reconquered city. The neighborhood is calm, residential, with flowery patios you'll only glimpse by craning your neck through half-open doors—this is the Córdoba of Córdobans, far from the tourist groups of the Judería.

Finish with the Palacio de la Merced (18th century), an imposing Baroque building that now serves as the seat of the Provincial Council. Its two-level cloister, with pink marble columns and azulejos, is one of the finest Baroque spaces in Córdoba. Entry is free and the place is rarely mentioned in guidebooks.

activities.text: In the evening, settle into the taverns of the San Basilio neighborhood or the Plaza de la Corredera for a tapas crawl. Córdoba is a city of generous tapas: salmorejo (cold tomato soup thicker than gazpacho, served with ham and hard-boiled egg), flamenquines (breaded ham and pork roll), berenjenas con miel (fried aubergines with honey), and a glass of Montilla-Moriles, the local fortified wine that resembles sherry but with its own character. The Plaza de la Corredera, the only enclosed square plaza in Andalusia (17th century), is perfect for starting.

If you're in Córdoba between May and June, don't miss the flamenco shows in the peñas flamencas of the Santiago and San Lorenzo neighborhoods—more intimate and authentic venues than the tourist tablaos. Córdoba's flamenco guitar tradition is distinct, inherited from Paco Peña and Vicente Amigo.

For your final evening, treat yourself to dinner in the Plaza de las Tendillas or San Miguel neighborhood, where the tapas bars are frequented by locals rather than tourists. Order a salmorejo (the Córdoban version is the most authentic in Andalusia), rabo de toro (braised oxtail, Córdoba's emblematic dish), and a glass of Montilla-Moriles amontillado. From Córdoba, you can take the AVE back to Madrid, Seville, or Málaga, or continue by car toward Jaén (our 'Madrid to Cazorla' itinerary) or toward Granada.

days_plan.itin_title: Andalusia Road Trip: Seville, Córdoba, Granada & Ronda
Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches
Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches
Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

places.name: Coria

visits.text: Start the day at the Monastery of Yuste, a European Heritage site nestled in the Sierra de Gredos foothills near Cuacos de Yuste. This is where Charles V—Charles I of Spain and V of the Holy Roman Empire, the most powerful man in the world—chose to retire after abdicating in 1556 in favor of his son Philip II. He arrived on February 3, 1557, with an entourage of about sixty people. His chamber was positioned against the church choir so he could attend mass from his bed—he suffered from terrible gout that confined him to his litter. He died here on September 21, 1558, at age 58, from malarial fevers. The visit shows both cloisters (one Gothic, one Renaissance), the 15th-century church, and the emperor's apartments—surprisingly austere for the man who'd ruled an empire where the sun never set.

Head to Coria in the afternoon (50 km, 45 minutes). This town is one of Spain's oldest—the ancient Caurium of the Vettones, which became a Roman municipium. Its Roman walls, built between the 3rd and 4th centuries, are probably the best-preserved as a whole set in all of Europe: over 1,100 meters of ramparts with about twenty square towers and four gates, two of them original Roman (Puerta del Sol and Puerta de la Guía). The walls are 8 meters thick in places. Walk the circuit—it's about a 30-minute stroll that gives you vertigo when you think these stones have been standing for 1,700 years.

Coria's cathedral, built between the 15th and 18th centuries on the remains of a Visigothic church, blends Gothic, Plateresque, and Baroque styles. Its Plateresque facade is remarkable, as is its Churrigueresque tower offering superb views over town and countryside. The cathedral guards an extraordinary relic: what's claimed to be the tablecloth from Christ's Last Supper, attested in a papal bull from 1404. Admission is free and opening hours are generous (closed Monday mornings).

Don't miss the bridge without a river—Coria's most surreal image. This five-arched stone bridge, built in the 16th century probably on earlier Roman foundations, spanned the Río Alagón. But the river changed course—likely gradually following floods, worsened by the 1755 Lisbon earthquake—and now flows several hundred meters away. Result: a bridge without a river and a river without a bridge. Walk under the arches for the best photo, especially late afternoon.

If daylight permits, detour 30 minutes to Granadilla, a fascinating ghost village. This fortified villa of Almohad origin (9th century), which governed 15 municipalities under its jurisdiction in the Middle Ages, was emptied of inhabitants in 1955 when Franco's regime decided to build the Gabriel y Galán dam. Cruel irony: the water never reached the village. Declared a Conjunto Histórico-Artístico in 1980, it was partially restored through an educational program. You can visit the cobbled lanes, the circular wall (one of Spain's best-preserved alongside Lugo and Ávila), and the 15th-century castle built by the Duke of Alba, whose terrace offers striking views over the reservoir and hills.

activities.text: In the evening in Coria, try dulces de las monjas—cloistered convent sweets. The town's convents sell handmade pastries (quemados, dulces de yema) through a rotating hatch, without seeing the nuns who make them. It's a typically Spanish tradition that persists in Extremadura's small towns.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

map_keywords: Monasterio de Yuste Cuacos de Yuste

visits.text: The Corralejo Dunes Natural Park stretches across 2,600 hectares of pristine wilderness. These white dunes, formed from disintegrated seashells and marine organisms, create a Saharan landscape that contrasts dramatically with the dark volcanic rock.

Climb Montaña Roja, a 314-meter volcano at the park's southern edge. The summit rewards you with 360-degree panoramas over the dunes, the turquoise Atlantic, and the volcanic silhouette of Lobos Island.

The Grandes Playas stretch for 10 kilometers of pure beachfront bliss. Immaculate white sand, turquoise waters, and unobstructed views toward Lobos and Lanzarote. Stone wind shelters called corralitos dot the beach, offering refuge from the Atlantic breezes.
Flag Beach and El Médano attract surfers and kitesurfers from around the world. The constant Atlantic winds create ideal conditions for riding the waves and catching air.

activities.text: Take the ferry to Lobos Island, a 6-square-kilometer protected nature reserve. Once home to a colony of monk seals, the island now shelters the Punta Martiño lighthouse and preserved volcanic landscapes where time stands still.
Swim in the shallow lagoons sheltered by natural reefs. Watch for strong currents in certain areas—always check the flag warnings before entering the water.
The Kite Festival in November draws participants from across the globe. Multicolored kites dance across the natural park's skies throughout an entire weekend of celebration.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

visits.text: Visit to Cortegana castle and the historic center
Walk in the village of Almonaster la Real

activities.text: Hiking in the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva Province, Spain: Mining Heritage and Untouched Atlantic Beaches

visits.text: Jameos del Agua is César Manrique's first major work in Lanzarote, opened to the public in 1966. The site sits inside a lava tunnel formed by the Corona volcano's eruption about 4,000 years ago—a 6 km tube, 1.5 km of which continues under the ocean (the Atlantis Tunnel, the world's longest underwater cave). You descend through Jameo Chico to an underground saltwater lake, fed by the Atlantic, where blind albino crabs (Munidopsis polymorpha) live—1 cm long and endemic to this place alone. Don't throw coins in the water—rust kills them. The visit continues to Jameo Grande, an open-air cave with a turquoise pool and an auditorium seating 600. Book your ticket online to avoid the line.

Cueva de los Verdes lies minutes from Jameos—it's the same Corona volcanic tube, but here the roof remained intact for nearly a kilometer. The guided tour (mandatory) takes you through geological formations lit by a system designed by Manrique that enhances colors without altering the rock. The cave was used in the 17th century as a refuge by locals fleeing pirates. The visit also hides a visual surprise: prepare for a memorable optical illusion that guides never reveal in advance.

Jardín de Cactus in Guatiza is César Manrique's final major work, inaugurated in 1990—he built it in a former abandoned quarry as a vegetable amphitheater. The garden houses about 4,500 cactus specimens from 500 species originating from five continents, planted on black volcanic stone terraces. The area was historically dedicated to prickly pear cultivation for cochineal dye. At the entrance, an 8-meter cactus sculpture signed by Manrique welcomes you. Allow 45 minutes for the visit.

activities.text: Detour to the César Manrique Foundation in Tahíche, 20 minutes from Costa Teguise. The artist built his house directly on the 1730 lava flows, integrating five natural volcanic bubbles into the architecture. Since his death in 1992, it's been a museum exhibiting his works and those of international contemporaries. This is where you best understand his philosophy: art and nature must coexist without one crushing the other.

Volcanic malvasía tasting at one of La Geria's vineyards, en route to Costa Teguise. This protected designation of origin produces white wine from grape varieties planted in pits dug into volcanic lapilli, surrounded by semicircular stone walls (zocos) that protect from wind. The landscape is so singular that a photo of La Geria was exhibited in 1964 at New York's MoMA in the "Architecture Without Architects" exhibition.

Late afternoon swim at one of Costa Teguise's beaches, Lanzarote's most family-friendly northern resort. Or head up to Haría and the Valley of a Thousand Palms—a green oasis in the northern hills, accessible in 20 minutes from Costa Teguise.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

places.name: Covarrubias

visits.text: The Collegiate Church of San Cosme y San Damián, a 15th-century Gothic gem, houses the tombs of Count Fernán González and his wife in 4th-century Hispano-Roman sarcophagi. The 16th-century cloister preserves the sepulcher of Princess Kristina of Norway, who journeyed from Scandinavia in 1257 to marry a Castilian prince.

Step inside the Collegiate Treasury Museum to admire the Adoration of the Magi triptych, a polychrome wooden masterpiece attributed to Gil de Siloé. Also on display: a Virgin painting attributed to Van Eyck, a canvas by Pedro Berruguete, and the 1620 organ—the oldest still functioning in Castile.

The Torreón de Fernán González, a 10th-century Mozarabic defensive tower, rises at the end of half-timbered streets. Its stone walls are the oldest remnants of the fortified enclosure that once protected the capital of the first County of Castile.

Enter the old town through the arch of the Archives of the Adelantamiento de Castilla, a 16th-century Renaissance building adorned with Philip II's coat of arms. In front of the arch stands a Gothic stone calvary.

Wander around Plaza de Doña Urraca, bordered by the town hall housed in the former palace of Fernán González. The cobbled streets around the square reveal half-timbered houses built with juniper wood on stilts, like the Casa del Obispo Peña with its carved corbels and the house of Doña Sancha with wooden balconies.

The Church of Santo Tomás, dating from the 15th century, preserves a Renaissance pulpit and beautiful altarpieces. Less visited than the collegiate church, it's worth a look for its peaceful atmosphere and quality woodwork.

activities.text: Walk down to the banks of the Arlanza for a riverside stroll. The path follows the remains of the old walls and offers a sweeping view of the village and its tiled roofs.
The Arlanza Valley produces wines under the AO Arlanza designation. Pair them with local sheep's cheese and valley cherries, a village specialty for centuries.

Late in the day, the half-timbered facades take on golden hues in the setting sun. Plaza de Doña Sancha and the triangular streets form the perfect setting to capture the village's intact medieval atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages

map_keywords: Collégiale San Cosme y San Damián Covarrubias
Archivo del Adelantamiento de Castilla Covarrubias

places.name: Cuenca

visits.text: The Casas Colgadas cling to the cliffside above the Huécar gorge, their wooden balconies jutting out into thin air as if floating in space. Inside, the Museum of Spanish Abstract Art showcases works by Zóbel, Tàpies and Chillida in rooms that once hung over the void.

The Cathedral of Santa María y San Julián dominates Plaza Mayor with its unlikely fusion of Norman Gothic, Romanesque elements and neo-Gothic additions. Begun in the late 12th century, its main façade opens onto the arcaded square at the heart of the old town.
Cross the Puente de San Pablo, a metal footbridge suspended 60 meters above the Huécar gorge. It offers the best view of the Hanging Houses, whether by day or at night when they're dramatically lit.

Climb back up through the steep lanes of the UNESCO-listed Casco Antiguo. Colorful façades stack one above another, forming 'suspended favelas' over the abyss—the medieval fabric remains intact here, without the crowds of Toledo.
The Fundación Antonio Pérez occupies a former 17th-century convent. Spanish and international contemporary art displayed in a setting of bare stone and exposed beams.

30 km from Cuenca, the Ciudad Encantada unfolds its karst formations sculpted by 250 million years of erosion. The dolomite rocks take the shapes of animals, faces and natural arches along a marked trail of about 3 km.

activities.text: Follow the trail that runs along the Huécar gorge below the old town. The path winds between limestone cliffs and vegetation, offering striking perspectives on the hanging houses seen from below.
Stop at the Ventano del Diablo, a natural balcony carved into the rock that overlooks the Júcar gorge. The plunging view of the green waters and limestone cliffs is spectacular.
Try the morteruelo, a warm pâté made with pork liver and spices—Cuenca's signature dish. Pair it with aged Manchego cheese and a wine from the Serranía.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains

map_keywords: Museo de Arte Abstracto Español Cuenca

places.name: Daroca

visits.text: Final stop: Daroca, 1h drive southeast from Calatayud through the Jiloca valley. This town of 2,000 inhabitants possesses one of Spain's longest fortified enclosures: 4 km of ramparts bristling with 114 towers, built from the 9th century by the Arabs and enlarged after the Christian conquest by Alfonso I the Battler in 1120. Enter through the Puerta Baja (14th century), an impressive gate-tunnel that also served as a river drain—note the Fuente de los Veinte Caños (Fountain of Twenty Spouts) just outside. Climb up Calle Mayor, the town's backbone, between Mudéjar brick churches (San Miguel, San Juan, Santo Domingo) and noble palaces.

The Basilica of Santa María de los Sagrados Corporales, at the town's heart, is one of Aragon's most remarkable. It houses the relics of a Eucharistic miracle that occurred in 1239 during the Battle of Chío, when six consecrated hosts supposedly survived intact wrapped in the corporals despite the heat of battle. The main altarpiece, the Chapel of the Corporals, and the adjacent museum merit careful attention. Exit through the Puerta Alta (15th century), adorned with Charles V's coat of arms.

activities.text: Climb up to the Castillo Mayor via the path that follows the ramparts. Allow 1h30 round-trip. The wall-walk lets you link several towers and offers plunging views over the town squeezed between two ridges and the Jiloca valley. From the castle, the panorama embraces cereal fields, red clay hills and, on clear days, the sierras of the Iberian System. Bring good shoes and water—no shade on the ramparts.

From Daroca, you can continue towards Teruel (1h30 south) to join the 'Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragon' itinerary (UNESCO Mudéjar towers), or head back up to Zaragoza (1h15 north). Daroca is also a starting point for the village of Anento (15 km), member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages, with its waterfall and hanging gardens.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel
Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

map_keywords: Fuente de los Veinte Caños Daroca

places.name: Donana

visits.text: Doñana National Park, created in 1969, spans 54,000 hectares of marshlands, mobile dunes, pine forests, and Mediterranean scrub at the Guadalquivir estuary. Designated a Biosphere Reserve and UNESCO World Heritage site, it protects Western Europe's largest wetland and over 360 bird species.

Four-wheel-drive circuits cover 70 kilometers through the park's four ecosystems in a 4-hour journey: beach and mobile dunes reaching 30 meters high, flooded marshlands, wooded reserves, and Guadalquivir riverbanks. Guides spot flamingos, imperial eagles, red deer, fallow deer, and marsh horses in their natural habitat.

The village of El Rocío, with its unpaved streets and low whitewashed houses, seems suspended in another era. The Ermita del Rocío, a Marian sanctuary, draws over a million pilgrims each spring during the Romería—one of Spain's largest religious pilgrimages.

El Acebuche Visitor Center, the park's main gateway, offers marked hiking trails and bird observatories. The more remote José Antonio Valverde observatory, accessible by 4x4, features a glass wall overlooking the marshlands where flamingo colonies can be observed at water level.

Palacio del Acebrón, near the Arroyo de la Rocina, houses an exhibition on the park's ecosystems. A circular trail through gallery forest allows you to interpret animal tracks and discover plant species endemic to Doñana's unique environment.

activities.text: Rise before dawn for a birdwatching session. Doñana sits at a migratory crossroads between Europe, Africa, and Asia: over 500,000 waterbirds winter here annually. Flamingos, spoonbills, storks, glossy ibises, and gray herons create a perpetual spectacle of wings, color, and sound.

With patience and considerable luck, you might glimpse the Iberian lynx, the world's most endangered feline. Around twenty individuals live in or near the park. Wooded areas crossed late in the day during the 4x4 circuit offer the best chances.

On June 26, for over five centuries, Almonte ranchers have organized the Saca de las Yeguas: hundreds of mares and foals cross the park in a spectacular transhumance. Outside this date, horseback rides in the park's peripheral zones offer immersion in the dune and forest landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages

map_keywords: Centro de Visitantes El Acebuche Doñana

places.name: El Cotillo

visits.text: El Cotillo's lagoons (Playa de los Lagos) stretch from the village to the lighthouse. These natural swimming pools, protected by coral reefs, shimmer with immaculate white sand and crystalline waters that glow emerald in the sunlight.

The Torre del Tostón, an 18th-century circular fortress, once defended the port from pirate raids. Thick local stone walls, a drawbridge, and quarters designed for 12 soldiers tell tales of maritime vigilance.

The Faro del Tostón lighthouse complex includes three structures built in 1897, 1955, and 1986. The site houses the Museum of Traditional Fishing, chronicling the village's timeless bond with the sea.
Playa de La Concha curves like a crescent moon of white sand. Shallow emerald waters make it perfect for families. Despite its proximity to the village, the beach retains a wild, untouched character.
Explore the old fishing harbor with its colorful boats bobbing in the water. Fishermen continue their ancestral trade, delivering fresh catch daily to the village's seafood restaurants.

activities.text: Feast on grilled fish and seafood at the harbor restaurants. Fresh squid, vieja parrotfish, and traditional fish stew are the house specialties, caught just hours before reaching your plate.
The surf spots south of the village draw surfers, windsurfers, and kitesurfers seeking Atlantic power. Consistent waves and steady winds create world-class conditions.
Watch the sunset from the old harbor or lighthouse. El Cotillo faces due west, offering spectacular views as the sun sinks into the Atlantic horizon in a blaze of orange and gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages
