days_plan.itin_title: Ibiza and Formentera Guide: Beyond the Clubs to Turquoise Coves and Beaches

visits.text: The Dalí Theatre-Museum is the only architectural work that Dalí designed himself, from A to Z. He built it on the ruins of Figueres' municipal theatre, burned at the end of the civil war in 1939. Dalí wanted to make it 'the world's largest surrealist object' - and he's not wrong: giant eggs crown the walls, loaves adorn the facade, and a glass geodesic dome tops the central hall. The museum opened on October 28, 1974, and has become Spain's second most visited museum after the Prado. Dalí is buried in its crypt, beneath the stage, against his will - he wished to rest at Púbol alongside Gala.

The Sant Ferran fortress is 18th-century Europe's largest bastioned fortification: 32 hectares, 3.2 km perimeter, 9 million liters of water capacity in its underground cisterns. Built from 1751 under the reign of Fernando VI to block a potential French invasion, it nevertheless capitulated twice without fighting - to French troops in 1793 and again in 1808. Salvador Dalí did his military service here. Allow at least 2 hours, including the cistern tour if available.

Salvador Dalí's birthplace, at 6 Carrer Monturiol, is open to the public with an audio-visual tour tracing his childhood in Figueres. It's the intimate complement to the Theatre-Museum spectacle: here, you understand where the man came from before measuring the myth.

activities.text: The Rambla de Figueres, with its cafes and neoclassical and modernist buildings, is the city's commercial heart. Stop here for lunch with Empordà products: Alt Empordà anchovies are among Spain's finest. The markets on Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday at Plaça del Gra are an opportunity to buy olives, Empordà AOC oils, and local cheeses.

Wine tasting of AOC Empordà wines at one of the downtown bodegas or in the surroundings: Empordà vineyards produce powerful reds and mineral whites influenced by the tramontana. The region also produces an excellent sweet fortified wine, Garnatxa d'Empordà.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

visits.text: Fornells, a fishing village of 1,000 souls, stretches along a protected four-kilometer bay. Whitewashed houses cluster around a working harbor where tradition lives on.
Torre de Fornells, built in 1801 during British rule, stands guard at the bay's entrance. Menorca's largest watchtower commands the seaward approach.
From the tower's summit, sweeping panoramas reveal the sheltered bay, the village below, and dramatic Cap Cavalleria beyond. An exhibition chronicles the island's defensive history.
Sant Antoni Abad Church dates to the 17th century, its understated architecture perfectly suited to this humble fishing community.
Remnants of Sant Antoni Castle, constructed in the 17th century then later dismantled, dot the landscape around the village as ghostly reminders of strategic importance.

activities.text: Caldereta de langosta—a slow-simmered lobster stew made with the day's catch—is Fornells' signature dish. Harborside restaurants serve this legendary specialty nowhere else found.
The sheltered four-kilometer bay offers ideal kayaking conditions with calm, protected waters. Rental shops at the harbor equip adventurers for peaceful exploration.
The Northern Marine Reserve's seabed invites snorkeling among posidonia meadows teeming with Mediterranean biodiversity.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Frías is considered Spain's smallest 'town'—it received this title in 1435 and today has only about a hundred inhabitants, a member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages since 2014. From the bottom of the Tobalina valley, the castle stands atop the limestone outcrop called La Muela: built between the 12th and 15th centuries on Alfonso VIII's orders, it contains 13th-century Romanesque capitals depicting warriors on horseback and fantastic birds. The panoramic view over the valley from the keep is worth the climb. Buy the ticket at the tourist office before heading up.

Along the slope below the castle, Frías's medieval lanes reveal the 'hanging houses'—half-timbered constructions clinging to the cliff edge, upper floors jutting out into space above the gorge. Descend to the medieval bridge over the Ebro (143 meters, 9 arches) with its 14th-century defense tower. The San Vicente church is also notable: its original Romanesque portal was detached and shipped to New York when the tower collapsed in 1904—it's now in an American museum.

Forty-six kilometers from Frías, Puentedey takes its name from its geology: the 50-inhabitant village is built on a natural limestone bridge sculpted by the Nela River over 85 to 90 million years. It's the most photographed image in Burgos province after the cathedral. From the suspended viewpoint of San Andrés, the overall view of the natural bridge and the houses perched on it is striking. On the arch itself, the San Pelayo church (11th-century Romanesque elements) and the Brizuela Palace (15th century, two rectangular towers with coats of arms) merit a stop.

activities.text: Walk the cobbled lanes climbing toward the castle. The medieval structure of the town can be read in the tangle of passages and stairs.
Go down to the bridge to photograph Frías from the opposite bank. The silhouette of the village and its castle stands out against the sky.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

places.name: Fromista

visits.text: The Church of San Martín de Frómista, founded in 1066 by Doña Mayor of Castile, is one of the purest Romanesque buildings in Europe. Three naves, three apses, two cylindrical towers on the gable, and an octagonal dome. The 315 sculpted corbels beneath the cornices depict fantastic animals, human faces, and vegetal motifs of exceptional finesse.

The Church of San Pedro, with its Renaissance portal and Gothic interior, houses the Museum of Sacred Art of Frómista. Its centerpiece: 29 Hispano-Flemish panels from the 15th century, recovered after a theft, which belonged to the main altarpiece of Santa María del Castillo.
Santa María del Castillo, built on the site of a former castle, blends late Gothic with elements from the 15th to 18th centuries. It has been classified as a Site of Cultural Interest since 1944.

The set of four locks on the Canal de Castilla (locks 17 to 20) forms the largest lock system of the canal's three branches, overcoming a drop of more than 14 meters. The aqueduct over the Ucieza River, with three round arches, completes this 18th-century hydraulic engineering work.

The former lock keeper's house, transformed into an interpretation center, traces the history of the Canal de Castilla, an Enlightenment project launched in 1753 under Ferdinand VI to connect the Castilian plateau to the northern ports. More than 350 boats plied its waters in the 1850s.

activities.text: The towpath along the canal offers a flat, shaded route perfect for cycling. Pedal between Frómista and Boadilla del Camino following the path once traveled by mules pulling barges.
The wetlands formed around the canal harbor bitterns, warblers, and other aquatic birds. Dawn and dusk are the best times to observe them from the banks.
A river cruise of about an hour departs from the Frómista lock keeper's house toward Boadilla del Camino. Onboard commentary presents the local wildlife and the operation of the locks.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María del Castillo Frómista

places.name: Fuente

visits.text: The Fuente Dé cable car climbs 753 vertical meters in just 4 minutes. From the lower station at 1,094 m, the glass cabin rises to 1,823 m into the heart of the central massif of the Picos de Europa.
The Mirador del Cable at the top offers a sweeping panorama over the glacial cirque of Fuente Dé, the Liébana valley and, on clear days, the Cantabrian Sea to the north.
Before or after the cable car, detour to Mogrovejo. This stone-built village boasts a medieval tower and spectacular views of the Picos.
The Monastery of Santo Toribio de Liébana, 3 km from Potes, shelters what tradition holds to be the largest surviving fragment of the True Cross. It is one of Christianity's holy sites.
Potes, capital of the Liébana valley, spreads its arcaded houses and bridges along the Río Quiviesa. The 15th-century Torre del Infantado dominates the small town.

activities.text: Take the PR-PNPE 24 trail, the Ruta de los Puertos de Áliva. From the upper station, a descending path crosses high pastures and karst landscapes down to Espinama.
Picnic facing the peaks. The high mountain pastures, dotted with shepherds' huts, provide an ideal setting between June and September.
In Potes, try cocido lebaniego, a chickpea stew with cabbage and compango sausage typical of the valley. Orujo de Liébana, the local eau-de-vie, accompanies dessert.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa

map_keywords: Puertos de Áliva Picos de Europa
Monasterio de Santo Toribio de Liébana

visits.text: Balenciaga Museum — haute couture and clean lines.
Port of Getaria — wood-fired grilling.
San Salvador Chapel and narrow streets.

activities.text: Txakoli tasting at an estate.
Sea outing with local fishing.
Coastal trail to Zarautz.

days_plan.itin_title: Bay of Biscay Coastal Drive: Fishing Villages & Basque Culinary Soul in France

visits.text: Climb into the Cimadevilla district, an old fishing quarter perched on a promontory between sea and harbor. Its cobbled alleys lined with colorful houses and cider bars lead to the Revillagigedo Palace, an imposing 17th-century baroque building now serving as a cultural center.

At the summit of Cerro de Santa Catalina, the Elogio del Horizonte overlooks the Cantabrian Sea. This monumental 10-meter-high sculpture—two pillars bearing a concrete arch—was inaugurated in 1990 and has become Gijón's icon.

Descend to the Campo Valdés Roman Baths, late 1st-century public baths discovered beneath the esplanade of San Pedro church. The underground museum displays hypocaust heating systems and remnants of the Roman wall that once protected the Cimadevilla peninsula.
San Lorenzo beach unfurls over a kilometer of golden sand along the bay. Its seafront promenade, bordered by 19th-century villas, connects the historic quarter to residential neighborhoods.

On Plaza de Jovellanos, the birthplace of the Enlightenment writer and politician houses the municipal museum: memorabilia, paintings, and sculptures in a handsome 16th-century building at the heart of Cimadevilla.

activities.text: Grab a bike on the cycling path that runs along the San Lorenzo promenade to the marina. The flat route offers constant views of the bay and Cimadevilla's cliffs.
Join the Paseo de San Pedro, a coastal path built along the cliffs south of Cimadevilla. In about twenty minutes, you'll be looking down at Sablón beach and the rocks where Cantabrian waves crash.
Sit down in a harbor chigre for an escanciado of cider accompanied by grilled sardines, cachopo (breaded cutlet stuffed with ham and cheese), or chorizo a la sidra.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

places.name: Girona

visits.text: Girona Cathedral dominates the city from its monumental staircase of 91 steps. Begun in the 11th century and completed in the 18th, it boasts the world's widest Gothic nave: 23 meters, surpassed only by St. Peter's Basilica in the Vatican. Inside, don't miss the Tapestry of Creation, an 11th-century Romanesque embroidery depicting the medieval calendar - a unique textile masterpiece in Europe. Game of Thrones fans will immediately recognize this staircase: Jaime Lannister rode up it in season 6.

From the ramparts of the Passeig de la Muralla - a 3 km free walk along 9th-century Carolingian walls - you'll have plunging views over the old town's rooftops and, on clear days, the Pyrenees ridges on the horizon. It's Girona's best vantage point, and entry is free.

The Jewish Quarter of El Call is one of the best preserved in Europe. The Jewish community lived here for over 600 years before being expelled in 1492. The stepped alleyways, stone arches, and tightly packed houses have changed function but retained their exact medieval morphology. The Museum of Jewish History, installed in one of the Call houses, presents Hebrew inscriptions and ritual objects recovered during excavations.

The colorful houses along the Onyar River are Girona's most photographed image. These ochre, pink, and yellow facades reflecting in the river have been painted in the tones mandated by local tradition. For the best angle, position yourself on the Pont de Ferro - yes, the one designed by Eiffel's workshops, the same Gustave Eiffel, delivered in 1877 before his Parisian tower was built.

activities.text: In the morning, visit the Arab Baths (built in 1194 following the model of Roman baths, despite their name) and the 12th-century Benedictine monastery of Sant Pere de Galligants - two stops that are unremarkable from the outside but whose interiors are truly worth seeing. The Baths were transformed into a Jewish mikveh in the 13th and 14th centuries.

For Game of Thrones fans, free guided tours of the season 6 filming locations are organized from the tourist office. More than 11 series sets were filmed in Girona between the cathedral (Great Sept of Baelor), the Jewish quarter, and the Arab Baths (House of Black and White). Even if you've never watched the series, the tour is a good excuse to explore the old town thematically.

In the evening, Plaça de la Independència and its terraces are Girona residents' headquarters for aperitifs. Order a glass of Catalan cava and xuixos - fried cream pastries, an absolutely local specialty you won't find anywhere else. Don't leave without eating at least one.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

map_keywords: City Walls Passeig de la Muralla Girona

visits.text: The Alhambra commands Granada from the Sabika hill. Start with the Alcazaba, the primitive citadel: from atop the Torre de la Vela, the panorama sweeps across the city, the fertile Vega plain, and the snow-capped peaks of the Sierra Nevada. Here in 1492, the Catholic Monarchs raised the cross and flag of Castile.

The Nasrid Palaces reach the summit of Hispano-Moorish art. In the Patio de los Arrayanes, a rectangular pool bordered by myrtles reflects the 45-meter-high Comares Tower. The Hall of Ambassadors, crowned by a cedar ceiling representing Islam's seven heavens, served as Yusuf I's throne room.

Cross into the Patio de los Leones, the Alhambra's most celebrated courtyard: 124 slender columns of white Macael marble encircle a fountain borne by 12 lions. Four channels symbolize the four rivers of Paradise. Mohammed V built this ensemble in the 14th century as his private residence.

The Generalife, the Nasrid sultans' summer residence, sprawls across the Cerro del Sol. Its Arabic name Jannat al-Arif means 'garden of the architect.' The Patio de la Acequia aligns its arching water jets above a long canal fed by Sierra Nevada snowmelt. Water is everywhere: channels, rills in the stair ramps, low fountains.

Follow the Carrera del Darro, considered Granada's most beautiful street, running along the Darro River at the foot of the Alhambra. The ancient Arab baths of El Bañuelo, dating from the 11th century, are among the best-preserved in Spain with their star-pierced vaults.

Climb to the Mirador de San Nicolás in the Albaicín for sunset. The Alhambra rises against the backdrop of the Sierra Nevada in golden light. The Albaicín itself—a UNESCO-listed Moorish quarter—is a labyrinth of steep lanes, small plazas, and carmenes, those garden-houses hidden behind high white walls.

activities.text: Push open the door of a tetería on Calle Calderería Nueva, nicknamed 'tea street.' This steep Albaicín lane lines up craft shops and Moroccan tea rooms where you can savor mint tea and honey pastries.
Granada keeps alive the tradition of free tapas: every drink comes with a plate that grows with each round. The Navas quarter and the streets around Plaza Nueva concentrate the best bars.
Attend a zambra performance in a Sacromonte cave. This gypsy dance, heir to primitive flamenco, is performed in troglodyte dwellings carved into the hillside since the 15th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Andalusia Road Trip: Seville, Córdoba, Granada & Ronda

visits.text: The Real Monasterio de Guadalupe (14th century) weaves together Gothic, Mudéjar, Renaissance, and Baroque. This UNESCO site was patronized by the Catholic Monarchs and visited by Christopher Columbus in 1486 and 1492.
The Claustro Mudéjar (1389-1405) ranks among Spain's finest. Red and white bricks, horseshoe arches, and a terracotta templete with fountain at the center.
The Gothic-Mudéjar basilica houses the Camarín de la Virgen. This dazzling Baroque chapel (stucco, marble, bronze, frescoes) displays the Virgin of Guadalupe carved in cedar (12th century).
The Baroque Sacristía (1638-1647) preserves eight canvases by Zurbarán depicting the life of Hieronymite monks. Known as the 'Sistine Chapel of Extremadura.'
The Museo de Bellas Artes displays three paintings by El Greco, a late Goya, works by Pedro de Mena, and a 16th-century ivory crucifix attributed to Michelangelo.
Wander through the village's whitewashed lanes. Half-timbered houses with flower-filled balconies climb the hillside around the monastery.
The Arco de Sevilla and Arco del Chorro are two medieval Mudéjar gateways. They once marked the entrance to the fortified enclosure.

activities.text: Hike up to the mirador del Humilladero (2 km from town). This Gothic-Mudéjar chapel offers panoramic views of the monastery nestled in the valley and the Sierras de las Villuercas beyond.
Trek through the Sierra de las Villuercas. Marked trails wind through holm oaks, cork oaks, and quartzite outcrops.
Savor migas extremeñas (breadcrumbs fried with garlic and pimentón) and morcilla de Guadalupe. Shepherd's specialties served in the village mesones.
Visit a hand-hammered copper workshop. Guadalupe preserves this Moorish craft tradition: pots, platters, and decorative pieces shaped by hand.

days_plan.itin_title: Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

places.name: Guadix

visits.text: Guadix is one of the most astonishing towns in Andalusia. Founded as Acci by the Iberians, colonized by the Romans (Julius Caesar allegedly founded the colony Julia Gemella Acci here in 45 BC), then governed by the Moors under the name Wadi-Ash, it preserves traces of all these civilizations. But what will strike you is the Barrio de las Cuevas: around 2,000 cave houses still inhabited, spread across 200 hectares - Europe's largest troglodyte settlement. The first caves were dug around 1452 by Moors seeking refuge in the clay hills. Today, 45% of the population lives there, with wifi, heating and fitted kitchens - and a constant temperature of 18-20°C year-round.

Start at the Mirador del Cerro de la Magdalena to take in the full panorama: the white chimneys emerging from the ochre hills, the contrast between the troglodyte quarter and the old town, and the Sierra Nevada as backdrop. Then descend into the cave quarter to walk among whitewashed facades and rainbow-colored doors. Visit the Cueva Museo (Cave Museum), facing the San Miguel church - it recreates life in a cave house with period furniture, and a good explanatory film. If a resident invites you to see their cave (it happens often if you show interest), accept - it's the best way to understand this way of life.

In the historic center, the Cathedral of the Incarnation is a surprising monument - built from the 16th to 18th centuries on a former mosque, itself built on an even older temple, it blends Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque and Neoclassical styles. Its red sandstone facade is particularly beautiful in late afternoon light. Right next door, the ruins of the Roman theater, discovered in 2008, date from Emperor Tiberius' reign. The Alcazaba, an 11th-century Arab fortress on the hill, offers the best views of the town and Sierra Nevada - but it's only partially open.

Don't miss the church of Santiago, declared a national monument, with its facade richly sculpted with figures, masks and demons - a striking contrast between the white walls and the reddish-brown ornaments. And if cinema interests you, know that Guadix served as location for "For a Few Dollars More" (1965), "Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade" and "Doctor Zhivago". The lunar landscapes around the town are an open-air studio.

activities.text: Sleep in a cave house - several are converted into guesthouses, and the experience is worthwhile. The silence is total, the temperature ideal, and you'll wake up understanding why these people don't want "normal" houses. If you're in Guadix in September, don't miss the Cascamorras, a wild festival where a character covered in black grease tries to cross the crowd to retrieve a statue - it's the ancestral rivalry between Guadix and Baza.

Late in the day, make a detour to Purullena, the neighboring village, known for its handmade pottery shops along the roadside. Further on, La Calahorra castle deserves a stop if you have time: this 15th-century fortress, with massive round towers, conceals an Italian Renaissance courtyard in Italian marble realized by Florentine craftsmen - an Italian palace hidden within a Castilian fortress.

visits.text: The Barrio de la Estación lines up the facades of century-old bodegas dating back to 1863, when the railway reached Haro. López de Heredia Viña Tondonia (1877), CVNE (1879), La Rioja Alta (1890), and Bodegas Bilbaínas (1901) form the golden kilometer of Rioja wine.
Step inside Bodegas López de Heredia. Oak vats stand beside cobblestones and stone vaults. This traditional bodega ages its wines in barrel and bottle longer than any other in the region.
Visit the wine bar at Bodegas Roda in the station quarter. You can taste their cuvées in a sleek modern space without booking a full tour.
Plaza de la Paz gathers the terraces and buzz of the historic center. Renaissance arcades frame the cobbled square.
Explore the La Herradura neighborhood, a pedestrian labyrinth of lanes and small plazas. Taverns serve pintxos and glasses of Rioja in a relaxed, local atmosphere.

activities.text: Arrange a vertical Tempranillo tasting at a bodega. You compare a Crianza, Reserva, and Gran Reserva from the same estate to understand how time transforms the wine.
Enjoy a wine-pairing lunch at a winery restaurant. The menu unfolds in five courses of Riojan specialties matched with as many vintages.
Walk along the Ebro River banks. The river curves in a wide meander below the vineyards and the Sierra de Cantabria.
Go out for a pintxo-pote in La Herradura. Flit from bar to bar, tasting each house specialty with a glass of Rioja.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries

places.name: Hervas

visits.text: Devote the morning to the Valle del Jerte, one of Extremadura's most spectacular landscapes. The name comes from Arabic "xerete"—crystalline river—and that's exactly what it is: a deep V-shaped valley framed by the Sierra de Tormantos and the Traslasierra mountains, where dozens of gargantas (gorges) run with mountain water. The valley holds 1.5 million cherry trees whose simultaneous flowering, usually late March to early April, transforms the slopes into a vast white mantle—this is the Cerezo en Flor festival, declared a National Tourist Interest celebration. In summer, the gargantas become natural swimming spots of rare beauty.

The valley's jewel is the Reserva Natural de la Garganta de los Infiernos, 7,000 protected hectares since 1994—Extremadura's second most-visited natural area. The circular trail (about 7 km, 2 hours) starts from the Interpretation Centre and descends to Los Pilones—giant's kettles carved into granite by water over centuries, forming natural pools of crystalline water where you can swim in summer. The path threads through oak, chestnut, and cherry forests, with waterfalls at every turn. Motor access is forbidden—tour operators offer trips in authorized 4x4s to the trailhead.

Heading down the valley, stop at Cabezuela del Valle, whose historic center is classified as a Conjunto Histórico-Artístico. Its narrow streets with half-timbered chestnut houses, overhanging balconies, and heraldic doorways make it one of the valley's prettiest villages. The church's detached tower and the Plaza de la Independencia deserve a photo stop.

In the afternoon, head to Hervás in the neighboring Valle del Ambroz. This village has one of Spain's best-preserved juderías—classified as Conjunto Histórico-Artístico since 1969. The Jewish community settled here after the 1391 pogroms, welcomed by the Duke of Béjar. At its peak in the 15th century, nearly 50 Jewish families lived here. Walk down Calle Abajo, the main street of the barrio judío: the adobe and timber-framed chestnut houses, streets so narrow you could touch both walls with outstretched arms (Travesía del Moral is 55 cm wide—one of Spain's narrowest), Stars of David carved on facades, and street names like "Amistad Judeo-Cristiana" (Jewish-Christian Friendship) all testify to a past that remains very present.

Finish at the Puente de la Fuente Chiquita, a sturdy medieval granite bridge spanning the Río Ambroz at the foot of the judería. It's one of Hervás's most photographed images, especially in autumn when the chestnuts turn golden. At number 19 Calle Rabilero, a building wider than the others occupies the site of the old synagogue. In July, the Fiesta de los Conversos reenacts medieval life and the 1492 expulsion in an open-air performance by the river.

activities.text: Hervás and Valle del Ambroz cuisine centers on the chestnut—which provides timber for houses, autumn fruit, and accompaniment to many dishes. In autumn (late October through December), the Otoñada celebrates chestnuts with markets, tastings, and walks through centuries-old chestnut groves. The rest of the year, try dulces sefardíes (Sephardic-inspired pastries) that some Hervás restaurants offer on themed menus.

places.name: Huesca

visits.text: The church and cloister of San Pedro el Viejo, a national monument since 1885, form one of Aragon's most important Romanesque ensembles. The 1149 cloister preserves 18 original capitals sculpted by the Master of San Juan de la Peña, depicting the life of Christ. The Chapel of San Bartolomé houses the royal pantheon of kings Alfonso I the Battler and Ramiro II the Monk.

Huesca's Gothic cathedral, erected on the former mosque, houses the monumental alabaster altarpiece by Damián Forment, a masterpiece of the Aragonese Renaissance. Its high vaults and stained glass filter soft light onto the side chapels.

Facing the cathedral, the Town Hall occupies a 16th-century Renaissance palace. This is where the bloody legend of the "Bell of Huesca" allegedly occurred: King Ramiro II supposedly beheaded rebellious nobles and arranged their heads in the shape of a bell to impose his authority.

The Provincial Museum of Huesca, housed in the 16th-century Casa de la Comunidad, displays a Roman archaeological section, a reconstructed old pharmacy, and, on the top floor, very fine ceramics from the Al-Andalus period. The terrace offers a view over the city's rooftops.

Wander through Huesca's Casco Antiguo, among arcaded squares and medieval lanes. The remains of the Roman theater of Semonia beneath the modern city remind us that Huesca was an important city of Hispania under the name Osca.

activities.text: Parque Miguel Servet, Huesca's green lung, lines its shaded walkways with century-old plane trees, fountains, and sculptures. It's the favorite walking spot for Oscenses, especially pleasant in late afternoon.

Sample the specialties of Upper Aragon: sheep cheeses from the Pyrenean valleys, longaniza de Graus sausage, chiretas (stuffed tripe with rice and meat), and ternasco, the roasted milk lamb that is the region's gastronomic emblem.

Around August 10th, the Fiestas de San Lorenzo, declared of national tourist interest, transform Huesca into a popular celebration where white and green invade the streets. Off-season, the terraces of Plaza de la Catedral let you enjoy the evening bustle.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragon Circuit: Zaragoza & Mudéjar Villages

map_keywords: Monasterio San Pedro el Viejo Huesca

visits.text: Dalt Vila—the fortified citadel—has towered over Ibiza Town since the 16th century. This UNESCO site unfolds Renaissance ramparts, bastions, and cobbled lanes offering sweeping harbor views.
Portal de ses Taules opens onto Dalt Vila. This monumental gateway flanked by Roman statues is the upper town's main entrance.
Santa María Cathedral was erected in the 14th century atop the former mosque. Its Gothic character gained Baroque additions in the 18th century.
The Archaeological Museum traces 3,000 years of history. Housed in the former university, it exhibits Phoenician, Punic, and Roman remains.
Ibiza's coves glow an unreal turquoise. Cala Comte, Cala d'Hort facing Es Vedrà, and Cala Salada rank among the most spectacular.
Eivissa harbor pulses day and night. Yachts, fishing boats, and Formentera ferries line the marina.
Ses Salines Natural Park extends between Ibiza and Formentera. This UNESCO site harbors flamingos, traditional salt pans, and pristine beaches.

activities.text: Ferries reach Formentera in 30 minutes. The neighboring island unveils white-sand beaches and crystalline waters worthy of the Caribbean.
Cala Comte delivers Ibiza's finest sunsets. The sun plunges behind rocky islets while beach bars spin chill music.
Dalt Vila's lanes come alive at night. Art galleries, bohemian boutiques, and terraces drift in a laid-back atmosphere.
The hippy markets of Las Dalias and Punta Arabí showcase crafts, jewelry, and bohemian clothing. Live music and a relaxed vibe reign beneath the pines.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves
