places.name: Jaca

visits.text: Jaca was the first capital of the Kingdom of Aragón in the 11th century and a major stopping point on the Camino Aragonés to Santiago de Compostela. Begin with the Cathedral of San Pedro, the first Romanesque cathedral built in Spain - construction began in 1077, almost simultaneously with Santiago de Compostela. The tympanum of the western portal features a Chi-Rho flanked by two lions, one protecting a repentant sinner, the other crushing a serpent. Inside, the pendentive dome above the transept crossing is the oldest in Spain. Note the checkerboard pattern (ajedrezado jaqués), created here and later adopted throughout Romanesque churches along the Way of Saint James.

Don't miss the Diocesan Museum of Jaca, housed in the cathedral's cloister - it's an underrated gem. The museum preserves Romanesque frescoes recovered from small churches throughout the Aragonese Pyrenees - some were detached from walls to save them from abandonment and looting. The Romanesque painting gallery, with its figures sporting enormous eyes and intense blue backgrounds, is worth the visit alone. Take the guided tour (included in the ticket price), which lasts about an hour and reveals details invisible to the naked eye.

The Citadel of Jaca is the only entirely preserved pentagonal fortress in Spain - one of only two in Europe, along with Liège. Designed by Italian engineer Tiburzio Spannocchi in 1592 under orders from Philip II to defend the border with France, it features a perfect star-shaped plan with five bastions connected by walkways, a deep moat (now home to deer), and a drawbridge. Walk around the ramparts to appreciate the perfect geometric precision of the design. Inside, the chapel and powder magazine are accessible, and the Museum of Military Miniatures recreates battles with 32,000 lead soldiers.

activities.text: If you have time at the end of the day, drive 20 minutes southwest to reach the Monastery of San Juan de la Peña, one of Aragón's most spectacular monuments. This 11th-century monastery is semi-troglodytic, wedged beneath an enormous rocky overhang that serves as its natural roof. It was the pantheon of the early kings of Aragón and one of the kingdom's cultural centers. The Romanesque cloister, carved directly beneath the rock, features historiated capitals of extraordinary fineness.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

map_keywords: Monasterio San Juan de la Peña

visits.text: Playa de Sotavento stretches for kilometers, famous for its lagoon that forms at low tide. Shallow turquoise waters, ever-shifting sandbanks, and spectacular vistas make this beach a photographer's dream come true.

The Faro del Matorral lighthouse, standing 60 meters tall, visually divides Playa del Matorral into two distinct zones. It's a prime observation point for watching sunsets paint the Atlantic in brilliant hues.
The Saladar de Jandía, a protected wetland, harbors the Canaries' largest salt flats. This area of scientific interest preserves a rare salt-resistant plant species found almost nowhere else.

Journey to the Punta Jandía lighthouse at the island's southwestern tip, built in the 19th century to guide ships toward Africa. The dramatic route cuts through Jandía Natural Park, revealing desert landscapes and wave-battered cliffs.
Stroll through Morro Jable village, once a humble fishing port. The marina now welcomes up to 300 yachts, while narrow streets shelter boutiques and restaurants serving the day's fresh catch.

activities.text: Relax on Playa del Matorral's 4 kilometers of golden sand, equipped with beach bars, restrooms, sun loungers, and lifeguards. Perfect for families seeking comfort and safety without sacrificing natural beauty.
Kitesurf and windsurf at Playa de la Barca. Constant Atlantic winds make Sotavento one of the world's premier spots, hosting international competitions that draw elite riders.
Join a boat excursion to observe dolphins and whales. Success rates run 50-70% depending on conditions. Curious and playful dolphins often approach boats, delighting passengers with their acrobatics.
Hike to Pico de la Zarza (807m), Fuerteventura's highest peak. The summit reveals panoramic views over the wild Cofete beach and the entire Jandía Peninsula stretching toward the horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

places.name: Jerez De La Frontera

visits.text: The Alcázar of Jerez, a 12th-century Almohad fortress, contains nearly every element of a Moorish palace: vaulted halls, Arab baths with starlight filtering through ceiling perforations, a tiny mosque converted into a chapel, and an ancient olive oil mill. From the tower summit, views sweep across the cathedral and Jerez's rooftops.

San Salvador Cathedral rises on a platform where a mosque once stood. It mingles Gothic, Renaissance, and neoclassical styles across centuries of construction. The bell tower, likely a former minaret, forms a beautifully illuminated ensemble with the Alcázar at night. Inside, a Zurbarán painting depicts the Virgin Mary as a contemplative child.

Step inside a sherry bodega to understand the solera and criaderas system: American oak barrels stacked in tiers, with wine slowly flowing from youngest to oldest, creating a perpetual blend that maintains consistency across decades. The Atlantic air from 12 kilometers away gives fino its characteristic saline tang.

The Royal Andalusian School of Equestrian Art, founded in 1973, occupies a 19th-century palace. Its 1,600-seat arena hosts "Cómo bailan los caballos andaluces"—a ballet of Andalusian horses in 18th-century costumes, accompanied by traditional Spanish music in a breathtaking display of precision and grace.

Wander through the Santiago quarter, birthplace of Jerez flamenco, where colorful buildings and flower-filled patios line picturesque alleys. The Gothic church of San Miguel rises at the end of a plaza where flamenco peñas keep the tradition of bulería alive with passionate performances.

activities.text: Join a guided tasting of different sherry types: bone-dry, saline fino; amber amontillado with nutty aromas; dark, powerful oloroso; and rare, complex palo cortado. Jerez, El Puerto de Santa María, and Sanlúcar form the "Sherry Triangle," the only region where authentic sherry can be produced.

Attend the equestrian show on performance days. Riders execute classical dressage and doma vaquera—the traditional horsemanship of Andalusian cattle herders—on pure Spanish horses selected for their grace, power, and connection with their riders.

In the evening, settle into a tabanco in the old town. These traditional sherry bars serve wine straight from the cask, paired with tapas: crispy chicharrones, shrimp fritters, and cheese from the Sierra de Cádiz mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages

map_keywords: Alcázar de Jerez de la Frontera
Real Escuela Andaluza Arte Ecuestre Jerez

places.name: Jerez De Los Caballeros

visits.text: Jerez de los Caballeros is the final stop on this itinerary—and what a finale. Birthplace of Vasco Núñez de Balboa, the first European to sight the Pacific Ocean in 1513, it has a spectacular historic center dominated by the silhouettes of its baroque towers. The Templar fortress, built in the 13th century when the Knights Templar controlled this frontier with Muslim Andalusia, overlooks the town from a hilltop. It's in the Torre Sangrienta (Bloody Tower) that Jerez's last Templars were supposedly massacred in 1312 during the dissolution of the order. The legend is probably exaggerated, but it fits the mood of the place.

Jerez's two baroque churches are among Extremadura's finest. The Iglesia de San Bartolomé has a bell tower covered in colorful azulejos that recalls Seville's bell towers—it's the town's iconic image. The Iglesia de La Encarnación, with its own slender baroque tower, completes an urban landscape you'd swear was Andalusian. Climb the sloping streets to discover the noble palaces, carved coats of arms, and flower-filled patios that built the reputation of this conquistador town.

On Plaza de Vasco Núñez de Balboa, a statue honors the town's most famous son. Born here around 1475, Balboa sailed for the New World in 1500 and crossed the Isthmus of Panama in 1513 to become the first European to see the Pacific Ocean. The plaza also offers fine views over the surrounding hills covered in holm oaks—the dehesa landscape that characterizes all of Lower Extremadura.

activities.text: Jerez de los Caballeros sits at the heart of the jamón ibérico de bellota production zone—the world's finest ham. Black Iberian pigs roam free in the dehesas, feeding on holm oak acorns during the montanera (autumn). If you're passing October through February, you'll see the pigs under the oaks—a unique landscape. Regional tour operators offer visits to ham-curing facilities with tastings. Even out of season, every bar in Jerez serves hand-sliced jamón ibérico—it's the best way to close this Extremadura journey.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

map_keywords: Fortaleza Templaria Jerez de los Caballeros
Iglesia San Bartolomé Jerez de los Caballeros
Iglesia La Encarnación Jerez de los Caballeros
Torre Sangrienta Jerez de los Caballeros

places.name: La Alberca

visits.text: La Alberca became the very first village in all of Spain to be declared a Historic-Artistic Site—in 1940, by decree of September 6, published in the BOE on the 18th of the same month. This pioneering title is well deserved: its granite houses with ground floors topped by upper stories in adobe and timber forming warm half-timbering are the perfect model of serrana architecture. The village name comes from the Arabic 'Al-Bereka,' meaning pond or water place—the numerous fountains dotting the lanes are its legacy. The Plaza Mayor, with its irregular layout, granite arcades, and flower-decked balconies, displays an 18th-century cross carved with symbols of the Passion.

The Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción, built in 1730 by Manuel de Larra Churriguera (architect of Salamanca Cathedral), is a baroque jewel in this medieval setting. Its grand tower was erected in 1518 by the first Dukes of Alba—their coat of arms still visible on one corner. Inside, ask to see the Cristo del Sudor, attributed to Juan de Juni, and the 16th-century polychrome wooden altarpiece.

Walk down to the Puente del Arroyo, the old granite bridge spanning the stream below the village—a classic view of serrana architecture. Along the way, you'll pass the Hornacina de las Ánimas, a wall niche where each evening a local woman comes out with her bell (esquila) to recite a prayer for the souls in purgatory. This nighttime tradition is still alive.

The Casa Museo Sátur Juanela, adjacent to the square, preserves the interior of a typical serrana house as it was in the early 20th century—farmyard, wine cellars on the ground floor, kitchen under the eaves. It's the most concrete way to understand how mountain villagers lived.

activities.text: Each year in the village, a pig is released into the streets on June 13 (Saint Anthony's Day) and fed by residents until January 17, when it's raffled off. Proceeds now go to an NGO. If you visit outside this period, you'll still find the statue of the marrano de San Antón at the church corner. This tradition reaches back to the Middle Ages and is one of the most photographed in Spain.

The route into Parque Natural Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia starts from La Alberca. The Batuecas Path descends into a steep, secret valley where Neolithic cave paintings and a Carmelite monastery survive. Count on a half-day for the round trip to the valley floor.

On the square, sample the obleas and artisan turrones sold in local shops—La Alberca's sweet specialties found throughout the region. Local markets also offer a good opportunity to buy honey, cheese, and sierra cured meats.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción La Alberca
Casa Museo Satur Juanela La Alberca

visits.text: Lake Enol appears first around the bend of the road, at 1,070 meters altitude. This glacial lake reaches 25 meters deep. Beneath its waters lies a replica of the Virgin of Covadonga, raised to the surface each September 8th by divers.

Two kilometers higher, Lake Ercina rests at 1,108 meters. Smaller than Enol, it's surrounded by pastures where Asturian cattle and horses graze freely. In the background, the summit of Peña Santa de Enol rises to 2,486 meters.

The Pedro Pidal Visitor Center, near the Buferrera parking lot, traces the glacial geology of the lakes and the history of the national park. Nearby, the old Buferrera manganese mines bear witness to the area's mining past.

Stop at the Mirador de la Reina on the upward road: on clear days, the panorama sweeps from the Cangas de Onís valley all the way to the Cantabrian Sea. The Mirador Entrelagos, on the hill between the two lakes, offers a simultaneous view of both Enol and Ercina.

activities.text: Walk the circuit of the lakes (PR-PNPE-2), a 5 km circular hike starting from the Buferrera parking lot. The marked trail passes through viewpoints, high-altitude meadows, and beech forests of the western Picos massif.
Keep your eyes and ears open: chamois, griffon vultures, and golden eagles frequent the limestone ridges above the lakes. The tinkling of bells from free-roaming herds accompanies the walk.
Set up for a picnic facing Lake Enol with views of the snow-capped peaks. The Vega de Enol, a vast high-altitude meadow dotted with shepherds' barns, is the perfect spot.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

places.name: Laguardia

visits.text: Pass through the medieval gates of Laguardia, capital of Rioja Alavesa, perched high on its hilltop. Thirteenth-century ramparts encircle three parallel streets lined with Renaissance palaces and Baroque mansions.
The polychrome portal of Iglesia Santa María de los Reyes dazzles with its preserved 17th-century colors. Gothic sculptures tell the life of the Virgin in tinted stone.
Casa de la Primicia, built in the 14th century, is Laguardia's oldest civilian structure. It once housed the collection of church tithes.
On Plaza Mayor, the town hall clock sets three mechanical figures dancing on the hour. This whimsical ballet has delighted visitors for decades.
Climb the stairs to the El Collado viewpoint. The panorama sweeps across rolling vineyards all the way to the Sierra de Cantabria.
Descend into the underground wine cellars that honeycomb Laguardia's bedrock. Over 300 family calados snake beneath the town, maintaining a constant 15–17°C year-round.

activities.text: Book a tasting at a Rioja Alavesa bodega. Winemakers explain the blends and aging of Tempranillo grown on limestone-clay soils.
Walk among the vineyard rows at sunset. Golden light sets the vines ablaze and the Sierra de Cantabria glows crimson in the distance.
Join a blending workshop at a winery. You create your own Rioja by mixing different tanks under the winemaker's guidance.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María de los Reyes Laguardia

visits.text: The Laguna Negra lies at 1,731 meters altitude in a glacial cirque carved by ice millions of years ago. Its dark, deep waters inspired Antonio Machado's poem 'La Tierra de Alvargonzález' with their mysterious beauty.
Vertical granite walls enclose the lake on three sides. Centuries-old Scots pines descend to the shores where trout and catfish populate depths reaching a maximum of 8 meters.
Pico Urbión rises to 2,228 meters, the highest point of the Iberian System. At its feet, the Douro River is born, destined to flow 897 kilometers to Porto in Portugal.
The natural park of Laguna Negra and Glacial Cirques of Urbión protects 4,617 hectares of glacial landscapes. The Laguna Helada and Laguna Larga complete this high-altitude lake system.
Scots pine forests almost entirely cover the valleys of Revinuesa and the Douro, forming the most extensive continuous pine forest of the Iberian Peninsula—a green ocean at high altitude.

activities.text: Hike from the Paso de la Serrá parking lot to the Laguna Negra on a 2-kilometer trail through pines. A shuttle bus service operates during high season for those who prefer.
Continue to the Laguna Helada then Pico Urbión. This complete hike crosses alpine meadows and rock formations with significant elevation gain and spectacular summit views.
Join the traditional swimming crossing held every first Sunday of August. Hundreds of swimmers brave the glacial waters of the lake in this local tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

visits.text: The Lagunas de Ruidera natural park strings together 15 lagoons over 20 km along the upper course of the Guadiana, separated by natural travertine barriers. The total elevation drop of 128 meters creates a succession of waterfalls between each basin.
Start at the Cascada del Hundimiento, the park's highest waterfall, just steps from the village of Ruidera. It's the starting point of the left-bank trail, the wildest and best-preserved route.
The Laguna del Rey is the park's deepest lagoon. From the Mirador de la Laguna del Rey, the view sweeps across its turquoise waters bordered by reeds. A developed beach allows swimming in summer.

The Laguna Colgada ('hanging' lagoon) extends into a tongue of land that advances into the water. At its tip, small waterfalls feed the Laguna de Batana below—a bucolic landscape typical of tufa formations.
The Laguna Lengua offers the park's most spectacular colors: deep blue and emerald green. The limestone rock barriers and waterfalls carving their way through create an almost surreal setting.

The Cueva de Montesinos, a cave mentioned by Cervantes in Don Quixote, opens onto the plateau of Campo de Montiel. This is where the errant knight claimed to have spent three days in an enchanted palace.

activities.text: Paddle a kayak on the Laguna del Rey or Laguna Colgada. The calm, crystal-clear waters allow you to glide along the travertine cliffs and access beaches and caves unreachable on foot.
Hike the left-bank trail between the Cascada del Hundimiento and Laguna Redondilla. The path crosses shaded zones and offers plunging views over the successive lagoons.
At dawn, the lagoons attract grey herons, coots, ducks and raptors. The marsh reeds and quiet shores of the park make excellent terrain for birdwatching.
Set up a picnic in the designated areas by the lagoons. The turquoise waters and birdsong compose an ideal setting for a nature lunch break.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca

places.name: Las Medulas

visits.text: Las Médulas is Castile and León's strangest landscape: reddish sand pinnacles rise from a forest of oaks and chestnuts, like a fantasy film set. This isn't natural geology—it's the direct result of the Roman Empire's largest open-pit gold mine. The Romans extracted approximately 800,000 kilos of gold here between the 1st and 3rd centuries, using a process called 'ruina montium': they stored colossal amounts of water in mountain basins, then released it all at once to explode the rock. Over 400 km of canals and aqueducts were built to feed this system. Listed as UNESCO in 1997, the site has remained intact since the mines were abandoned. Start at the Interpretation Center in the village to understand the operation's scale before heading out on the trails.

Climb up to the Mirador de Orellán for the overview of the site—from here the spectacle is most striking: the jagged red formations stand out against the green forests, with the Aquilanos mountains in the background. The sunset from this viewpoint is one of the most photographed in León province. Allow 30 minutes uphill from the Orellán parking lot.

activities.text: Walk the Senda de las Valiñas, a circular route from Las Médulas village that penetrates the heart of the mining site. The trail crosses the open-air galleries, skirts Lake Carucedo (formed by stagnant water accumulation during Roman works) and passes through the caves of La Cuevona and La Encantada—galleries hand-dug for the hydraulic system. Easy, marked, about 2 hours. If you have more time, the Peripheral Trail (4h) makes the complete circuit and includes the ruins of the Orellán settlement.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

map_keywords: Cueva de La Cuevona Las Médulas

places.name: Las Negras

visits.text: Cala San Pedro must be earned: an hour's walk from Las Negras along a coastal path overlooking the Mediterranean. The ruins of a 15th-century castle guard the entrance to this isolated cove, a former pirate hideout. A freshwater spring—the only one in the park—flows at the beach's edge.

Las Negras takes its name from Cerro Negro, the dark volcanic mass that dominates the village. About 350 people live here year-round, including many foreigners drawn by the tranquility. Fishing boats still beach on the black pebble shore each evening.

Take the road to Rodalquilar, a former mining village reinvented. The ruins of the gold mines, worked until 1966, can be freely visited. The El Albardinal Botanical Garden showcases the park's semi-arid flora.

The Playazo de Rodalquilar offers a striking contrast with the enclosed coves: 400 meters of golden sand bordered by the Playazo tower, an 18th-century fortification. The water here is calm and shallow.
From the trail above Cerro Negro, viewpoints offer spectacular vistas of black cliffs plunging into the sea and, in the distance, the silhouette of Isleta del Moro.

activities.text: Hike Las Negras to Cala San Pedro via the coastal trail. The route crosses semi-arid landscape punctuated by red and yellow cliffs before diving toward the turquoise cove.
Kayak along the volcanic cliffs from Las Negras. Rental shops offer guided outings that follow caves and rock formations inaccessible by land.
Snorkeling in the coves around Las Negras. The volcanic rocky seabeds create varied habitats for Mediterranean underwater fauna.
Evening drinks facing the sea on the Las Negras promenade. The village's bohemian vibe carries through to the small waterfront bars.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert

map_keywords: Castillo de San Pedro Cabo de Gata

places.name: Las Palmas

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santa Ana has dominated Vegueta since the 16th century. Four centuries of construction blend late Gothic, Renaissance, and Neoclassical styles. Its three naves of equal height—called "hall church" style—rise on slender fluted columns whose ribs burst into palm-shaped vaults.

Step through the door of the Casa de Colón, just behind the cathedral. Built on the exact site where the city was founded in 1478, its green stone façade opens onto colonial courtyards. The museum traces the connections between the Canaries and the Americas.

The Museo Canario, founded in 1879, is the archipelago's most important archaeological museum. Its pre-Hispanic collections evoke the culture of the Guanches—the islands' first inhabitants—through ceramics, mummies, and idols.

Stroll down Calle Triana, the long pedestrian street that concentrates every architectural style the city has known: Modernist façades from 1900–1910, Neoclassical buildings, Neo-Baroque and Art Nouveau in succession.

Playa de Las Canteras stretches three kilometers through the heart of the city. A natural rocky barrier protects swimmers and creates a turquoise lagoon sheltering a marine reserve. Blue Flag beach since 1989.
The Teatro Pérez Galdós, Italian-inspired, has been the city's cultural emblem for over a century. Flanked by a contemporary extension, it programs opera, concerts, and dance year-round.

activities.text: Grab your mask and snorkel at Las Canteras: waters protected by the rocky barrier shelter a wide variety of fish in a protected natural area, accessible right from the sand.

The Mercado de Vegueta, in operation since 1854, comes alive each morning with stalls of spectacular tropical fruits, fresh fish, and Canarian cheeses. On Sundays, a craft market sets up in Plaza del Pilar Nuevo.
Stay on the Las Canteras promenade for sunset. The Auditorio Alfredo Kraus closes the perspective at the end of the beach, its silhouette cut against the Atlantic horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria

map_keywords: Catedral de Santa Ana Las Palmas
Playa de Las Canteras Las Palmas

visits.text: Discovery of Ses Salines beach surrounded by dunes and salt flats

activities.text: Walk along the beach and observation of Ibiza's salt flats

days_plan.itin_title: Ibiza and Formentera Guide: Beyond the Clubs to Turquoise Coves and Beaches

visits.text: Las Tuerces unfolds a labyrinth of Upper Cretaceous limestone blocks sculpted by karst erosion. Giant stone mushrooms, natural arches, and narrow corridors compose this lunar landscape.
The limestone plateau rises above the village of Villaescusa de las Torres. Erosion has created streets, passages, and caves in the rock across several square kilometers of geological wonderland.
The canyon of La Horadada carves into the plateau with 40-meter vertical walls. The Pisuerga River digs its gorges between limestone and riverside vegetation in dramatic fashion.
The site is classified as a natural monument and integrated into the UNESCO Global Geopark of Las Loras. Holm oaks, junipers, and cliff plants colonize the rocky interstices.

activities.text: Explore the labyrinth by following the Ruta Verde, a 9.5-kilometer loop from Aguilar de Campoo. The trail winds between rock formations and crosses the páramo highland.
Descend into the canyon of La Horadada via one of the paths departing from Olleros de Pisuerga. You'll walk beside the river between limestone walls populated by golden eagles and peregrine falcons.
Climb Monte Cildá to see the remains of an Iberian oppidum later occupied by Romans. The panorama embraces Las Tuerces, the canyon, and the Palentine mountains stretching to the horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

places.name: Ledesma

visits.text: Ledesma is built on a rocky spur between two meanders of the Tormes—a naturally defensive position inhabited since prehistoric times. The Romans called it Bletisama in honor of Consul Bleto (93 AD). Medieval repopulation dates to 1161, when Fernando II raised the walls and fortress. In 1462, Enrique IV handed lordship of Ledesma to Don Beltrán de la Cueva, his favorite, who made it a city of splendor in the 15th century. Classified as a historic site in 1975, Ledesma preserves fragments of Roman and medieval wall.

Ledesma's castle, partially demolished, has kept what matters: its entrance gate with a Gothic arch of keystones founded directly in bedrock, flanked by a semicircular cube and a polygonal one. It's the most eloquent entrance to the walled enclosure. Inside, the lanes lead to the Gothic church of Santa María la Mayor and the Casa de las Almenas—a 15th-century palace with cloister and interior garden, one of the province's finest noble residences.

The Mocho Bridge, of Roman origin, spans the Tormes at the foot of the cliff on which the town sits. The forest of centuries-old oaks bordering the road to the bridge is a remarkable habitat where you can spot griffon vultures and black kites in flight. A plaque recalls that Mesta flocks crossed this passage for centuries, paying portazgo (toll) to the town's lord.

activities.text: The Mocho Bridge route, marked from the town, crosses a forest of oaks centuries old to reach the Tormes banks. Birdwatchers will easily spot black storks, kites, and vultures from the heights of the riverbank. Allow 1½ hours round trip.

Ledesma is renowned for its thermal mud baths—the aguas termales have been known since Roman times. Wellness establishments still exploit the springs. If you have fatigue from the miles in your legs, it's a welcome break.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Église Santa María la Mayor Ledesma

visits.text: León Cathedral—affectionately called the 'Pulchra Leonina'—was built starting in 1205 atop the ruins of 2nd-century Roman baths, which King Ordoño II had earlier transformed into a royal palace in the 10th century. What sets it apart from every other cathedral in Spain is its stained glass: 737 individual panels covering 1,765 square meters, created between the 13th and 20th centuries. This is the largest collection of medieval stained glass in Europe after Chartres. Visit late morning—that's when sunlight streams through the main rose window, flooding the naves in red, blue, and gold. Its twin Gothic towers soar to 65 and 68 meters.

Just steps from the cathedral, the Basilica of San Isidoro ranks as the most important Romanesque complex in Spain. King Fernando I and Queen Sancha had the relics of Saint Isidore of Seville transferred here in 1063—the remains of this Visigothic Church Father have rested here ever since. The Royal Pantheon, burial place of eleven kings of León and several queens, is adorned with 12th-century frescoes so perfectly preserved they've earned it the nickname 'the Sistine Chapel of Romanesque art.' Legend has it that Jimena, wife of El Cid Campeador, came here to attend Mass. The museum also houses the Nodicia de Kesos, considered the earliest written text in Spanish.

Walk along the 3rd-century Roman walls on Avenida de los Cubos—their semicircular towers, known as 'cube towers,' are the best preserved in Spain and have been protected since 1931. The wall originally encircled the Roman camp in a perfect quadrilateral. Then make your way to Casa Botines, one of the few buildings Gaudí designed outside Catalonia, commissioned in 1891 by textile merchants from León and now a museum.

The Valporquero Caves, 45 km north of León, rank among the most spectacular in the Iberian Peninsula. This 3,300-meter network of underground galleries carved into Carboniferous limestone harbors giant stalactites, translucent draperies, and underground lakes with turquoise reflections. The grand chamber—the Sala de las Maravillas—soars over 20 meters high. Guided tours last about 75 minutes. Unlike the Collegiate Church of Arbas, the caves stay open year-round except January and February.

Back in León, don't miss the San Marcos Convent: its 16th-century Renaissance façade stretches 100 meters and is covered in scallop shells sculpted in bas-relief—one of the most sumptuous in Spain. This was the headquarters of the Order of Santiago, charged with protecting pilgrims on the Camino. The main façade is freely accessible; the interior now houses a Parador, but you can enter the cloister and adjacent church.

Right next door, the Palacio de los Guzmanes is León's most elegant Renaissance palace, commissioned in the 16th century by the Guzmán family from architect Rodrigo Gil de Hontañón. Its two-story interior courtyard gallery is remarkably understated. The provincial government offers free guided tours—inquire at the entrance.

Use the morning to visit what you missed on your first visit: the Palacio de los Guzmanes, a 16th-century Renaissance building by Rodrigo Gil de Hontañón, now the provincial seat and open to the public during guided tours. Its interior courtyard is one of the city's finest. Or the cathedral one last time, this time lit differently depending on the hour—the light shifts throughout the day through the 737 stained-glass windows.

activities.text: The Barrio Húmedo is León's beating heart. This labyrinth of medieval lanes around Plaza de San Martín earned its name—the 'Wet Quarter'—from the countless wine cellars and taverns that have packed it for centuries. The rule is simple: every drink comes with a free tapa. Midday belongs to vermouth, evenings to lively bars. Must-tries: morcilla de León (blood sausage), cecina (cured beef), caldo gallego (Galician soup), and botillo del Bierzo. The neighboring Plaza Mayor, rebuilt after a 1654 fire, has hosted a market every Wednesday and Saturday since 1660.

Wander the lanes of the Barrio Romántico, north of Calle Ancha. Less touristy than the Barrio Húmedo but equally authentic, it's packed with excellent restaurants and some of the city's finest wine bars. This is the best place to dine with locals.

Plaza Mayor market (Wednesdays and Saturdays since 1660) to pick up León products: vacuum-packed cecina, mantecadas, mountain cheeses, and a few bottles of Bierzo mencía or Canary malvasía if you've extended the trip. One last tapa tour in the Barrio Húmedo before hitting the road.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

visits.text: Lerma appears in the distance like an ocean liner perched on a hill. The Palacio Ducal dominates the Plaza Ducal—one of Spain's largest squares at 6,862 m². This 17th-century palace, built between 1601 and 1617 by architect Francisco de Mora, is the work of the Duke of Lerma, favorite of Philip III and the most powerful man in Spain at the time. The duke, so influential he dared add four towers to his palace (a privilege normally reserved for kings), made Lerma a parallel court with six monasteries, a collegiate church, and secret passages connecting everything. Its austere façade has 210 balconies and 35 windows. You can visit the cloister and covered inner courtyard.

Take the Pasadizo del Duque, the covered passage the duke built to attend Mass without stepping into the street. It connected the palace to the Collegiate Church of San Pedro (early 17th century), an austere temple whose interior preserves a gilded bronze praying statue of Archbishop Cristóbal de Rojas, the duke's uncle. From the Mirador de los Arcos at the end of the passage, the view plunges over the vega of the Arlanza—the agricultural plain stretching as far as the eye can see.

Cross through the Arco de la Cárcel, the only preserved medieval gate from the old walls. It now houses the headquarters of the DO Arlanza regulatory council. Around the square, visit the convents: San Blas (where Dominican nuns sell hand-painted ceramics), La Ascensión (where the infanta Margarita, daughter of Philip III, was baptized), Santa Teresa (now city hall and interpretation center). Lerma had six monasteries in the 17th century—a village of 600 inhabitants surrounded by convents, that's Habsburg Castile.

activities.text: Use the afternoon for an excursion to Santo Domingo de Silos, 35 km southeast of Lerma. The Benedictine monastery, founded in the 10th century, houses an 11th-century Romanesque cloister considered one of Europe's finest. The carved capitals and corner pillar bas-reliefs are exceptionally refined. The monks still chant Gregorian chant at services.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

visits.text: Visit the Archaeological Museum of Linares, housed in the former 17th-century Casa del Torreón Palace. Discover a fascinating collection of artifacts from the Cástulo excavations, highlighting the famous Ibero-Roman sandstone lion originally from the northern wall of the ancient city.

Explore the Hospital of the Marquises of Linares, an impressive neo-Gothic building constructed between 1904 and 1917 commissioned by José de Murga and Raimunda de Osorio. Admire its Victorian architecture with gargoyles on the entrance columns and visit the Marquises' crypt with life-size sculptures.

Discover El Pósito, home to the Raphael Museum, one of the main cultural attractions in the singer's hometown. Admire his countless awards, records, and film costumes, including a very rare 1980 uranium disc that only artists like Michael Jackson or Queen have achieved.

Contemplate the Church of Santa María la Mayor, the oldest building in Linares constructed over a mosque in the 13th century. In Gothic-Renaissance style with participation from architect Andrés de Vandelvira, it was declared a basilica by Pope Francis in 2016.

activities.text: Stroll along the Paseo de Linarejos, a pleasant boulevard among green areas where locals enjoy tapas and gather. It begins at the white marble Constitution Fountain and culminates with the statue of guitarist Andrés Segovia, one of the city's most illustrious personalities.

Visit the Andrés Segovia House Museum in the 17th-century Orozco Palace. View the guitars that belonged to the maestro, his recognition medals, and the crypt where his remains rest. In November, it hosts the International Guitar Encounter.

days_plan.itin_title: Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: The historic center of Llanes preserves about 300 meters of 13th-century wall. The Torreón, a five-story circular crenelated tower, served as a watchtower and defense against seaborne invasions. It adjoins the Casino, an elegant 'Indiano' building from 1910.

The Basilica of Santa María del Conceyu, begun in the 13th century, is a fine example of the Romanesque-Gothic transition. Its claim to fame: it was funded by a tax paid by all inhabitants, making it a people's church rather than a clergy church.

Walk down to the fishing harbor to behold the Cubos de la Memoria. More than 200 concrete blocks on the breakwater were painted by Basque artist Ibarrola between 2001 and 2006: abstract figures, ancestral symbols, and vivid colors that shift with the light and tides.
Toró beach, 500 meters from the center, is distinguished by its erosion-sculpted rocks that rise from the sand like sentinels. At low tide, natural pools form between the boulders.
Head to Barro beach, accessible by a short road from Llanes. More sheltered than Toró, it offers a long stretch of fine sand framed by green meadows sloping down to the water.

activities.text: Walk the Paseo de San Pedro, a coastal path built along the cliffs east of the harbor. This trail overlooks the sea and offers panoramas of Sablón beach, the rocks of Toró, and the Sierra del Cuera in the background.
The municipality of Llanes boasts more than thirty beaches. Sample a smoked Gamoneu or a cave-aged Cabrales accompanied by cider in a harbor bar—mountain cheeses are the local pride.
Stay until sunset from Punta de Toró or the Llanes lighthouse (1860) at the end of the San Antón promenade. Ibarrola's colored cubes catch fire in the low-angle light.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

places.name: Logrono

visits.text: Calle Laurel stretches 300 meters of pintxo bars where each establishment perfects a single recipe. Over 80 spots line this legendary street—locals move from bar to bar, glass of Rioja in hand, tasting the house specialty at each stop.

The Concatedral Santa María de la Redonda raises its twin Baroque towers above Plaza del Mercado. Inside, 16th-century Gothic vaults fan out like stone palm trees, sheltering a Crucifixion attributed to Michelangelo.
Puente de Piedra has spanned the Ebro River since 1884. This stone bridge marks the entrance for pilgrims on the Camino de Santiago entering Logroño and appears on the city's coat of arms.
Head to Paseo del Espolón, the leafy avenue lined with plane trees. Café terraces animate this promenade where the San Mateo festival takes over every September.
The 12th-century church of San Bartolomé preserves a carved Romanesque portal. It's Logroño's oldest building, a witness to the city's medieval past.

activities.text: Order a zurito (small beer) or txikito (small wine) with your pintxo at three different bars to capture the local spirit—it's all about variety and movement.
Taste a Rioja DOCa at a wine shop in the city center. Specialists offer vertical tastings of Tempranillo with expert commentary on each vintage.
Rent a bike and ride the Vía Verde along the Ebro. The cycling path cuts through vineyards and follows the river's wide bends.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries

map_keywords: Concatedral Santa María de la Redonda Logroño

places.name: Lorca

visits.text: The Castillo de Lorca—Fortaleza del Sol—stretches 640 meters, one of Spain's largest. Climb the Alfonsina and Espolón towers, walk Almohad ramparts, and see the 15th-century synagogue discovered intact in 2003.

Plaza de España gathers baroque architecture in one glorious square. The Palacio de Guevara flaunts carved coats of arms, the Casa de los Mula displays noble lineage—Lorca earned the nickname "City of a Hundred Shields."
The medieval Jewish Quarter lies beneath the Parador. Tour the semi-excavated synagogue with its Torah niche and women's gallery—the only one in Spain never converted into a church.
The Colegiata de San Patricio lifts baroque columns and gilded altarpieces toward heaven. Construction spanned the 16th to 18th centuries.

The Brotherhoods' museums display embroidered banners, floats, and costumes from the rival White and Blue confraternities. Lorca's Holy Week is recognized as an event of international tourist interest.

activities.text: Join a nighttime theatrical tour of the ramparts. Historical reenactments, floodlit towers, views across the Guadalentín Valley.
Sample Murcian goat cheeses, savory meat pies, and wine from Bullas.
Embroidery workshops preserve Holy Week techniques. Watch demonstrations of gold-thread and silk work passed down through generations.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida

map_keywords: Castillo de Lorca Fortaleza del Sol

places.name: Los Llanos De Aridane

visits.text: Los Llanos de Aridane is La Palma's most populous town, set in the green Aridane valley on the western slope—a microclimate warmer and sunnier than the capital's. The historic center houses the Museum of the Street, a permanent outdoor contemporary art exhibition unique in Europe: since 1999, murals painted directly on the city's blank facades by recognized artists cover up to 140 m² of walls. Wander the alleys to find them.

The Benahoarita Archaeological Museum traces the life of La Palma's prehistoric inhabitants, the Benahoaritas—a people of Berber origin who arrived on the island before the Spanish conquest. Reconstructions of their habitat, beliefs, and crafts help understand how they lived in this volcanic environment before being expelled in the 15th century.

Tajogaite volcano—also called Cumbre Vieja—erupted on September 19, 2021 and flowed for 85 days until December. The flow covered 1,218 hectares, destroyed 1,345 houses and 73 km of roads, and expanded the island by 47 hectares into the sea. From Los Llanos, Mirador de Tajuya offers the best view of the entire lava field and the new volcanic cone. Guided 5 km trails allow you to approach the crater's base from Llano del Jable.

Fuencaliente, at La Palma's southern tip, is the territory of the island's most recent volcanoes. San Antonio volcano (430 m) erupted in 1677 and its crater is freely accessible with an interpretation center tracing Canarian volcanic history. Just beside it, Teneguía spewed lava as recently as October 1971—less than 55 years ago. You can walk on its still-warm flanks and observe vegetation slowly recolonizing the lava. Don't miss Fuencaliente lighthouse at the cape's end: from the point, you have a panorama of both island coasts simultaneously.

Fuencaliente's vineyards merit a stop. As in Lanzarote, vines grow here in volcanic soils on terraces—but the landscapes are even more dramatic because Teneguía's lava fields descend right to the vineyards. The La Palma appellation produces volcanic malvasía and red wines from listán negro whose reputation far exceeds the island.

If weather permits, climb to Roque de los Muchachos (2,426 m), the island's highest point. The road winds through Canarian pine forests and alpine zones to the summit, where domes of the astrophysical observatory stand—one of the world's most advanced observation centers, benefiting from the island's low light pollution. On clear days, you can spot Tenerife's Teide from the summit.

activities.text: Hike the Route of the Volcanoes (Cumbre Vieja) from the El Pilar refuge. This GR-131 trail follows the volcanic ridge where all the island's historic eruptions occurred—1949 (Hoyo Negro), 1971 (Teneguía), and 2021 (Tajogaite). The panorama from the ridges embraces the entire Aridane valley on one side and the Atlantic on the other. Start early: wind can be fierce and heat intense on this shadeless terrain.

Relaxation at Puerto de Tazacorte, a colorful fishing port 10 minutes from Los Llanos. Waterfront restaurants serve the day's catch—grouper, vieja (parrotfish), and local prawns. The sunset from the terrace facing the Atlantic is one of La Palma's finest. Boat trips to observe dolphins and whales depart from this port.

Tasting at Casa Museo del Vino Las Manchas, which traces La Palma's winemaking history and offers local wine tastings. The island produces malvasía, listán negro, and several Canarian grape varieties that exist nowhere else.

Swimming in the natural pools of La Fajana or Charco Verde, on La Palma's west coast. These seawater basins carved into black volcanic rock are the island's most photogenic swimming spots—the contrast between lava black, water blue, and foam white is striking. Check weather before departing, as Atlantic swells can close certain pools.

Market or end-of-trip stroll in Los Llanos de Aridane. The town is also the finish line of the Transvulcania, one of the world's best mountain races. The lively pedestrian center and cafe terraces offer a final immersion in local life before heading to the airport.

Closing lunch with La Palma specialties: potaje de berros (watercress soup), pork in mojo sauce, and bienmesabe for dessert. The island is renowned for exceptional agricultural products—avocados, bananas, and palmero PDO cheese that you can bring home vacuum-sealed.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature
Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

map_keywords: Los Llanos de Aridane centre ville
Musée de La Calle Los Llanos de Aridane
Musée Archéologique Benahoarita Los Llanos de Aridane
Piscine naturelle La Fajana La Palma
Plage El Charco Verde La Palma
Roque de los Muchachos observatoire La Palma

visits.text: Begin your Madrid stay along the Paseo del Arte, Europe's most concentrated museum triangle. The Prado Museum, founded in 1819 in a neoclassical building by Juan de Villanueva, houses over 8,000 paintings—only a fraction of which are on display. Velázquez's Las Meninas (1656), Hieronymus Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights (circa 1500), Rubens's The Three Graces, and Goya's Black Paintings await you in the main galleries. Allow at least 2 hours for the major works, longer if you're a painting enthusiast. Arrive at opening time to avoid the queues—they lengthen quickly after 11am.

A ten-minute walk south along the Paseo del Prado brings you to the Reina Sofía Museum, housed in the former 18th-century San Carlos Hospital. The museum opened in 1992 and covers Spanish art from 1881 onwards. The absolute star is Picasso's Guernica (1937)—7.76 metres wide, painted in response to the bombing of the Basque village by the German Condor Legion during the Civil War. The room is always crowded, but the canvas is so immense you can see it well from anywhere. Don't miss Dalí's The Great Masturbator and Miró's canvases on the upper floors.

For a breather between museums, cross the street to Parque del Retiro—125 hectares of greenery right in central Madrid. The park was created in the 17th century as the garden of the Buen Retiro Palace, residence of Philip IV. Rent a rowing boat on the Estanque Grande, facing the Alfonso XII monument, then head to the Crystal Palace (1887), a glass-and-iron greenhouse inspired by London's Crystal Palace, which hosts contemporary art exhibitions from the Reina Sofía. Entry is free.

Devote the morning to the Royal Palace, official residence of the Spanish monarchs—though the royal family no longer lives there. Built between 1738 and 1764 on the site of the former Habsburg Alcázar destroyed by fire, it's the largest palace in Western Europe by floor area: 135,000 m² and 3,418 rooms. You'll visit the state rooms—the Throne Room with its Tiepolo frescoes, the Gala Dining Room with its 140-seat table, and the Royal Armoury, one of the world's most complete collections with armour belonging to Charles V and Philip II. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours.

Leaving the palace, cross Plaza de la Armería to the Almudena Cathedral. Begun in 1883, it wasn't completed and consecrated until 1993 by Pope John Paul II—over a century of construction. The interior is surprisingly modern, with brightly painted ceilings that contrast sharply with the sobriety of the neoclassical façade. Climb to the dome for views over the palace and Casa de Campo.

Walk down to Plaza Mayor via Calle Mayor. This arcaded rectangular square, completed in 1619 under Philip III, was the scene of Inquisition autos-da-fé, bullfights, and public executions. Today, the terraces beneath the porticos are tourist traps—just admire the architecture and the equestrian statue of Philip III at the centre, then head to Mercado de San Miguel, just next door. This covered market from 1916, with its iron-and-glass structure, has become a gourmet temple: oysters, jamón ibérico, churros, vermouth, and creative tapas.

Head up to Gran Vía, Madrid's main commercial artery, cut through between 1910 and 1929. The Edificio Metrópolis (1911), at the corner of Calle de Alcalá, with its gilded dome and winged Victory, is the city's postcard image. Continue west to the Templo de Debod, an authentic Egyptian temple from the 2nd century BC, gifted by Egypt to Spain in 1968 in thanks for its help saving the Nubian temples threatened by the Aswan Dam. At sunset, the temple's reflections in the pool with Casa de Campo in the background offer one of Madrid's most beautiful views.

activities.text: In the late afternoon, head to Plaza de Santa Ana for a tapas crawl through the Las Letras neighbourhood—the literary quarter where Cervantes, Lope de Vega, and Quevedo lived in the 17th century. The tapas bars around the square and along Calle de las Huertas serve generous portions of tortilla, croquetas, and jamón ibérico. Wash it down with a glass of Ribera del Duero or a vermut de grifo (vermouth on tap), Madrid's traditional aperitif drink.

If you're in Madrid on a Sunday morning, don't miss El Rastro, Spain's largest flea market. It spreads out every Sunday from 9am to 3pm around Calle de la Ribera de Curtidores, in the La Latina neighbourhood. Antiques, vintage clothing, books, vinyl, curiosities—it's a joyful bazaar that attracts Madrileños as much as visitors. After the market, the tapas bars of La Latina are mobbed—it's Madrid's Sunday ritual.

In the evening, catch a flamenco show. Madrid isn't Andalusia, but the city has a historic flamenco scene—many Andalusian artists have settled here since the 19th century. Tablaos in the La Latina and Lavapiés neighbourhoods offer intimate performances with singing, guitar, and dance. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours for a show.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums

places.name: Mahon

visits.text: Mahón's harbor, the world's second-largest natural port, carves six kilometers inland from its narrow entrance to the city waterfront—a stunning fjord-like passage.
The Museum of Menorca, housed in a former Franciscan monastery, traces the island's remarkable journey from prehistoric talayotic culture through British occupation.
Santa Maria Church showcases a spectacular 18th-century organ with over 3,000 pipes. The austere architecture contrasts beautifully with the ornate baroque altarpiece.
Xoriguer Distillery has been crafting Mahón gin since 1736 using the same recipe. Ancient copper stills, heated over wood fires for 250 years, still produce this legendary spirit.
Mercat del Claustre del Carme occupies a beautifully preserved 17th-century convent cloister. Hunt for authentic Mahón cheese, fresh seafood, and local specialties among the atmospheric stalls.
La Mola Fortress, a 19th-century military complex, towers 90 meters above the harbor mouth. The ramparts offer breathtaking panoramic views across the Mediterranean.

activities.text: Hour-long boat tours navigate around King's Island, Quarantine Island, and Lazaretto Island, revealing hidden history and stunning coastal perspectives.
Free tastings at Xoriguer Distillery let you sample artisanal gin and local liqueurs. Don't miss the Pommada cocktail—gin mixed with fresh lemonade, utterly refreshing.
Harborside terraces serve exceptional caldereta de langosta (lobster stew), grilled octopus, and fresh seafood paired with crisp local wines.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

map_keywords: Mercat del Claustre del Carme Mahón

visits.text: Málaga's Alcazaba, built between 1057 and 1063 by Berber king Badis of Granada, is nicknamed 'the little Alhambra.' Its three concentric enclosures blend Roman columns and capitals recycled from the neighboring theater with Nasrid patios adorned with pools and gardens. From the ramparts, the view embraces the port and the Mediterranean.

At the Alcazaba's foot, the Roman Theater dates from the 1st century under Emperor Augustus. Discovered by chance in 1951 after centuries of burial, it unfolds its cavea of 31 meters diameter and 16 meters height. The adjacent Interpretation Center traces the history of ancient Malaca.

Climb to the Castillo de Gibralfaro, connected to the Alcazaba by the fortified path of La Coracha. This 14th-century fortress, built by Nasrid sultan Yusuf I to protect the Alcazaba, offers Málaga's finest panorama: the city, port, bullring, and Mediterranean in 360 degrees.

The Picasso Museum occupies the Palacio de Buenavista, a 16th-century Renaissance building in the heart of the old town. Born at 36 Plaza de la Merced in 1881, Picasso lived his first ten years in Málaga. The museum presents over 200 works covering all his periods.

Málaga Cathedral, begun in 1528 on the former mosque, blends late Gothic, Renaissance, and baroque styles. Its south tower was never completed, earning it the affectionate nickname 'La Manquita' (the one-armed lady). The rooftop climb offers a bird's-eye view of the city.

Stroll down Calle Marqués de Larios, a pedestrian artery lined with Belle Époque buildings from the late 19th century. It opens onto Plaza de la Constitución, Málaga's beating heart since the 15th century. Stop at Café Central, an institution offering ten ways to prepare coffee.

activities.text: Head to La Malagueta beach in the morning for a swim facing the port. The chiringuitos lining the sand serve espeto de sardinas—sardines grilled vertically over olive-wood fires, a quintessential Málaga tradition.

Wander along Muelle Uno, a promenade developed on the port docks. Between boutiques, restaurants, and the Centre Pompidou Málaga—the first outpost outside France, inaugurated in 2015—this modern waterfront contrasts with the historic city.
As evening falls, head to a rooftop in the center for a final drink with views of the illuminated Alcazaba, the port, and the Mediterranean at twilight.

days_plan.itin_title: Andalusia Road Trip: Seville, Córdoba, Granada & Ronda

places.name: Manzanares El Real

visits.text: Manzanares el Real Castle, built in 1475 for the Mendoza family, rises in granite at the foot of La Pedriza. Architect Juan Guas crafted this masterpiece of Isabelline Gothic with Mudéjar flourishes. Seven levels, a hexagonal tower, Gothic gallery, and a collection of 16th-century tapestries await inside.

The Old Castle (Castillo Viejo) crowns a hillside above the village. This 14th-century rectangular fortress in granite masonry and Mudéjar brickwork still stands proud. Four towers and its feudal layout reveal the Mendoza family's earliest fortifications.

La Pedriza sprawls across 2,600 hectares of granite chaos sculpted by erosion. Balanced boulders, domed rocks like El Yelmo and Las Torres, narrow corridors carved by wind and time. Classified as a Biosphere Reserve, it's the crown jewel of Sierra de Guadarrama National Park.
Follow the shores of Santillana Reservoir, a turquoise mirror reflecting granite peaks. The waterfront path winds through pine groves with sweeping views of the castle and La Pedriza rising beyond.

The 15th-century church of Nuestra Señora de las Nieves preserves its traditional architecture. Nearby, the Puente de la Cañada Real spans the old Segovian transhumance trail where shepherds once drove their flocks.

activities.text: Hike the Trail of Shapes (Senda de las formas) where wind-carved rocks resemble animals: the seal, the turtle. Views open onto Cancho de los Muertos and the dome of El Yelmo rising against the sky.
Climb to Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Peña Sacra. The ascent rewards you with spectacular views across La Pedriza's mountains and the valley sprawling below.
Rock climbing made La Pedriza famous. These historic cliffs birthed the Madrid climbing school. Routes for all levels on solid granite where generations have tested their mettle.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

map_keywords: La Pedriza Parque Nacional Sierra de Guadarrama
Iglesia Nuestra Señora de las Nieves Manzanares
Ermita Nuestra Señora de Peña Sacra Manzanares

visits.text: The Dunes of Maspalomas form a 403-hectare nature reserve protected since 1994. Organic sand extends 8.5 kilometers between the lighthouse and Playa del Inglés, with crests reaching 10 meters high. Wind reshapes this Saharan landscape perched on the Atlantic's edge every single day.

The Maspalomas Lighthouse rises 56 meters at Gran Canaria's southern tip. Commissioned in 1861 and completed in 1890, it once guided steamers on routes connecting Europe, Africa, and the Americas. An interpretation center traces the Canaries' maritime history.

The Charca de Maspalomas, a brackish lagoon 200 meters from the lighthouse, hosts up to 24 species of migratory and resident birds: grey herons, little egrets, coots, sandpipers. Boardwalks and observation hides line the protected zone.

The Palmeral de Maspalomas, the last remnant of the ancient palm groves in the island's south, shelters Canary palms (Phoenix canariensis). The Tony Gallardo ecological park offers an interpretive circuit through this oasis.
Ready for the beach? Playa de Maspalomas extends the dunes toward the sea for kilometers of golden sand. Further on, Playa del Inglés offers three more kilometers facing the hotels.

activities.text: Set out early to cross the dunes on foot before the heat arrives. Trail #3 from the information center viewpoint traverses different dune types and reaches the beach.
Follow the Barranco de Fataga inland: volcanic ravine landscapes, white villages clinging to slopes, and almond trees. The contrast with the coast is striking.

Stroll the Paseo Costa Canaria in Meloneras at sunset. The promenade follows the sea from the lighthouse, lined with terraces where you can sample local seafood and wines from the Caldera de Tirajana.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria

visits.text: Discovery of Doñana National Park (Almonte sector)
Walk on Matalascañas beach

activities.text: Trail in the Matalascañas dune park

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva Province, Spain: Mining Heritage and Untouched Atlantic Beaches

places.name: Medina Del Campo

visits.text: Drive east to Medina del Campo in the heart of the Castilian plain. The Castillo de la Mota greets you from afar—it's one of Castile's largest castles. Built in the 15th century by John II and completed by the Catholic Monarchs in 1483, it presents a trapezoidal plan with a 40-meter-high keep flanked by four crenellated towers. The castle served successively as royal archives, state prison—César Borgia was imprisoned here in 1506—and residence of Joanna the Mad. You can visit the parade ground, chapel, dungeons, and Queen's mirador—allow 1h30 with the guided tour.

Descend to the Plaza Mayor de la Hispanidad, one of Spain's largest. This is where the international fairs that made Medina's fortune in the 15th and 16th centuries were held—merchants from Portugal, Italy, and Flanders negotiated wool, textiles, and bills of exchange. On one corner of the square stands the Palacio Real Testamentario: it was in this palace that Isabella the Catholic dictated her will in October 1504 and died on November 26 following. The interpretation center recounts her life and her connection to the city.

Visit the Museo de las Ferias, housed in the former church of San Martín (16th century). This museum traces the history of the medieval fairs that made Medina the commercial capital of Castile. The pieces on display—bills of exchange, weights and measures, coins—tell how this small town became one of 15th-century Europe's most important financial centers.

The Collegiate Church of San Antolín, a Gothic-Renaissance building from the 16th-17th centuries, merits a stop for its richly decorated chapels. Then stroll the streets to spot Renaissance palaces built by bankers and merchants from the fair era: the Palacio de Dueñas, Casa Blanca, and Hospital de Simón Ruiz.

activities.text: Medina del Campo hides beneath its streets a network of medieval underground cellars, once used to store wine. Operators organize guided tours of three cellars with different characteristics—allow 1h30 with a tasting. It's an original way to discover the town's underground heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Castillo de la Mota Medina del Campo
Palacio Real Testamentario Medina del Campo
Plaza Mayor de la Hispanidad Medina del Campo
Colegiata de San Antolín Medina del Campo
Museo de las Ferias Medina del Campo

visits.text: Head northeast. After 30 minutes, you reach Atienza, a fortified village clinging to a rocky outcrop at 1,169 m altitude. Seven medieval gates still pierce its ramparts (including the Arco de Arrebatacapas and the Puerta de la Virgen). At the summit, the castle ruins—described by Lonely Planet as seeming like 'a natural extension of the rock'—command a complete panorama over the Guadalajara sierras. Atienza preserves several Romanesque churches, including San Gil (12th century) now converted into a sacred art museum. Plaza del Trigo (Wheat Square), lined with half-timbered houses, is the village heart.

Continue 50 minutes east to enter Soria province and reach Medinaceli. This hilltop village at 1,200 m altitude bears the legacy of every civilization that passed through: Celtiberian, Roman, Visigothic, Arab, Castilian. Its star monument is the Roman Arch, dating from the 1st century AD—the only triple-arched monumental gateway preserved in Spain. Set atop the hill facing the Jalón valley, it measures roughly 13 m long and 8 m high. Its exact dating is debated—long thought to be Trajanic, recent research attributes it more likely to the Domitian era.

Beyond the arch, wander through Medinaceli (population 700) which harbours dense heritage: the Collegiate Church of Santa María de la Asunción (16th century), the Palace of the Dukes of Medinaceli on Plaza Mayor (hosting contemporary art exhibitions), the convent of Santa Isabel, and remnants of the Arab walls. Plaza Mayor, with its arcades and discreet evening lighting, has an atmosphere out of time. This is also where Al-Mansur (Almanzor), the fearsome military leader of the Caliphate of Córdoba, supposedly died in 1002, after his defeat at Calatañazor.

activities.text: Savour Medinaceli's tranquillity in the late afternoon. The village is almost deserted—perfect for walking along the ramparts with views over the Jalón valley and the Castilian plateaux. If you're a foodie, the region produces Soria lamb, seasonal mushrooms, and black truffles in winter.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

visits.text: The Teatro Romano (16-15 BC) seated 6,000 spectators. The two-tiered stage with veined blue marble columns has been reconstructed through anastylosis.
The neighboring Anfiteatro (8 BC) hosted gladiatorial combat. The elliptical arena measuring 64 by 44 meters could hold 15,000 people.
Rafael Moneo's Museo Nacional de Arte Romano (1986) showcases mosaics, sculptures, and inscriptions. The red-brick building forms a contemporary Roman arch.
Cross the Roman Bridge over the Guadiana. At 792 meters with 60 arches, it's one of the longest Roman bridges still in use.
The Temple of Diana (1st century AD) raises its Corinthian columns in the city center. A Renaissance palace was built against it in the 16th century.
The Alcazaba (835 AD) is an Umayyad fortress. Descend into the aljibe, a vaulted cistern fed by the Guadiana through an underground gallery.
The Arco de Trajano (early 2nd century) stands 15 meters tall. This monumental granite gateway marked the entrance to the provincial forum.

activities.text: The Festival Internacional de Teatro Clásico has brought the Roman theater to life every summer since 1933. Greek tragedies and Latin comedies echo from the ancient stage.
Walk along the Guadiana riverbank to the Los Milagros aqueduct. Its 38 arches of brick and granite soar 25 meters high.
Taste DO Ribera del Guadiana wines at downtown bodegas. Full-bodied reds and fruity whites from Extremadura's vineyards.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas
Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

Observation of the red waters of the Tinto River
