days_plan.itin_title: Huelva Province, Spain: Mining Heritage and Untouched Atlantic Beaches

places.name: Miranda Del Castanar

visits.text: Miranda del Castañar is, along with Ávila, one of only two villages in Spain to have preserved its entire medieval wall circuit intact. The 631 meters of 13th-century fortification encircle the historic core with four gates oriented to the cardinal points. The village sits on a rocky promontory between the Francia and San Benito rivers—its origins trace back to the 12th century with the Hospitaller Order of Jerusalem. In 1213, Alfonso IX of León made it a villa y concejo, administrative capital of Sierra de Francia.

The Zúñiga Castle (14th-15th century) dominates the walled enclosure from its eastern promontory. Its Torre del Homenaje bears an inscription dated 1451 and a Zúñiga coat of arms. The parade ground in front of the castle still serves as a plaza de toros during festivals—locals claim it as Spain's oldest square-shaped bullring. King Enrique IV created the County of Miranda del Castañar in 1457 for his son Diego López de Zúñiga.

The Hermitage of Cristo del Humilladero (13th century) stands at the village entrance, just before you pass through the walls. Here you'll grasp the scale of the defensive system: after the hermitage, a rectangular esplanade that once served as a moat, then the walls and towers. Climb the camino de ronda—the walkway atop the ramparts—for a plunging view over the sierra.

The Bell Tower (Torre de las Campanas) stands separate from the church—deliberately so. It was financed and built in 1612 by the residents so they could ring the bells at will, without depending on the clergy. The tension between municipal power and nobility is readable in every stone of Miranda: more than 80 houses bearing noble coats of arms belonged to great families drawn to the Villa Condal.

activities.text: The Camino de los Prodigios, a 10 km loop linking Miranda del Castañar to Villanueva del Conde through oaks, strawberry trees, and olive trees, is dotted with contemporary art installations integrated into nature. It's the most popular route in the province's 'Arte en la Naturaleza' series. Count on 3½ hours at an easy pace.

In the evening, stroll out to the Hermitage of Virgen de la Cuesta, outside the walls to the south, for the view of Peña de Francia lit by sunset. Legend says the Virgin appeared to children in the hollow of an olive tree at this spot—a 17th-century apparition that remains at the heart of September's patron saint festivals.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Ermita Cristo del Humilladero Miranda del Castañar
Torre de las Campanas Miranda del Castañar
Ermita Virgen de la Cuesta Miranda del Castañar

visits.text: Arriving in Mogarraz, you'll sense you're being watched—and it's no illusion. Over 800 portraits of former residents gaze down from every house façade in the village. The story behind this gallery is striking: in 1967, a local photographer took ID photos of the 388 inhabitants so they could obtain identity cards without traveling to Salamanca. Those negatives slept in a cigar box for decades until artist Florencio Maíllo—born in the village—rediscovered them and decided in 2012 to paint each face on metal plates and hang them on the façade where the person had lived. The exhibition, called 'Retrata2-388,' was meant to last six months—villagers refused to remove the portraits, and the project has only grown since.

The Hermitage of Humilladero, a small 13th-century temple at the village entrance, marks the start of the visit. Just in front, the crucero de los judíos (Jews' cross from the 17th century) bears carved skulls recalling the village's past as a converted Jewish quarter—Mogarraz was founded and repopulated in the 11th century by French and Gascon settlers according to historical sources, and its historic center is classified as a Site of Cultural Interest.

The Ethnographic Museum Casa de las Artesanías, on the main square, presents Sierra de Francia traditions and crafts: embroidery, leatherwork, filigree in silver and gold. It's a useful complement for understanding the human context behind the portraits on the façades.

activities.text: Don't leave without doing the Camino del Agua—a marked 6 km loop departing from the village through chestnut and oak forests, with waterfalls and ruined mills. It's one of the trails in the 'Arte en la Naturaleza' series developed by Salamanca province: you'll encounter contemporary art installations in the midst of nature. Count on 2½ hours at an easy pace.

At the table, don't leave without trying limón serrano—a cold salad typical of Sierra de Francia blending citrus, cured meat, hard-boiled egg, and canned fish, originating with the converted Jews who inhabited the region and composed their dishes with whatever they had on hand.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

visits.text: Mojácar Pueblo perches at 170 meters altitude at the end of the Sierra Cabrera. Its maze of whitewashed alleys preserves the village's Al-Andalus structure. On the facades, the Indalo—a Neolithic cave figure from the Cueva de los Letreros—is painted on doors to ward off the evil eye.
The Mirador del Castillo, built on the ruins of a 16th-century castle, offers 360-degree views: the sea, the Río Aguas valley, and the Sierra Cabrera mountains. Come at sunset for the best light.

The Fuente Mora is where, according to tradition, Mojácar's last Moorish governor negotiated peaceful surrender to the Catholic Monarchs in 1488. Its twelve water jets flow beneath a commemorative plaque.

The Church of Santa María, built around 1560 on the site of a former mosque, retains a fortress-like appearance. At its feet, the arcade-bordered Plaza del Parterre serves as a terrace with valley views.

Pass through the Puerta de la Ciudad, a 16th-century entrance gate adorned with Mojácar's emblem: a double-headed eagle. Just inside, the Casa del Torreón served as a customs house where visitors paid to enter the village.
Descend to Mojácar Playa for its 17 km of coastline. Among the beaches, Marina de la Torre and El Descargador fly the blue flag. Fine sand and calm waters make them family-friendly beaches.

activities.text: Get lost in the flower-filled alleys of Mojácar Pueblo. Purple bougainvillea spills from white balconies, and every corner reveals a new sea view.
Olive oil and roasted almond tasting in the village's grocery shops. Almería province produces renowned extra virgin olive oil from Picual and Arbequina olives.
Grilled fish dinner on the Mojácar Playa waterfront. The espeto—sardines grilled on wood-fire skewers—is a local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert

places.name: Molinaseca

visits.text: Molinaseca is one of the most beautiful villages on the Camino Francés in El Bierzo. Declared a Historic-Artistic Site in 1975, this stone village belonged in the Middle Ages to the lordship of Ramiro Froilaz, nephew of El Cid Campeador. Its seven-arched medieval bridge over the Meruelo River—built of ashlar and cobblestone in the 12th century—is the traditional entrance to the village for pilgrims. In summer, the river below becomes a natural swimming hole. Calle Real, which leads from the bridge, is lined with armorial palaces, stone houses, and the 17th-century sanctuary of Nuestra Señora de las Angustias, whose Virgin is the village's patroness.

Twelve kilometers beyond Molinaseca on the LZ-24 road, the village of El Acebo deserves a stop: slate houses aligned along a single street, wooden balconies overhanging the Camino. This mountain village at 1,150 meters elevation was the dreaded stage for pilgrims before descending to Molinaseca. A wooden cross at the path's edge pays tribute to Camino cyclists—a tradition established by the riders themselves. The return descent to Molinaseca offers panoramic views over the Bierzo valley.

Lombillo de los Barrios, en route to your next stop, has been classified as a Historic-Artistic Site since 2014. Its church of San Martín and the chapel of Nuestra Señora de la Encarnación form the village's monumental core. The Mirador del Bierzo on the heights offers a panoramic view over the entire comarca—stop for 20 minutes to take in the vista.

activities.text: Swimming in the Meruelo River at Molinaseca in late afternoon—the terraces of bars overlooking the bridge and river are the perfect spot to end the day with a glass of Bierzo mencía. In summer you need to arrive before noon to get a good spot on the riverbanks.

Hiking the section of the Camino Francés between El Acebo and Molinaseca: 7 km descending from the mountain hamlet, first through oak forest then along the river. It's one of the Camino's most physical stages—you'll do it in reverse from the pilgrims, downhill, which changes everything.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

map_keywords: Sanctuaire Nuestra Señora de las Angustias Molinaseca
Mirador del Bierzo Lombillo de los Barrios

places.name: Monforte De Lemos

visits.text: Monforte de Lemos is the capital of the Tierra de Lemos and the most important town in the interior of Lugo province. Dominating the town from its hilltop, the monumental complex of San Vicente del Pino brings together a Benedictine monastery (founded in the 10th century, rebuilt in the 16th–17th), the Torre del Homenaje (a 30-metre keep, one of the largest in Galicia, 13th century), and the ruins of the palace of the Counts of Lemos. From the tower's summit, the view sweeps across the entire Cabe valley and the surrounding mountains.

Monforte's star monument is the Colegio de Nuestra Señora de la Antigua, nicknamed the 'Escorial of Galicia' for its monumentality. This Jesuit college was founded in 1593 by Cardinal Rodrigo de Castro, Archbishop of Seville and a Monforte native. The building, in Herreran style (the same as Madrid's Escorial), is immense: an austere granite façade, a church on a Greek-cross plan with a retable by Francisco de Moure, and above all an extraordinary collection of paintings including works attributed to Andrea del Sarto and the school of El Greco. The college's picture gallery is one of the most important in Galicia.

Head back down to the centre for the Puente Viejo over the Río Cabe, of Roman origin, and wander through the lanes of old Monforte—noble houses with coats of arms, seigneurial palaces, and quiet little squares. The church of Santa María de A Régoa, former church of the Franciscan convent, preserves a Gothic portal and a venerated Romanesque Virgin.

activities.text: Monforte's gastronomy is that of deep Galicia: empanada (tuna or meat pie), lacón con grelos (pork shoulder with turnip greens), caldo gallego (thick cabbage and bean soup). For dessert, filloa (Galician crêpe) and bica de Monforte, a light almond-paste cake.

If time allows, take an excursion into the wine villages around Monforte—Doade, Sober, Chantada—to see the terraced vineyards up close and visit a cellar. Ribeira Sacra's Mencía is starting to gain international recognition.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

map_keywords: Colegio del Cardenal Monforte de Lemos
Monasterio San Vicente del Pino Monforte

visits.text: The Salto del Gitano is a 300-meter-high quartzite gorge. The Tagus River threads between rock faces where 80 pairs of griffon vultures nest.
The Salto viewpoint offers one of Europe's premier birdwatching spots. Griffon vultures, black vultures (250 pairs), Spanish imperial eagles, and black storks soar at eye level.
The Centro de Interpretación de Villarreal de San Carlos presents the park's ecosystem. Three exhibition rooms explain Monfragüe's geology, fauna, and flora.
Climb to the Castillo de Monfragüe (134 steps). From this medieval fortress, 360-degree views sweep across holm oak forests and the Tagus meanders.
The Portilla del Tiétar forms another spectacular rocky gorge. Quartzite cliffs shelter nesting golden eagles and eagle owls in the crevices.
The banks of the Tagus and Tiétar harbor otters, kingfishers, and cormorants. The two rivers meet at the heart of the national park.

activities.text: Birdwatching session with spotting scope. Identify griffon vultures, black vultures, Egyptian vultures, Bonelli's eagles, booted eagles, and common buzzards.
Follow the red trail (8 km). Starting from Villarreal, passing the Salto del Gitano, climbing toward the castle along the quartzite ridge.
Picnic beneath the holm oaks of the dehesa. This agro-silvo-pastoral ecosystem typical of Extremadura combines open woodland, pastures, and Iberian pig farming.
The park is certified as a Starlight Destination. On clear nights, watch the Milky Way from viewpoints far from artificial light.

days_plan.itin_title: Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

visits.text: Monleón is one of Salamanca's best-kept secrets. This village of fewer than 100 inhabitants, perched at 900 meters and encircled by medieval walls, barely registers in guidebooks. At the entrance, just beside the puerta de la villa, a zoomorphic granite sculpture—a verraco (bull), typical of the Vettones and dating to the 4th century BC—announces the site's antiquity. The village appears in archives as early as 1199 and was repopulated around 1215.

The 15th-century castle, attached to the walls at the southwest corner of the enclosure, has a wild history. Its owner in 1477, one Don Bernardo Maldonado called 'the Tyrant,' counterfeited money and committed all manner of abuses on neighboring lands. Troops of King Ferdinand the Catholic had to besiege the castle to put him out of commission. The square keep tower still counts 5 levels and 8 turrets.

Outside the walls, on the path leading to the 'Las ollas de la sapa' site, lie the ruins of a 6th-8th century AD medieval village (Monte Alcaide) and several lagares—wine presses carved directly into granite bedrock. These stone basins scattered across the landscape testify to the region's medieval viticulture.

activities.text: The circular hike on GR-181 'Ruta de los Caminos Históricos de Entresierras' between Monleón and El Tornadizo edges along the Alagón River and crosses landscapes of scrubland and granite ravines that are virtually pristine. Count on 3-4 hours depending on your pace for the complete loop.

Monleón has only one bar—an advantage really, since it concentrates all the village's social life. It's the ideal place to talk with the handful of residents and hear the village's history from their mouths. The tapas are renowned for their generosity.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

places.name: Montemayor Del Rio

visits.text: Montemayor del Río occupies a spectacular enclave in the Sierra de Béjar, at the historic frontier between the kingdoms of León, Castile, and Extremadura. Its San Vicente castle commands a precious valley planted with chestnut and oak. The fortress was built in the late 13th century on orders of Infante Don Pedro, son of Alfonso X the Wise—but it's the 14th and 15th centuries that gave the castle its present appearance, with four towers (two round, two square) and its keep. In 1538, Charles V created the Marquessate of Montemayor for Juan de Silva, Captain General of Navarre. Declared a Site of Cultural Interest in 1949, the castle now houses a Medieval Interpretation Center with guided tours.

The village itself deserves a long wander. Its medieval streets, church, and old center lanes hosted merchants and muleteers for centuries on the Cañada Real (old transhumance route) and the Calzada—the Roman Via de la Plata linking Mérida to Astorga. The arcades on the plaza and carved lintels bear witness to a town of passage and exchange.

activities.text: In summer, Montemayor del Río's natural pools—carved into granite and fed by a mountain stream—rank among the region's finest. Water flows straight down from the Sierra de Béjar peaks. Count on a half-hour walk from the village to reach them.

Lunch in Montemayor offers a chance to try the stews and mountain sausages typical of the Sierra de Béjar—quality cured meats, some prepared in villagers' own homes. Nearby in Béjar, you'll also find local specialties featuring Iberian ham.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Château San Vicente Montemayor del Río
Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción Montemayor del Río

visits.text: En route southeast, cross the Sierra de Cardeña y Montoro Natural Park—the highest in Córdoba province. The Mediterranean forest of holm oaks and cork oaks is dense, and the wildlife rich: Iberian lynx (reintroduced), wolf (last Andalusian populations), otter, Spanish imperial eagle. The Río Yeguas trail crosses spectacular granite rock landscapes. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a hike in the park before descending toward Montoro.

Montoro is a surprise. This village of 10,000 inhabitants, classified as a Historic-Artistic Ensemble since 1969, sits on a promontory encircled by a spectacular meander of the Guadalquivir—almost a peninsula. What strikes you first is the color: the monuments are built in piedra molinaza, a local red sandstone that gives the church, ducal palace, and manor façades terracotta hues. The church of San Bartolomé (15th century), in the center on Plaza de España, has a red sandstone bell tower nicknamed 'the Red Giralda' for its resemblance to the Sevillian campanile. Opposite, the Ducal Palace of the Casa de Alba (16th century), now the town hall, presents Mudéjar elements in piedra molinaza.

Descend toward the Puente de las Donadas, built in 1498 by Enrique Egas—one of the finest bridges in the province. Its name ('bridge of the women who gave') comes from the fact that the women of Montoro sold their jewelry to finance its construction, and the king granted them in exchange the privilege of no longer having to lodge or feed troops. From the bridge, the view of the Guadalquivir's meander encircling Montoro is the village's emblematic image. Before leaving Montoro, seek out the Casa de las Conchas—a house whose façade and interior patios are entirely covered with seashells, 45 million in total, accumulated over 50 years by a passionate local.

activities.text: Montoro is at the heart of Córdoba's olive-growing zone—the surrounding olive groves produce DOP Montoro-Adamuz olive oil. Cooperatives offer visits and tastings. Local gastronomy reflects the terroir: ajo sopeao (tomato, pepper, and garlic stew), tortilla de faisanes (bean omelet), perdices en salsa (partridge in sauce), and tortas de manteca for dessert.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

visits.text: Moratalla is a perched village in northwestern Murcia, dominated by a medieval castle from which the view over the mountains and pine forests is superb. The village climbs in narrow lanes up to the fortress, with whitewashed houses and an end-of-the-world atmosphere. But what makes Moratalla famous is its rock art: the region shelters several rock shelters decorated with prehistoric paintings classified as UNESCO World Heritage as part of the Mediterranean Basin Rock Art of the Iberian Peninsula. The most accessible sites are the abrigos del Cañaíca del Calar, with hunting scenes dating back several millennia.

The road between Moratalla and Lorca (about 1h30 drive) crosses landscapes of dry mountains and spectacular gorges. The Cañón de los Almadenes, accessible from Calasparra or Cieza, is one of the most impressive canyons in southeastern Spain. Lorca, your exit point towards Murcia, is dominated by its fortress with seven towers - this place is already covered in another ORT itinerary.

activities.text: Operators offer canoe or kayak descents through the Segura gorges, between Calasparra and Cieza, passing through the Cañón de los Almadenes. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a leisurely descent through rock walls and moderate rapids. It's an activity accessible to beginners and perfect for ending the trip on a nature note.

Before leaving northwestern Murcia, taste the rice of Calasparra - the only rice in Spain to have a protected designation of origin. Grown in the terraces irrigated by the Segura since Arab times, it's smaller and firmer than other Spanish rice varieties, perfect for paellas and caldero dishes (fish and rice stew).

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras

places.name: Morella

visits.text: The castle of Morella crowns the rocky outcrop at 1,070 meters altitude. Built in the 13th century, it witnessed battles involving El Cid and sheltered Pope Luna in 1414.
The ramparts stretch for 2.5 kilometers and have protected the town since the 14th century. Their 14 towers and 6 gates testify to Morella's strategic importance.

The Basilica of Santa María la Mayor displays its Gothic portals—the Portal of the Apostles and the Portal of the Virgins—carved in the 14th and 15th centuries. The spiral staircase of the choir loft is entirely covered with painted reliefs.

The aqueduct of Santa Llúcia dates from 1338. Its two surviving Gothic arches once carried water from the springs to the town. The trail alongside offers panoramic views across the Els Ports countryside.
The Torres de San Miguel form the main gateway. These twin octagonal towers from the 14th century house a collection of medieval games and offer stunning views over Morella's rooftops.
Push open the door of the San Francisco convent to discover the Danse Macabre, an enigmatic 14th-century fresco depicting nobility, clergy, and common people facing death.
Follow the arcades of Blasco de Alagón and climb the stepped alleys. Noble houses—Ciurana, Piquer, Rovira, Cardinal Ram—punctuate the route.

activities.text: Morella is the kingdom of black truffles. Restaurants offer special menus and tastings from November through March, accompanied by local cheeses aged in cellars.
The Paseo de la Alameda circles the mountain. This shaded promenade offers changing perspectives on the ramparts and the surrounding countryside.
Textile workshops keep alive a centuries-old tradition. Some artisans open their doors to demonstrate old-fashioned weaving techniques.
The cave paintings of Morella La Vella lie 6 kilometers away. These UNESCO-listed hunting scenes can be visited in small groups on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves

map_keywords: Basilique Santa María la Mayor Morella

visits.text: The Cathedral of Saint Mary blends a soaring Gothic interior with a baroque façade designed by Jaime Bort in 1751. Its 95-meter tower—Spain's second tallest—offers sweeping views over Murcia's vast orchards and market gardens.

Step through the doors of the Real Casino and prepare to be dazzled. The Moorish patio glitters with 20,000 sheets of gold leaf, the neo-baroque ballroom displays allegorical ceiling frescoes celebrating the arts, and the English library is adorned with wrought-iron flamingos.
Plaza de las Flores fills with terrace cafés by 5 PM. Cross the Puente de los Peligros over the Segura River, where a statue of the Virgin has stood watch since the 18th century.
The Salzillo Museum showcases the baroque processional sculptures of Francisco Salzillo. Polychrome wood, hauntingly lifelike faces, meticulous detail in every gesture.
The Episcopal Palace unfurls an exuberant rococo façade across the Plaza del Cardenal Belluga, a riot of ornamental flourishes.

activities.text: Go on a tapas crawl around Plaza Julián Romea. Marineras (anchovy toasts), michirones (spiced fava beans), pasteles de carne (savory meat pies)—every bar has its signature bite.
Paparajotes are lemon-tree leaves dipped in sweet batter and fried crisp—a uniquely Murcian dessert that tastes like citrus-scented doughnuts.
The Teatro Romea stages concerts, operas, and comedies in a stunning 19th-century hall with remarkable acoustics.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida

places.name: Muxia

visits.text: The Sanctuary of Nosa Señora da Barca, built in the 18th century on a rocky headland, marks the spiritual terminus of the Fisterra pilgrimage route.
The Pedra de Abalar rocks and sways when storms batter the coast. The Pedra dos Cadrís is said to cure back pain if you crawl beneath it nine times.
The A Ferida monument commemorates the 2002 Prestige oil spill disaster. Two granite blocks separated by a fissure symbolize the wound inflicted on the coast.
Cape Vilán thrusts its white lighthouse 345 feet above the pounding surf. Spain's first electrified beacon, lit in 1896, warns ships of the treacherous Costa da Morte.
The English Cemetery near the cape holds the graves of 172 sailors from HMS Serpent, wrecked on these rocks in 1890.

activities.text: Hike the coastal trail from Muxía to Monte Corpiño for sweeping views across the Ría de Camariñas and out to Cape Vilán.
The Atlantic-battered cliffs draw cormorants and kittiwakes. Bring binoculars to watch the seabird colonies wheel and dive.
Sunset from the sanctuary illuminates the polished granite boulders in fiery gold. Waves explode against the Pedra da Vela in thunderous bursts.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia Road Trip: Santiago de Compostela and the Coast of Death

map_keywords: Sanctuaire Nosa Señora da Barca Muxía
Cimetière des Anglais Costa da Morte

visits.text: Navacerrada Pass (Puerto de Navacerrada) sits at 1,860 meters, straddling Madrid and Segovia provinces. A natural crossroads between Siete Picos to the west and Bola del Mundo to the east, the latter crowned by rocket-shaped television antennas.
Gaze across Cuerda Larga, a mountain ridge stretching toward infinity. Scots pines (Pinus sylvestris) cling to steep slopes, alpine meadows burst with wildflowers come summer.
Bola del Mundo peaks at 2,258 meters, crowned by historic antennas that once broadcast television across the Iberian Peninsula. From the summit, panoramas unfold in every direction.
The Siete Picos massif arrays seven granite peaks in a dramatic line. The seventh summit reaches 2,138 meters, offering 360° views across the Sierra de Guadarrama.

activities.text: Hike to Siete Picos via the PR-M trail. The rocky ridge demands some easy scrambling. With 764 meters of elevation gain, spectacular panoramas are guaranteed every step of the way.
The Guarramillas chairlift operates in summer. Ride up for panoramic views to Bar La Bola, a high-altitude establishment at 2,000 meters—the highest point in the Community of Madrid.
In winter, the ski resort offers family-friendly slopes and breathtaking views. Light, dry snow characteristic of high altitude. Snow cover typically arrives in January.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

visits.text: Ramparts and historic center of Niebla
Soto Dolmen in Trigueros

activities.text: Walk in the countryside between Niebla and Trigueros

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva Province, Spain: Mining Heritage and Untouched Atlantic Beaches

places.name: O Cebreiro

visits.text: The road from Monforte to O Cebreiro crosses the mountainous heart of Lugo province—chestnut forests, meadows, stone hamlets where time seems to have stopped. O Cebreiro (1,300 m altitude) is the gateway to the Camino de Santiago in Galicia for pilgrims coming from Castile on the Camino Francés. The village has barely a handful of houses, a pilgrims' hostel, and a church—but the atmosphere is extraordinary, especially when fog rises from the valley and wraps around the pallozas.

The pallozas are the very reason for visiting O Cebreiro. These pre-Roman dwellings—circular or oval stone houses with conical thatched roofs—were inhabited until the mid-20th century. They descend directly from pre-Roman Celtic constructions (the castros). Several have been restored, and one houses a small ethnographic museum showing how peasant families lived: a single room shared with animals, a central hearth with no chimney (smoke escaped through the thatched roof). These structures are unique to this mountainous zone of Galicia and the Bierzo.

The church of Santa María la Real in O Cebreiro, pre-Romanesque (9th century), is one of the oldest in Galicia still standing. It's also the site of the 'Miracle of O Cebreiro': in the 14th century, a priest who doubted transubstantiation saw the bread and wine literally transform into flesh and blood during Mass. The chalice and paten from the miracle are still preserved in the church—and it's precisely these that are said to have inspired the legend of the Holy Grail in Wagner's Parsifal. The simple, moving Romanesque chalice is displayed in a case to the left of the altar.

activities.text: Walk a section of the Camino de Santiago from O Cebreiro—even if you're not a pilgrim, the descent to the village of Liñares then Hospital da Condesa (4 km, 1h) gives you a taste of what walkers experience: an earth path between stone walls, birch forests, views of the Lugo mountains. You'll encounter pilgrims of all nationalities carrying their scallop shells and walking sticks.

If you continue by car to the Alto do Poio (1,337 m), the highest point of the Camino de Santiago in Galicia, you'll cross a landscape of moorland and peat bogs that recalls Scotland or Ireland. It's also excellent territory for spotting wild Galician horses (garañóns). In the evening, dine at O Cebreiro on caldo gallego and empanada—Galician mountain cuisine is simple and comforting.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María la Real O Cebreiro

visits.text: Royal Palace of Olite — towers and walkways.
Santa María and San Pedro churches.
Medieval alleys and cellars.

activities.text: Navarre DO wine tasting.
Falconry workshop (according to season).
Sunset from the ramparts.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre, Spain: Castles, Pyrenean Forests and the Camino de Santiago

places.name: Orbaneja Del Castillo

visits.text: Orbaneja del Castillo is perhaps Spain's most improbable village: a 25-meter waterfall emerges directly from the Cueva del Agua, flows through the village dividing houses into two quarters, and plunges into the Ebro below. Along the way, the water has formed terraced pools with turquoise reflections surrounded by green moss—the contrast is striking. The village, declared a historic site in 1993, has about fifty inhabitants and stone lanes where time seems frozen. Park at the lot outside the center.

The Cueva del Agua, from which the waterfall springs, is a karst cave you can visit guided in summer and on weekends. From the upper part of the town, climb to Orbaneja's natural viewpoint for a plunging view over the Ebro canyon—the limestone walls cut out rock silhouettes that locals compare to African profiles. Also look for the natural arch that the river has sculpted in the cliff above the village.

activities.text: The Ebro canyon (Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón) offers spectacular hikes from Orbaneja. The circular route linking Orbaneja del Castillo to Valdelateja follows the canyon for 17 km—allow a good half-day. If you prefer something shorter, the GR-99 between Pesquera de Ebro and Manzanedo offers the best gorge views in 2-3 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Cueva del Agua Orbaneja del Castillo

visits.text: Ourense is Galicia's 'thermal capital'—and thermal waters have been the very reason for its existence for 2,000 years. Start at As Burgas, right in the historic centre: two monumental fountains (the 17th-century Burga de Arriba and the 19th-century Burga de Abaixo) from which water gushes out at 67°C. The Romans built baths here and worshipped the 'nymphs of the springs.' Local legend claims the springs originate beneath the Santo Cristo chapel inside the cathedral. You can touch the water (careful—it's scalding) and even drink it, though its sulphurous taste isn't particularly pleasant; its curative properties, however, are well documented. The modern, free-entry As Burgas Interpretation Centre explains the geology and history of the site.

Walk up to the Cathedral of San Martín, a national monument begun in the 12th century. Its hidden treasure is the Pórtico del Paraíso (Paradise Portico), a polychrome Romanesque sculptural ensemble that has retained its original colours—an exceptionally rare thing. It's the 'cousin' of Santiago's famous Pórtico de la Gloria, but here the medieval paintwork remains intact. In the Chapel of San Cosme and San Damián, there's a quirky feature: a permanent nativity scene displayed all year round, with figures dressed in Galician costume. Exit via the Praza Maior, surrounded by arcades, where you'll find the Casa Consistorial and the archaeological museum housed in the old episcopal palace.

Descend towards the Miño through the old town—medieval lanes, Renaissance palaces, shady squares. The Ponte Vella (Old Bridge), of Roman origin but rebuilt in the 13th century, spans the river on seven stone arches. On the far side, the ultra-modern Ponte do Milenio (2001) creates a spectacular contrast with its millennial neighbour.

activities.text: In the afternoon, treat yourself to the thermal circuit along the Miño—a 3 km riverside walk with four open-air thermal stations. Start at A Chavasqueira (water emerges at 41°C, free pools), then O Tinteiro (43°C, good for skin conditions), the Pozas de Muíño das Veigas (65–72°C at source, four pools), and finally Outariz (61°C, the largest thermal area, six pools). All are free and open year-round. The experience is magical in winter, when steam rises into the cold air, but it's pleasant in any season. Bring your swimsuit and a towel.

In the evening, Ourense transforms into a tapas and wine town—locals love going out. Rúa do Vino and the streets around Praza Maior form the heart of the nightlife. Try the pulpo á feira (Galician-style octopus), pimientos de Padrón, and a crisp, chilled Ribeiro white. Ourense's cuisine is among the finest in Galicia.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

visits.text: San Salvador Cathedral blends flamboyant Gothic with pre-Romanesque roots. Its solitary tower dominates the old town and once served as a compass for pilgrims walking the Camino Primitivo. Inside, three ribbed-vault naves converge on a towering main altarpiece.

Step through the door of the Cámara Santa, a 9th-century chapel built by Alfonso II the Chaste. Six pairs of apostles carved as Romanesque statue-columns support the vault. The treasury holds the Cross of the Angels (808), Oviedo's emblem, and the Cross of Victory (908), symbol of Asturias—both masterpieces of chiseled gold and cabochon jewels.

Climb Mount Naranco to Santa María del Naranco, the palace of Ramiro I consecrated in 848. This rectangular 20 × 6-meter two-story structure is the finest example of European pre-Romanesque architecture. The upper floor reveals a barrel vault with transverse arches, triple-arched loggias, twisted columns, and medallions carved with animals and horsemen.

A hundred meters away, San Miguel de Lillo preserves only a third of the original church, but its door jambs carved with Roman circus scenes and stone lattice screens remain unique in 9th-century religious architecture. Both monuments have been UNESCO-listed since 1985.
Descend to Plaza del Fontán, an arcaded square that hosts Oviedo's covered market. Asturian cheeses, fabada in jars, local cider—the stalls overflow with regional treasures.

Detour to La Foncalada, a 9th-century fountain built under Alfonso III the Great. Still functioning today, it's the only hydraulic engineering monument from the early Middle Ages with UNESCO status in Spain.

activities.text: Push open the door of a sidrería in the Casco Antiguo and order a culín. The server pours the cider from high above to aerate it—the escanciado, an unmissable Asturian ritual.
Wander through Parque San Francisco, the green lung of downtown. Its shaded paths shelter free-roaming peacocks and contemporary sculptures scattered among centuries-old trees.
Follow Oviedo's urban sculpture trail: dozens of bronze statues line the pedestrian streets of the center, from La Maternidad to the seated silhouette of Woody Allen on Calle Milicias.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

visits.text: La Seu Cathedral has commanded the bay since the 13th century. Its nave soars to 45 meters—only Beauvais rises higher. The 14-meter Gothic rose window is the world's largest.
Almudaina Palace was built in the 14th century atop an Arab alcazaba. Still the official royal residence in Majorca, it houses the Chapel of Saint Anne and opulent state rooms.
Parc de la Mar stretches beneath the ramparts. Its artificial lake mirrors La Seu in what's become Palma's most iconic photographic composition.
Old town lanes wind between flower-filled patios and ochre facades. The quarter hides Baroque churches, noble houses, and shaded squares.
Step through the doors of the Arab Baths, dating to the 10th century. Their barrel vaults and columns bear witness to Moorish occupation.
Bellver Castle crowns a hill 3 kilometers away. This circular 14th-century fortress—unique in Europe—commands sweeping views across the entire bay.
Passeig del Born is Palma's elegant artery. Lined with plane trees and modernist facades, it connects Plaça de la Reina to the waterfront.

activities.text: The coastal bike path follows the bay for several kilometers, tracing the waterfront from Parc de la Mar to the city beaches.
Ensaimada is best savored in traditional pastry shops. This spiral brioche dusted with powdered sugar defines Majorcan baking.
Position yourself at Bellver Castle as evening falls. The sun sinks behind Palma while lights begin to twinkle across the city.
Olivar Market overflows with local produce. Fresh fish, sobrassada, Mahón cheese, and Majorcan vegetables crowd the stalls.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Discovery of Palos de la Frontera, Christopher Columbus's historic port
Visit to Moguer and its monuments

activities.text: Walk along the banks of the Tinto River near Palos

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva Province, Spain: Mining Heritage and Untouched Atlantic Beaches

visits.text: Santa María Cathedral and Gothic cloister.
Ramparts and citadel of Pamplona.
Old town — encierro streets.

activities.text: Pintxos tasting on the estafeta.
Walk in Taconera Park.
Museum of Navarre according to interest.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre, Spain: Castles, Pyrenean Forests and the Camino de Santiago

visits.text: Drive 45 minutes southeast to Brihuega, nicknamed the 'Garden of Alcarria'. If you visit between late June and mid-July, the lavender fields surrounding the village put on a breathtaking show—endless acres of violet stretching to the horizon, with the Lavender Festival held the second weekend of July. Outside the flowering season, Brihuega still rewards a visit: medieval ramparts with the fortified gates of Cadena and Cozagón, three handsome 13th-century Romanesque churches (Santa María de la Peña, San Miguel, San Felipe), and the former Real Fábrica de Paños, a royal cloth manufactory founded in the 18th century by Charles III.

Continue 30 minutes south to Pastrana, the 'ducal town' of Alcarria, a designated Historic Ensemble since 1966 and member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. The Ducal Palace, designed by Alonso de Covarrubias in the 16th century with a façade replicating Toledo's Alcázar, is inseparable from the Princess of Éboli—Ana de Mendoza y de la Cerda. One-eyed (she wore an eyepatch), beautiful, scheming, first Duchess of Pastrana then mistress of Antonio Pérez (Philip II's secretary), she ended up imprisoned by the king in the palace's east tower (the Cámara de la Reja Dorada) from 1581 until her death in 1592. Look up at the barred window from Plaza de la Hora—that's where she watched the world go by.

Don't leave Pastrana without visiting the Tapestry Museum in the collegiate church of the Asunción. The six 15th-century Flemish-Gothic tapestries, woven in Tournai from cartoons attributed to Nuño Gonçalves, depict the conquest of North African cities by King Afonso V of Portugal. They rank among the world's most important tapestries from this period. The collegiate church itself, of Templar origin (12th century), holds paintings by El Greco and Carreño de Miranda. The Princess of Éboli is buried in the crypt.

activities.text: Pastrana is also linked to Saint Teresa of Ávila, who founded two Discalced Carmelite convents here in 1569—one for women (San José) and one for men (San Pablo, now del Carmen), where Saint John of the Cross served as novice master. The Carmen convent houses a small Teresian museum and a natural history museum. Stroll through the lanes of the upper town, between Calle Princesa de Éboli lined with noble mansions and Calle de la Palma with the House of the Inquisition and the House of the Calatrava Knight.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon
Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

visits.text: Pedraza is the best-preserved medieval village in Castile and León—perhaps in all Spain. Declared a historic site in 1951, it has only one access gate (16th-century wood), cobbled lanes lined with houses bearing noble coats of arms, and an arcaded Plaza Mayor ranking among the region's most beautiful. Park at the outside lot and enter on foot. To avoid weekend crowds, prefer a weekday or arrive before 10am.

Pedraza's castle occupies a rocky outcrop at the village's edge. Built in the 13th century, transformed into a Renaissance palace in the 16th-17th centuries, it was saved from ruin by painter Ignacio Zuloaga who made it his residence and studio. Its keep now houses a museum with some of his works. A little-known historical anecdote: the sons of King Francis I of France were imprisoned in this castle in 1526 as hostages to guarantee the Treaty of Madrid—they stayed four years. Book tickets online.

Pedraza's medieval prison (Cárcel de la Villa), a 13th-century building rebuilt in the 16th, is one of Spain's best preserved. It's visited with guided tours that recreate medieval prison life. If you're there on the first or second Saturday evenings of July, don't miss under any circumstances the Noche de las Velas: electric lights are turned off and thousands of candles illuminate the lanes while concerts take place outdoors.

activities.text: Stroll Calle Mayor between blazoned houses and noble 16th-17th century constructions, witnesses to the village's textile prosperity.
Savor cordero asado, wood-oven roasted lamb that made Pedraza's gastronomic reputation. The British Times in 2024 ranked restaurant El Soportal among the 28 best Mediterranean tables.
Attend the Candle Nights on the first two Saturdays of July. Thousands of candles illuminate lanes, balconies and squares while classical music concerts resound.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

visits.text: Pedroche is the village that gave its name to the entire comarca. Perched on a hill, it's the former capital of the Siete Villas de los Pedroches—the seven villages that governed themselves in the Middle Ages. The landmark monument is the Torre de Pedroche, a 56-meter-high bell tower that dominates the entire landscape. Begun in the 15th century, it was completed by Hernán Ruiz II—the same architect as at Hinojosa, the Mezquita, and the Giralda. Four superimposed sections (square, octagonal, square, cylindrical) rise above the church of El Salvador (Gothic-Mudéjar). Climb to the top if access is open—the view embraces the entire dehesa as far as the eye can see. Around the Plaza de las Siete Villas, the narrow lanes with granite houses preserve the atmosphere of a medieval Sierra Morena market town.

Drive 15 minutes east to Dos Torres, whose historic center is classified as a Property of Cultural Interest. This village was born from the merger of two medieval settlements—Torrefranca and Torremilano—in 1839. The Pedroches granite architecture is best preserved here: manor houses with dark stone façades highlighted with white lime, carved coats of arms, wrought-iron balconies. The Centro de Interpretación de la Arquitectura Popular explains local construction techniques. Don't miss the Plaza de Toros integrated into the urban fabric and the Pozo de la Nieve (snow well), where ice was stored in winter.

Push 10 minutes south to Añora, the smallest village in Los Pedroches. Its dark granite houses striped with white bands create a unique architectural style in the comarca. The church of San Sebastián (16th century) is considered one of the purest examples of Mudéjar art in Los Pedroches. The village is tiny—30 minutes suffices to tour it—but the end-of-the-world atmosphere is total.

activities.text: The Valle de los Pedroches is classified as a Starlight Reserve by UNESCO for its night sky quality—very little light pollution in this rural area. If you stay a night in Pedroche or Pozoblanco, take advantage of it to observe the stars. Operators offer astronomical observation sessions with telescopes, especially in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

visits.text: Arriving in Peñafiel, the castle appears first—impossible to miss. Perched on a narrow ridge between the Duero and Duratón valleys, it hugs the terrain so perfectly it's nicknamed el barco de Castilla (Castile's ship). At 210 meters long but only 33 meters wide, it does indeed resemble a stone vessel sailing the plateau. The earliest mentions date to 943; the current form dates from the 15th century when Pedro Téllez Girón remodeled it in Gothic style. Climb the central keep for a 360° panorama over the Ribera del Duero.

In the castle's south courtyard, the Museo Provincial del Vino traces the entire history of wine in the Duero valley—from vine to glass, covering grape varieties, vinification techniques, and barrel aging. The museum is one of Castile and León's most visited. You finish with a guided tasting of Ribera del Duero wines. Allow 1h30 for the museum and tasting.

Descend into town to discover the Plaza del Coso, an oval square unique in Spain with its painted wooden balconies. Since the 15th century, it has served as a bullring during festivals—bulls run through this enclosed space while spectators watch from the balconies. Just beside it, the convent of San Pablo, founded by the infant Don Juan Manuel in the 14th century—one of medieval Castile's greatest writers—preserves a fine cloister and a collection of sacred art.

activities.text: Peñafiel is the capital of Ribera del Duero, one of Spain's most prestigious wine appellations. The king grape is Tempranillo (called Tinto Fino here), which produces powerful, elegant reds. Operators offer cellar visits with tastings in the surrounding area—the Ribera del Duero wine route has over 300 cellars. The Pintia archaeological site, 4 km away, proves wine was made here 2,500 years ago.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

places.name: Penalba De Santiago

visits.text: To reach Peñalba de Santiago, you must cross the entire Valley of Silence by a single-lane road once called 'the monks' path.' Perched at 1,100 meters in the Aquilanos mountains, this village of about fifty inhabitants is one of Spain's most beautiful: stone and slate houses with wooden balconies, lanes so narrow they seem never to have changed since the 10th century. Park at the entrance lot—the lanes are impassable by car.

The Mozarabic church of Santiago de Peñalba is the only remnant of the monastery founded by San Genadio in the 10th century, completed in 937 by Abbot Salomón. It's a jewel of Spanish Mozarabic architecture—a style born from the fusion of Visigothic traditions and Arab influences. Enter through the double door with horseshoe arches supported by three marble columns: inside, the horseshoe arches divide the nave, and remnants of medieval wall paintings survive on the walls. This is where the body of San Genadio rests.

From the village, a half-hour walk on the old monks' path leads to the Cave of San Genadio, carved into a vertical rock face at the very entrance to the Valley of Silence. The Bishop of Astorga retreated here to meditate in the 9th century. A local legend says he ordered the Oza River to be silent so as not to disturb his prayer—and the river disappeared underground at that spot.

activities.text: The Valley of Silence is also a beautiful invitation to hike in a landscape of oak, chestnut and walnut forests crossed by the Oza River. The trail climbing to the Monastery of San Pedro de Montes (founded in the 7th century by San Fructuoso, rebuilt in the 12th-13th centuries) is one of the region's most beautiful. Allow 3 hours round trip from Peñalba.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Monasterio San Pedro de Montes Bierzo

visits.text: The castle of Peñíscola has dominated the rocky promontory since 1307. Built by the Knights Templar atop an Arab alcazaba, it became the palace of Pope Benedict XIII—Papa Luna—after 1411.
Step through the Portal de San Pedro to enter the old town. Cobbled lanes climb between whitewashed facades all the way up to the castle's parade ground.
Renaissance ramparts wrap around the peninsula for 2 kilometers. From the muralla del Olvido, you can take in the entire coastline and the old town spread out below.
The church of Santa María occupies a former mosque converted in the 14th century. Its bell tower has served as a landmark for sailors for centuries.
The Casa de las Conchas boasts a facade entirely covered in seashells—a unique decoration commissioned by a local family in the 19th century.
Walk out to the lighthouse built in 1899, still keeping watch over the Mediterranean horizon. The surrounding maritime garden offers a cool refuge from the sun.
The Artillery Park spreads out at the foot of the castle. Its terraced gardens host bird-of-prey shows and provide access to the underground casemates.

activities.text: Playa Norte stretches for 2 kilometers of fine sand. Its calm waters are perfect for swimming with views of the castle perched above.
Rice with cuttlefish stains the dish a deep black. This emblematic specialty is best savored at restaurants along the harbor or the seafront promenade.
Find a spot on the rocks as the day draws to a close. The sun drops behind the castle while lights begin to flicker on throughout the old town.
Want to see the castle from a different angle? Rent a kayak to paddle along the base of the rock and discover its marine caves.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves

visits.text: Plasencia keeps its treasures close to the chest. Founded in 1186 by King Alfonso VIII of Castile, it boasts a unique quirk in Spain: two cathedrals stuck side by side. The Catedral Vieja, begun in the 13th century, is a fine example of Romanesque-Gothic transition—sober, compact, with a baroque altarpiece and a stunning ivory Christ. The Catedral Nueva, started in the 15th century, was never finished—but what exists is breathtaking: soaring ribbed vaults, a magnificent altarpiece attributed to Gregorio Fernández, and a heady mix of flamboyant Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque. They're visited together—don't choose, see both.

The Plaza Mayor of Plasencia has been the beating heart of the city since medieval times. Irregularly shaped and ringed by soportales (arcades) lined with bars and restaurants, it hosts a market every Tuesday that's been running uninterrupted since the 12th century. Grab a terrace seat and watch the unhurried rhythm of this 40,000-soul town that carries itself like a regional capital.

Plasencia is part of the Red de Juderías de España—the network of Spain's historic Jewish quarters. The Jewish community settled here from the city's founding in 1186. Traces survive in two neighborhoods: the old judería de la Mota (around today's Parador, a former San Vicente Ferrer convent built on the site of the first synagogue confiscated in 1477) and the "new judería" around Calle Trujillo and Calle Zapatería. In these alleys, plaques set into the pavement bear the names of Jewish families who lived here—Yuçe Alaçán, Abraham Almale, Samuel... Don't miss the Jewish cemetery of El Berrocal outside the walls: it's the only preserved Jewish burial ground in Extremadura, with about twenty anthropomorphic tombs carved into rock, facing Jerusalem.

Walk the circuit of Plasencia's medieval ramparts, still well-preserved along much of their length. You'll pass through the Trujillo, Coria, and Berrozana gates and cross paths with the acueducto—a restored medieval aqueduct running through the San Antón neighborhood. The Palacio del Marqués de Mirabel, built on the site of the old judería de la Mota in the 15th century, is worth a look for its facade and garden (visible from the street).

activities.text: Northern Extremadura's cuisine is generous and underrated. Try Torta del Casar—a creamy soft-paste cheese eaten with a spoon—migas extremeñas (fried breadcrumbs with chorizo and pimiento), jamón ibérico de bellota, and Valle del Jerte cherries in season. The Plaza Mayor and surrounding streets are packed with tapas bars where you'll eat well and abundantly.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

visits.text: Walk and swim on the long beach of Playa d'en Bossa

activities.text: Boat trip along the south coast of Ibiza

days_plan.itin_title: Ibiza and Formentera Guide: Beyond the Clubs to Turquoise Coves and Beaches

places.name: Ponferrada

visits.text: The Templar Castle of Ponferrada is one of the most impressive medieval fortresses in northwest Spain. In 1178, the kings of León ceded the town to the Order of the Temple to protect pilgrims on the Camino. The Templars remained until 1312, when the Order was dissolved. The castle spreads over 8,000 square meters with a complex system of courtyards, barbicans, and towers—the original twelve reproduced constellations. In 1850, the town council sold the walls as a quarry—it took designation as a National Monument in 1924 to halt the destruction. Climb the rampart walk for views over the city and the Bierzo mountains.

The 16th-century Renaissance Basilica de la Encina houses the statue of the Virgin de la Encina—patroness of El Bierzo. According to legend, the Templars found her hidden in an oak trunk (encina) during the Reconquista. Right next door, the Renaissance Clock Tower and the Baroque Town Hall form one of the city's best-preserved civic ensembles.

Peñalba de Santiago perches at 1,110 meters elevation at the bottom of the Valle del Silencio—a valley so deep-cut into the Aquilanos mountains that the Oza River briefly disappears underground, hence the name. Saint Genadio, bishop of Astorga, withdrew here starting in 920 to live as a hermit until his death in 936. He had founded a monastery of which only the Mozarabic church of Santiago remains, completed in 937 by Abbot Salomon—one of the rare and most beautiful examples of preserved Spanish Mozarabic art. Its south portal, with its double horseshoe arch supported by three marble columns with Corinthian capitals, is remarkably graceful. The interior harbors early-medieval wall paintings uncovered during recent restorations. The village itself, with its stone and slate houses with wooden balconies, has been classified as a Cultural Heritage Site since 1998. Plan for the road—narrow and winding, it can be closed in winter.

From the village, a 30-minute trail leads to the Cueva de San Genadio, the cave carved into the vertical cliff at the valley's entrance where the saint withdrew each night to pray. The view from the cave over Peñalba's slate roofs below is the iconic image of the Valle del Silencio.

activities.text: Visit the Museum of Bierzo, housed in the former 15th-century Convent of San Francisco. It traces the comarca's natural and human history, with a remarkable room devoted to the Roman mines—Ponferrada sits at the heart of Las Médulas territory, the most productive gold mine in the Roman Empire.

Evening stroll along the Río Sil. The riverbank has been turned into a tree-lined promenade; it's locals' favorite spot for jogging and aperitifs. The illuminated castle viewed from the riverbanks is the city's signature photo.

Hiking in the Valle del Silencio from Peñalba: the trail continues beyond the Cueva de San Genadio toward the monastery of San Pedro de Montes, founded in the 7th century by Saint Fructuoso and restored by San Genadio in 895. The complete round trip from Peñalba is about 10 km for a half-day in oak forest.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

places.name: Pontevedra

visits.text: Pontevedra's car-free historic center—Galicia's second-largest after Santiago—unfolds in cobbled plazas and stone mansions emblazoned with noble coats of arms.
The Basilica of Santa María a Maior, funded by the mariners' guild in the 16th century, flaunts a Plateresque façade featuring carvings of Christopher Columbus and Hernán Cortés.
The baroque altarpiece on the main altar, carved from chestnut and walnut, dates to the 19th century. Look for the Cristo del Buen Viaje sculpture left of the south portal.
The ruins of Santo Domingo—a 14th-century Dominican monastery—preserve a Gothic transept and choir in a romantically overgrown setting.
The Museum of Pontevedra exhibits Galician art, archaeology, and painting across six interconnected historic buildings.

activities.text: Rías Baixas wineries offer tastings of Albariño—a crisp, mineral white wine grown on granite pergolas overlooking the estuaries.
The Paseo del Lérez follows the river through landscaped parkland dotted with medieval bridges and shaded picnic spots.
The terraces on Plaza de la Leña come alive after 8 PM. Order scallops, razor clams, and Padrón peppers with a glass of local white.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia Road Trip: Santiago de Compostela and the Coast of Death

map_keywords: Basilique Santa María a Maior Pontevedra

places.name: Puente Del Congosto

visits.text: Puente del Congosto owes its name and existence to a single feature: the bridge over the Tormes. This strategic crossing on the Cañada Real Soriana Occidental—the great transhumance route descending toward Extremadura—generated enough toll revenue to finance castle, church, and village. The old bridge, built on orders of the Catholic Monarchs around 1500, is solidly founded on granite bedrock and counts 13 arches; it still retains one of its two original defensive towers. Declared a Site of Cultural Interest with historic site status in 2019, it's the last village in the province to receive this title.

The Dávila Castle, on the left bank of the Tormes, was built in the 14th century to defend and control the strategic bridge crossing. In 1393, King Enrique III ceded the lordship of Puente del Congosto to Gil González Dávila, who rebuilt the fortress. In 1539, the Duke of Alba bought it from Charles V and added a second twin tower. Beautifully restored, it's now private property and can only be viewed from outside—but the silhouette from the bridge is remarkable.

Don't leave without walking the riverside path along the Tormes—the crystalline waters and natural swimming holes make Puente del Congosto a popular summer stop. The entire setting, declared a UNESCO biosphere reserve in 2006, is ideal for spotting European bee-eaters and grey herons along the banks.

activities.text: For whitewater enthusiasts, the Tormes upstream from Puente del Congosto offers rafting sections navigable in spring and early summer. Local outfitters offer 2- to 3-hour trips on the river's rapids. Inquire at the tourist office.

Come evening, the menu del día in village bars invariably features hornazo—a savory pie with ham and hard-boiled eggs, a Salamanca specialty you'll find throughout the province. Also try the cured meats from the Sierra de Béjar.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción Puente del Congosto

visits.text: The natural bridge of Puentedey measures 70 meters long and 15 meters high. The Nela River sculpted it over 85 million years by abandoning an ancient meander to pierce through the hill.
The village was built atop the limestone arch, taking its name from 'Puente de Dios'—the Bridge of God. About fifty inhabitants occupy stone houses with flower-filled balconies overlooking the void.
Walk through the interior of the arch where light sketches changing reflections on the rock. The Puerta del Viento, a lateral cavity, once served to winnow grain in the wind tunnel.
The Romanesque church of San Pelayo dates from the 11th century. Its oldest elements testify to the medieval architecture of the Merindades region.

The palace of Brizuela, also called the palace of Porras, was built in the 15th century. This fortified manor with two rectangular towers rises on the rocks at the highest point of the natural bridge.

activities.text: Descend to the level of the Nela River to admire the arch from below. The clear water reflects the stone and vegetation colonizing the walls in a natural mirror.
Follow the circular trail toward the canals of La Dulla, a series of deep canyons between the municipalities of Valdeporres and Sotoscueva offering wilder landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials

places.name: Puerto De La Cruz

visits.text: Lago Martiánez covers 100,000 m² of seawater pools, gardens, and terraces. Designed by César Manrique in 1977, the complex integrates volcanic rock, sculptures like Las Raíces al Cielo (upside-down eucalyptus trees), and a giant fountain of volcanic stone feeding 27,000 m³ of seawater.
The La Orotava Botanical Garden was founded in 1788 by order of King Charles III. Across 20,000 m², more than four thousand tropical species from around the world flourish among fountains and ponds.

Head to Plaza del Charco, the beating heart of the old town, then walk down to the old harbor where fishing boats land their catch in the early morning. The Hermitage of San Telmo, built in 1780, watches over sailors from the waterfront.

The Castillo de San Felipe, a former defensive fortress, marks the entrance to Playa Jardín—a trio of black volcanic sand beaches landscaped by César Manrique with gardens of endemic species and waterfalls.
The Church of Nuestra Señora de la Peña de Francia, built in 1684 on the site of an older hermitage, dominates the main square. Its tower dates from 1898. Inside, paintings by Canarian masters.

activities.text: Climb to Mirador de La Paz for sweeping views of the Atlantic, Martiánez beach, the Lago, and the Orotava Valley with Teide rising in the background. On clear days, the island of La Palma emerges on the horizon.
Martiánez beach—350 meters of black sand beneath cliffs—draws surfers year-round. Waves roll in with a laid-back atmosphere.

Sample volcanic wines from the Tacoronte-Acentejo appellation at a guachinche, those ephemeral traditional restaurants where you'll also find papas arrugadas, rabbit in salmorejo sauce, and grilled vieja fish.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria
