days_plan.itin_title: Navarre, Spain: Castles, Pyrenean Forests and the Camino de Santiago

places.name: Sequeros

visits.text: Sequeros, once the administrative capital of Sierra de Francia, sits at 870 meters altitude with three-story houses and wrought-iron balconies that tell the story of a prosperous 19th-century local bourgeoisie. Declared a site of historic interest in 2004, the village counts fewer than 250 inhabitants, yet its center remains remarkably preserved. Don't miss Plaza de Eloy Bullón—this is what's left of a former aljama, the medieval Jewish quarter, with lanes so narrow the rooftops nearly touch. The labyrinthine layout reflects centuries of coexistence among Christian, Jewish, and Muslim communities.

The Church of Virgen del Robledo stands as Sequeros's most symbolic monument. Its baroque altarpiece and mudéjar coffered ceiling at the main altar deserve your attention. In its crypt lie the remains of Simón Vela—the French pilgrim Simon Roland who, according to legend, received an apparition of the Virgin in 1434 directing him to a statue hidden for centuries atop Peña de Francia. When he died in 1438, he was buried here in Sequeros, not on the mountain.

Push up to the summit of Peña de Francia at 1,727 meters—count on 20 minutes' drive from Sequeros. The sanctuary of Notre-Dame-du-Rocher-de-France, run by Dominicans since the 15th century, offers 360-degree views spanning the plains of Salamanca, Portugal, and Extremadura. Cervantes mentioned it in Don Quixote, and the Black Virgin is venerated as far away as Latin America and the Philippines. The road is passable from April to November—avoid other months when the summit can remain snowbound.

activities.text: Walk to the Cruz viewpoint to contemplate the Béjar-Candelario range—the village's finest lookout. The Barrero trail leading there edges past the old circular bullring, now converted to public space. Have a glass of mountain wine in one of the bars on Church Square, beneath the granite arcades.

Come evening, explore the Robledo trail departing from the village—a marked 2-3 km path crossing oak forests and reaching the hermitage of Robledo where rest the bones of Juana, 'la Moza Santa,' Sequeros's local prophetess whose legend is intimately bound to the discovery of the Virgin of Peña de Francia.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Sanctuaire Peña de Francia El Cabaco

visits.text: Day at Ses Illetes beach with shallow turquoise waters

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling on the sandbar of Ses Illetes

days_plan.itin_title: Ibiza and Formentera Guide: Beyond the Clubs to Turquoise Coves and Beaches

visits.text: Seville Cathedral is the world's largest Gothic cathedral. Construction began in 1403 on the site of the former Almohad mosque, preserving the Patio de los Naranjos with its 60 orange trees. Inside, Christopher Columbus's tomb—repatriated from Havana—rests on the shoulders of four bronze bearers.

Climb the 35 ramps of La Giralda, a 104-meter minaret built in 1184 by architect Ahmad Ben Baso. Muezzins once rode horses up these ramps for the call to prayer. At the summit, El Giraldillo—a 4-meter bronze statue representing Faith—has been spinning in the wind since 1568.

The Royal Alcázar is Europe's oldest inhabited royal palace. In 1364, Peter I summoned Mudéjar craftsmen from Toledo, Granada, and Seville to create this masterpiece in just two years. Step through the doorway of the Patio de las Doncellas: its rectangular pool flanked by marble columns and intricate stucco rivals the Alhambra itself.

The Alcázar gardens spread across 7 hectares of palms, orange trees, and fountains. The Mercury Pool, the Dance Gardens, and the Grotesque Gallery unfold in welcome coolness. Summer concerts at 'Noches en los Jardines' extend the magic as dusk falls.

Lose yourself in the Barrio de Santa Cruz, the former Jewish quarter where lanes are so narrow two people barely pass. Patios reveal fountains, azulejo tiles, and bougainvillea cascading from wrought-iron balconies. Find your way to Plaza de los Venerables to discover the 17th-century baroque hospice.

The General Archive of the Indies occupies the former Lonja de Mercaderes, a 1598 Renaissance building. Its vaults hold 43,000 files and 80 million pages documenting four centuries of Spanish colonization. The building, cathedral, and Alcázar together form the UNESCO World Heritage site inscribed in 1987.

activities.text: Catch a flamenco performance at a peña in the historic center. Seville is the birthplace of trianero style, born in the Triana neighborhood across the Guadalquivir.
Hop between tapas bars from Alfalfa to Alameda de Hércules. Seville keeps alive the tradition of free tapas with every drink: montaditos, espinacas con garbanzos, carrillada ibérica.

Cross the Puente de Isabel II toward Triana at sunset. The former quarter of potters, sailors, and gypsies offers the finest views of the Torre del Oro and La Giralda's silhouette gilded by slanting light.

days_plan.itin_title: Andalusia Road Trip: Seville, Córdoba, Granada & Ronda

visits.text: Contemplate the Tranco Reservoir, the heart of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. One of Spain's largest reservoirs storing Guadalquivir waters, its calm waters are suitable for swimming, kayaking, canoeing, and electric boat rides.

Visit the source of the Guadalquivir River, Andalusia's main river that rises in the Sierra de Cazorla. After traveling a 12-kilometer track, you reach a quite peculiar spot—not the typical rock but a powerful springtime watercourse surrounded by nature and native wildlife.

Enjoy Charco de la Pringue, a natural pool in the Natural Park whose name comes from the legend of a donkey that fell in loaded with oil. The area is equipped with a swimming zone with clear Guadalquivir waters, recreational area, restaurant, and hiking trails.

activities.text: Take in the views from the Félix Rodríguez de la Fuente Viewpoint, named in honor of the documentary the famous naturalist filmed about bearded vultures on the island of Bujaraiza Castle. From Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint at 1,200 meters, you can see the beginning of the Guadalquivir valley.

Hike the Borosa River and Cerrada de Elías route, one of the best hiking trails in the sierra. The 22-kilometer trail runs along wooden walkways beside the canyon to the Salto de los Órganos waterfall, Black Waters lagoon, and the source of the Borosa River.

Visit the Bearded Vulture Breeding Center in Nava de San Pedro, where work is being done to reintroduce this endangered bird that became extinct in Andalusia in 1986. Guides explain how the center operates and the breeding with minimal human intervention.

days_plan.itin_title: Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: The regional park of Sierra de Gredos protects 86,000 hectares of high mountains shaped by glacial erosion. Alpine lakes, cirques, and gorges compose this granite massif of the Central System.
Pico Almanzor rises to 2,592 meters, the highest summit in central Spain. Its steep north face dominates the Circo de Gredos, a rocky amphitheater sculpted by glaciers into a cathedral of stone.
The Laguna Grande de Gredos lies at 1,940 meters in the glacial cirque. Its crystalline waters reflect the granite walls and eternal snows of Almanzor in a mirror of mountain majesty.
The Elola refuge, built in 1917, welcomes hikers and climbers at the edge of Laguna Grande. Capacity of 65 places with meals and drinks available—a welcoming haven after the ascent.
The fauna counts over 230 vertebrate species including the Iberian ibex populating the heights, the Spanish imperial eagle, the black vulture, and the black stork—a wildlife paradise.

activities.text: Hike from the Plataforma de Gredos at 1,770 meters to the Laguna Grande. The trail climbs 200 meters through heathland then descends to the lake in 6 kilometers of stunning alpine scenery.
Continue to the Mirador del Circo de Gredos at 2,210 meters to embrace the panorama of the glacial amphitheater, Almanzor's walls, and the Laguna Grande below in a sweeping vista.
Watch for Iberian ibex that frequent the trails by the hundreds. Cows and livestock guardian dogs also graze in the alpine meadows protecting their herds from wolves.

days_plan.itin_title: The Castile and León Essentials
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

visits.text: Head north across the Alcarria plateau. After 45 minutes, stop at Cifuentes (population 2,000). This quiet town boasts a 14th-century castle built by Don Juan Manuel—a lettered prince, nephew of Alfonso X the Wise, and author of El Conde Lucanor. The castle, square-planned with round corner towers, dominates the Cifuentes valley. Below, the arcaded Plaza Mayor and the church of El Salvador (13th century), transitional Romanesque-Gothic, round out the visit. Allow 45 minutes.

Drive 30 minutes onwards to Sigüenza, one of Castile's hidden gems. Perched at 1,000 m altitude, this town of 4,500 souls, designated a Historic and Artistic Monument, is crowned by its 12th-century castle—now one of Spain's finest Paradores. Built on an Arab citadel which itself stood on a Roman site, it hosted the Catholic Monarchs and survived the Napoleonic Wars, Carlist Wars, and Civil War. Even if you're not staying overnight, climb up to see the cobbled courtyard, the 13th-century Romanesque chapel, and the vaulted dining hall.

The Cathedral of Santa María de Sigüenza is a fortress-cathedral—its two massive façade towers give it the air of a castle keep. Begun in 1124 after the reconquest from the Muslims, it blends Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance styles. But it's the tomb of the Doncel that justifies your pilgrimage. Martín Vázquez de Arce, a page to Isabella the Catholic who died in battle in 1486 aged 25, is sculpted in alabaster in a unique pose: not lying asleep like other recumbent effigies, but half-reclining, one leg crossed over the other, reading a book. It's one of the most celebrated funerary sculptures of Spanish Gothic, a symbol of wisdom wedded to strength.

activities.text: Take your time descending from the castle to Plaza Mayor through the cobbled lanes of Travesaña Alta, the medieval quarter. Plaza Mayor, laid out in the 15th century, is surrounded by arcades and noble blazoned mansions. Push on to the Casa del Doncel (15th century), the former residence of the Vázquez de Arce family, with its crenellated façade—it now hosts exhibitions. In the evening, the plaza's terraces come gently to life in the golden sunset light—Sigüenza is a town to savour at a slow pace.

If you have time the following morning before departing, the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park (10 km from Sigüenza) offers a limestone canyon with griffon vultures, golden eagles, and hiking trails between the cliffs. The village of Pelegrina, clinging to the canyon walls, is worth the detour.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon
Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

visits.text: The Sóller train has rattled along in varnished wooden carriages since 1912. It traverses 27 kilometers of Serra de Tramuntana between Palma and Sóller, threading through tunnels, over bridges, and around impossible curves.
Modernist Sant Bartomeu Church was restored by Joan Rubió i Bellver, a disciple of Gaudí. Its neo-Gothic facade and colored windows dominate Plaça Constitució.
The wooden tram links Sóller to the Port in 30 minutes. It meanders between orange groves, lemon trees, and traditional houses for 5 kilometers.
Port de Sóller curves around a protected bay. Its waterfront restaurants serve Sóller's red prawns and seafood paella.
Can Prunera—a modernist museum—occupies an elegant 1911 mansion. Its Nolla ceramics, ironwork, and stained glass illustrate the wealth of citrus merchants.
Can Prunera's gardens spread beneath palms and magnolias. An 18th-century pergola shades paved walkways and fountains.

activities.text: Boats depart for Sa Calobra and its vertiginous cliffs. The cove nestled at the base of a canyon offers turquoise waters and a pebble beach.
The Port's crystalline waters invite snorkeling. Fish, starfish, and posidonia meadows populate the rocky seabed.
The Sóller Valley has cultivated oranges and lemons since the 10th century. Orange sorbet is an unmissable specialty.
The village of Fornalutx perches just 10 minutes away. Ranked among Spain's most beautiful, its flower-draped lanes climb between stone houses.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

places.name: Soria

visits.text: The cloister of San Juan de Duero Monastery is one of Spain's strangest—and that's precisely what makes it unforgettable. Built in the 13th century by the Order of the Hospitallers of Jerusalem on the banks of the Duero, its four galleries each present a different arch style: Romanesque, Gothic, Islamic and interlaced horseshoe arches with no equivalent in the West. Some are inspired by Andalusian mosques, others by the Crusades. This unique architectural syncretism reflects exactly medieval Spain at the crossroads of cultures. Entry is free for EU citizens. On leaving, walk along the Duero to the Hermitage of San Saturio, carved into the rock in the 18th century over older caves—the Baroque interior is a surprise in this rock-hewn setting.

In Soria's historic center, the Co-Cathedral of San Pedro preserves a 12th-century Romanesque cloister with capitals carved with fantastic animals, vegetation and biblical scenes—three of its four original galleries still stand. Tradition has it that the Romanesque tower fell in 1531 because a city councilor asked an architect to remove a pillar that blocked the view of a statue of the Virgin. The Alameda de Cervantes Park, along the Duero, is the city's green lung with over 130 recorded plant species.

activities.text: Eight kilometers from Soria, the ruins of Numancia are a place steeped in history: here the Celtiberians heroically resisted the siege by Scipio Aemilianus's Roman legions for 14 months, before choosing collective death over surrender in 133 BC. The site on Cerro de la Muela features house reconstructions and an archaeological route. The Numantine Museum in town gathers objects found at the site.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Monasterio San Juan de Duero Soria

places.name: Sos Del Rey Catolico

visits.text: Head west (2h30) to reach Sos del Rey Católico, in the Cinco Villas comarca. Perched on a rocky promontory on the border between the ancient kingdoms of Aragón and Navarre, this fortified city witnessed the birth on March 10, 1452 of one of the most important figures in Spanish history: Ferdinand II of Aragón, the Catholic King, who, together with Isabella of Castile, unified Spain and financed Christopher Columbus's voyage. The village, classified as a Historic Site since 1968, is one of Aragón's best preserved - seven fortified gates still pierce the Gothic ramparts.

Begin at the Palacio de Sada, Ferdinand's birthplace, recognizable by its austere facade with coats of arms and crenellations. It now houses the tourist office and an interpretation center about the monarch. The church of San Esteban, the village's Romanesque jewel, preserves the baptismal font where Ferdinand was christened. But the real treasure is its 11th-century crypt, the oldest structure in the village, adorned with Gothic murals depicting scenes from Christ's life.

Lose yourself in the cobbled streets of the casco antiguo. The Jewish quarter (Judería), around the Plaza de la Sartén, retains its medieval atmosphere - about thirty families lived here in the 12th century before their expulsion in 1492 by the Catholic Monarchs. Climb up to Peña Feliciana castle (12th century), whose keep offers a panorama over the comarca's five villages and, on clear days, the Pyrenees. The Plaza de la Villa, with its Renaissance town hall and medieval market hall (Lonja), completes the visit.

activities.text: Enjoy Sos's tranquil atmosphere at day's end. The Parador, housed in a new building integrated into the ramparts, offers a terrace overlooking the village rooftops. For dinner, try Cinco Villas specialties: ternasco de Aragón (roast suckling lamb), migas, and artisanal sheep's cheese. Before leaving the village, stop by the Delfín Puente pastry shop for its handmade chocolates - a local institution.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

map_keywords: Palacio de Sada Sos del Rey Católico
Iglesia San Esteban Sos del Rey Católico
Castillo Peña Feliciana Sos del Rey Católico

visits.text: The Tabernas Desert stretches across 280 km², Europe's only true desert with less than 250mm of annual rainfall. The ravines, canyons, and badlands in ochre hues recall American West landscapes—which attracted filmmakers from the 1950s onward.

Fort Bravo preserves Europe's finest western film sets, built in the 1960s for Sergio Leone's films. The wooden Far West town, Mexican village, and military fort still serve as active filming locations. Stunt and shootout shows are performed on weekends.

Oasys MiniHollywood, on the filming locations of A Fistful of Dollars and The Good, the Bad and the Ugly, has been transformed into a theme park. The reconstructed western town, 800-animal wildlife reserve, and cactus garden round out the visit.

The Castillo de Tabernas, an 11th-century Arab fortress, dominates the village from its hill. Though in ruins but undergoing restoration, it offers panoramic views over the desert and the Río Andarax valley.
The Plataforma Solar de Almería, a solar thermal energy research center, takes advantage of the desert's 3,000 hours of annual sunshine. Its giant parabolic mirrors are visible from the road.

activities.text: Hike through the desert badlands on the GR-140 trail. From Oasys, a 9km route crosses plateaus, canyons, and ravines among eroded sandstone and clay formations.
Photography walk through the desert landscapes. The golden hours (sunrise and sunset) offer raking light that sculpts the relief and brings out the colors in geological strata.
Stargazing in the Tabernas Desert. Low light pollution and almost permanently clear skies make it one of Andalusia's best astronomical sites.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert

visits.text: Teide National Park sprawls across 19,000 hectares around the Las Cañadas caldera—a 17-kilometer-wide crater. A UNESCO site since 2007, it's the most visited park in Spain and Europe, drawing three million visitors annually.

The Roques de García thrust their rocky spires up from the volcano's base. Roque Cinchado, a precarious lava column balancing impossibly, is the park's defining image. The Mirador de la Ruleta offers sweeping views across the caldera.
The cable car departs from 2,356 meters and reaches La Rambleta at 3,555 meters in eight minutes. From the upper station, the Pico Viejo and La Fortaleza viewpoints are accessible without a permit.
At the Minas de San José, lava fields stretch endlessly beneath Teide. Variations in texture and color tell the volcano's geological story. Interpretive panels help decode the landscape.
The El Portillo Visitor Center presents exhibitions on the park's geology, fauna, and endemic flora: the Teide bugloss, which can reach 3 meters tall; the Teide violet; the blue chaffinch.

activities.text: Hike the Roques de García loop (Trail #3). The well-maintained path runs along the rock formations with constant views of Teide, then descends to the Llano de Ucanca plateau.
At over 2,000 meters elevation and far from any light pollution, Teide ranks among the world's finest stargazing sites. Guided telescope sessions begin after sunset.

Drive the road that crosses the park from north to south to appreciate the contrast between slopes: humid pine forests to the north, arid landscapes to the south. Stop at the many viewpoints along the way.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria

visits.text: Arrival in Teruel to conclude the itinerary. This small provincial capital (36,000 inhabitants) possesses Aragón's finest Mudéjar ensemble - four towers and the cathedral's timber ceiling are UNESCO World Heritage listed since 1986. Begin with the Torre de El Salvador (14th century), which you can climb for a panoramic view. Its structure is typical of Aragonese Mudéjar: an outer tower in brick decorated with green and white ceramics enveloping an older inner tower, with a vaulted passage between the two.

Step inside the Cathedral of Santa María de Mediavilla - you'll be amazed by its painted Mudéjar timber ceiling (13th century), nicknamed the 'Sistine Chapel of Mudéjar.' Over a surface 32 meters long, painted wood panels depict scenes from medieval daily life: craftsmen, animals, knights, as well as Islamic geometric motifs and human figures in a rare style. Look up at the cimborrio (octagonal lantern) - it's a masterpiece of fusion between Christian and Muslim traditions.

Stop at the Mausoleum of the Lovers of Teruel - it tells one of Spain's most famous love stories, the Spanish Romeo and Juliet, but three centuries earlier. Diego de Marcilla and Isabel de Segura, childhood sweethearts, were separated by Isabel's father who refused a suitor without fortune. Diego left to make his fortune and returned five years later, on the very day of Isabel's wedding to another. He died of grief asking for a kiss she refused; she in turn died the next day on his coffin. Look at the two alabaster tomb effigies - their hands reaching toward each other without touching convey rare emotion.

Finish at the Plaza del Torico, Teruel's living room, with its column topped by a tiny bull (the torico) - the city's symbol. The surrounding Modernist facades (including Casa El Torico from 1912) testify to an era of prosperity. The Escalinata, a monumental neo-Mudéjar staircase built in 1921 by architect José Torán, links the train station to the old town - its ceramics and ornamental bricks encapsulate Teruel's entire aesthetic.

activities.text: For a final Aragonese meal, Teruel is the city of jamón. Jamón de Teruel PDO was the first Spanish ham to receive protected designation of origin status (1984). The pigs are raised at altitude in the surrounding sierras, producing firm, aromatic meat. Order a ración of jamón with pan con tomate and a glass of Garnacha from Bajo Aragón - it's the perfect conclusion to this journey through Aragón.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel
Aragon Circuit: Zaragoza & Mudéjar Villages

visits.text: Discover the magnificent Toledo Cathedral, one of the most impressive in Spain with its blend of Gothic, Mudéjar and Renaissance styles. Don't miss the Chapel of the Kings, the famous gold Monstrance, the magnificent paintings in the Sacristy, and the Choir. An essential visit in the city of the three cultures.

Explore the imposing Alcázar of Toledo, a fortress dominating the city from above. Climb to one of its towers to enjoy a spectacular panoramic view of Toledo from the sky. It currently houses the Castilla-La Mancha Library and the Army Museum.

Visit the Santa Cruz Museum, a complex formed by the former Hospital of Santa Cruz and the Convent of Santa Fe. The Hospital building features an impressive courtyard, a marble staircase by Covarrubias, and enormous interior arches well worth contemplating.

Admire El Greco's masterpiece, 'The Burial of the Count of Orgaz', in the Church of Santo Tomé. This 16th-century painting is one of the jewels of Spanish art and masterfully represents the mysticism of the era with its composition divided between the earthly and celestial worlds.

activities.text: Wander through Toledo's Jewish Quarter, one of the most charming neighborhoods in the city. Visit the Synagogue of El Tránsito, home to the Sephardic Museum, and the Santa María la Blanca Synagogue with its filigree work and capitals that amaze everyone. Discover the legacy of Jewish culture in Spain.

Stroll through the convent district, where calm and silence reign. In some convents you can buy sweets handcrafted by the nuns. Discover the elevated passageways that the congregations used to move from one building to another unseen.

Visit the Mosque of Cristo de la Luz, the Puerta del Sol, and the Puerta de Bisagra. At sunset, take a taxi to the Parador de Toledo to enjoy superb panoramic views of the city during sunset—the best reward after a day of sightseeing.

days_plan.itin_title: The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains
Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums

places.name: Tordesillas

visits.text: A thirty-minute drive from Valladolid brings you to Tordesillas, perched on the banks of the Duero. This small town witnessed one of world history's most decisive moments: on June 7, 1494, Spain and Portugal signed the treaty that divided the world between the two crowns, drawing a line 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands. Everything west went to Spain, everything east to Portugal—this treaty explains why Brazil speaks Portuguese today. The Museo del Tratado, housed in the Casas del Tratado, recounts this episode through period documents and maps. The original treaty is listed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register.

The Real Monasterio de Santa Clara is Tordesillas's star monument. This Mudéjar palace, built in the 14th century by Alfonso XI and transformed into a convent by his son Peter the Cruel, showcases Mudéjar art—Christian art created by Muslim artisans—in all its splendor: carved stucco, azulejos, 15th-century Arab baths, and an arcaded patio reminiscent of the Alhambra. But another episode haunts these walls: Joanna I of Castile, called Joanna the Mad, was imprisoned here from 1509 to 1555—46 years of captivity ordered by her own father, Ferdinand of Aragon, then maintained by her son Charles V. She left the palace only to reach San Antolín church via an underground passage. The guided tour lasts about an hour.

Climb to the church of San Antolín, now converted into a museum of sacred art. From its tower—the 57 steps that Joanna the Mad climbed to show herself to her people, according to legend—the view over the Duero and the Castilian plain is superb. On your way down, wander the lanes around the Plaza Mayor: you'll notice modern artistic graffiti depicting historical scenes, including a face-off between Joanna and her son Charles V.

Cross the medieval bridge over the Duero—ten stone arches spanning the river since the 13th century. It's one of Castile's finest bridges. From the opposite bank, Tordesillas's silhouette with its bell towers and ramparts offers a highly photogenic view, especially late in the day.

activities.text: Tordesillas is renowned for its Castilian gastronomy: lechazo (milk-fed lamb), judiones (large white beans), and rosquillas (traditional doughnuts). Settle into one of the arcaded restaurants on Plaza Mayor for an unhurried lunch, as befits the rhythm of the Meseta.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Real Monasterio de Santa Clara Tordesillas

visits.text: Full day dedicated to Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park, UNESCO World Heritage since 1977 and Spain's second oldest national park (established 1918). The Monte Perdido massif (3,355 m) is Europe's highest limestone massif. Stay the night before in Torla, a Pyrenean village of stone and slate guarding the entrance to the Ordesa Valley. In the morning, reach Pradera de Ordesa (by car off-season, by shuttle from Torla in summer) and set off on the Cola de Caballo trail - 17.5 km round trip, 500 m elevation gain, 5 to 6 hours of hiking.

The trail ascends the Ordesa Valley along the Arazas River. You'll first pass through dense forests of beech and fir, then by the Arripas waterfall, the Estrecho waterfall (water plunges between two narrow walls into turquoise pools), and the Gradas de Soaso - stepped waterfalls of surreal beauty. At the end, the glacial cirque of Soaso opens onto Cola de Caballo (Horsetail), a waterfall over 50 meters high cascading in a fan from the cirque walls, with the snow-capped Monte Perdido as backdrop. Marmots accompany you for the final kilometers. This is one of the most beautiful hikes in the Pyrenees.

activities.text: Back in Torla at day's end, enjoy the village. Stone streets converge on the church of San Salvador (16th century) and the viewpoint over the valley. In the evening, restaurants serve Upper Aragón specialties: migas de pastor (fried breadcrumbs with garlic and chorizo), roast lamb, setas (wild mushrooms) in season. It's a well-earned meal after the hike.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

places.name: Toro

visits.text: Toro rises on a cliff above the Duero, 30 minutes west of Tordesillas. The Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor, begun in the late 12th century, is the major monument. Its model is Zamora Cathedral: a tower-lantern crowned by a ribbed dome, typical of the Duero valley Romanesque. But it's the Portada de la Majestad, the west portico completed around 1240, that alone justifies the journey: over 125 carved figurines and—exceptionally rare—they still preserve their original polychrome from eight centuries ago. It's the only intact polychrome Romanesque portico in Spain.

Tour Toro's Romanesque-Mudéjar churches: San Lorenzo el Real (12th century), the oldest, with its brick walls; El Salvador, San Pedro del Olmo, and the hermitage of Santa María de la Vega. Toro had 40 churches in the Middle Ages—about twenty remain, some in poetic ruin. The Alcázar, partially restored, offers a plunging view over the Duero and the vineyards below.

Descend to the medieval bridge spanning the Duero below the town. From the bridge, look up: Toro's silhouette perched on its cliff, with the collegiate's dome rising from the rooftops, is one of the most spectacular sights in Duero Castile.

activities.text: Toro is the heart of DO Toro, a wine appellation whose wines already traveled in Christopher Columbus's holds. The local grape, Tinta de Toro—a cousin of Tempranillo—grows on sandy soils that protected it from phylloxera in the 19th century. Some vines are pre-phylloxera, among Europe's oldest. Operators offer cellar visits with tastings. The town's subsoil hides over 300 medieval underground cellars—some can be visited.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Colegiata Santa María la Mayor Toro
Iglesia San Lorenzo el Real Toro

places.name: Tossa De Mar

visits.text: The Vila Vella of Tossa de Mar is the only intact medieval fortified town on the entire Catalan coast, listed as a historic monument in 1931. Its walls were built from 1186 to defend against Barbary pirates - Ramon de Berga, the abbot who governed the town, ordered their construction after centuries of raids. The perimeter includes three cylindrical towers and four keeps, including the Clock Tower (which housed the town's only public clock) and the Tower of Homage overlooking Codolar beach. Climb up to the lighthouse - built in 1917 on the site of the old castle - for the best coastal view.

Platja Gran stretches 400 meters at the foot of the medieval ramparts - it's one of the rare beaches in the world where you sunbathe beneath the walls of a 12th-century fortress. The waters are clear and sheltered. For more tranquility, walk 15 minutes to Cala Codolar, a small rocky nook nestled beneath the Vila Vella ramparts, ideal for snorkeling.

The Tossa Municipal Museum, founded in 1935 in the former governor's residence, is Spain's first contemporary art museum. It holds a painting by Marc Chagall - who stayed in Tossa and dubbed it 'blue paradise' - and Roman mosaics from the 1st-century BC villa dels Ametllers found on the hillside.

activities.text: Sea kayaking along the cliffs and coves between Tossa and Sant Feliu de Guíxols: the coast is carved with caves and small beaches accessible only by water. Local operators offer 2 to 4-hour guided outings. Allow 2 hours to paddle to Cala Giverola.

Snorkeling at Es Codolar cove or Cala Pola: the transparent waters of the Costa Brava allow you to see the bottom several meters deep. Posidonia (seagrass meadows) and sea urchins are the most common species. Bring your mask if you have one.

Dinner at the port with suquet de peix, a local fish stew simmered with potatoes and saffron - the house specialty in almost every waterfront restaurant. The region's white wine (Alella or Empordà) pairs perfectly with seafood.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

map_keywords: Platja Gran Beach Tossa de Mar

visits.text: Take the road east through the Alpujarras, passing through Pitres and Pórtugos. At Pórtugos, stop at the Fuente Agria - a spring rich in iron that stains the waterfall and surrounding rocks rust-orange. Locals attribute healing properties to it. It's a lovely 20-minute stop in a surprisingly verdant setting for Andalusia.

Trevélez is the highest village in mainland Spain, at 1,476 meters altitude, on the slopes of Mulhacén. The village is divided into three neighborhoods (barrios) - bajo, medio and alto - connected by steep cobbled lanes. The barrio bajo is the most touristy, the alto the most authentic. Here, the smell of ham is everywhere: Trevélez is famous throughout Spain for its jamón serrano, naturally cured for 14 to 24 months in drying rooms above 1,200 meters altitude, without any preservatives. The IGP designation "Jamón de Trevélez" dates back to 1862, when Queen Isabella II granted the village the privilege of stamping its hams with the royal seal after they won the national competition.

Continue to Válor, 30 minutes east of Trevélez. This discreet village carries a dramatic history: this is where Fernando de Córdoba y Válor was born, who took the Arabic name Abén Humeya claiming descent from the Umayyad caliphs of Córdoba. On Christmas Eve 1568, he led the last great Morisco revolt against Philip II. Within weeks, 25,000 armed men joined him and the insurrection engulfed the entire region. It took the intervention of Don Juan of Austria with 20,000 soldiers to crush the revolt in 1571. More than 80,000 Moriscos were subsequently deported across the peninsula. Every September, Válor reenacts this episode during its Moors and Christians festival.

activities.text: Visit a ham-drying facility in Trevélez - several producers open their doors to visitors. You'll see hundreds of legs suspended from the ceiling, smell the dry Sierra Nevada air doing the curing work, and taste slices cut right in front of you. The secret of this ham is the altitude: the cold, dry air of the Sierra Nevada, combined with the Mediterranean breeze, creates natural curing conditions that no cold storage facility can replicate.

If you're a hiker, Trevélez is the starting point for climbing Mulhacén (3,479 m), the highest peak of the Iberian Peninsula. The round trip via the Siete Lagunas trail takes 8 to 10 hours and requires good physical condition. For a gentler option, the trail along the río Trevélez through the chestnut groves is family-friendly.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras

visits.text: The equestrian statue of Francisco Pizarro (1929) dominates Plaza Mayor. Charles Rumsey's bronze depicts the conqueror of Peru, born in Trujillo in 1478.
The Castillo de Trujillo (9th century) crowns the hilltop. This Moorish fortress offers panoramic views across the plains stretching 30 kilometers in every direction.
The Iglesia de Santa María la Mayor blends Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance styles. Fernando Gallego's plateresque altarpiece is magnificent.
The Palacio del Marqués de la Conquista belonged to Hernando Pizarro. Its carved corner balcony displays busts of the Pizarro family and their Inca wives.
Step inside the Casa-Museo de Pizarro. This 15th-century mansion tells the conquistador's story through period documents and artifacts.
The Iglesia de San Martín (14th-16th century) combines Gothic and Renaissance. Two towers flank a single nave with an octagonal presbytery.
The Palacio de Orellana-Pizarro features a Renaissance portal and plateresque courtyard. It belonged to Francisco de Orellana, first European to navigate the Amazon.

activities.text: Climb to the castle for sunset. Golden light sets the tile roofs ablaze and the Renaissance palaces of Plaza Mayor glow below.
The local market sells Ibores cheese (goat cheese aged with pimentón), Iberian cured meats, and honey from the nearby sierras.
Settle into a terrace on Plaza Mayor at dusk. Migas extremeñas, moraga, and ibérico tapas pair with local wine.
The Festival Nacional del Queso transforms Plaza Mayor into a massive cheese market in late April-early May. Over 100,000 visitors sample cheeses from across Spain.

days_plan.itin_title: Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

visits.text: If Baeza is the ecclesiastical and university town, Úbeda is the noble and palatial one—the other half of the UNESCO inscription. It all begins at Plaza Vázquez de Molina, considered one of Spain's most beautiful Renaissance squares. Francisco de los Cobos y Molina, Secretary of State to Charles V, and his nephew Juan Vázquez de Molina, who succeeded him under Philip II, had an ensemble of palaces and chapels built here that rival anything in Tuscany. The architect Andrés de Vandelvira, a native of the region, designed most of the buildings—his influence would later be found in Latin American cathedrals.

The Sacra Capilla del Salvador, commissioned by Francisco de los Cobos as a private funerary chapel, is Vandelvira's masterpiece in Úbeda. Completed in 1559, its Plateresque façade displays extravagant sculptural richness—mythological scenes, grotesques, atlantes, the entire vocabulary of the Italian Renaissance translated into Andalusian stone. Inside, Vandelvira's sacristy is a jewel of proportion, with its caryatids and atlantes framing the windows. Berruguete's monumental altarpiece was partially destroyed during the Civil War, but what remains is impressive.

Opposite the chapel, the Palacio del Deán Ortega (16th century), now a Parador hotel, deserves a visit even if you're not staying there—go in to see the two-level arcaded Renaissance courtyard and the gallery with views over the olive groves. At the other end of the square, the Palacio de las Cadenas (Palace of the Chains), former palace of Juan Vázquez de Molina, now serves as the town hall. Its façade is a model of Spanish purist Renaissance.

Head down to the old quarter to discover the Sinagoga del Agua, discovered by chance during renovation work in 2007. This underground site comprises a prayer hall, a ritual bath (mikveh), a women's gallery, and a well—the most complete medieval synagogue ensemble found in Spain. The guided tour is fascinating and illuminates the life of Úbeda's Jewish community before the 1492 expulsion.

activities.text: The alfarero quarter (potters' quarter) of San Millán, in the lower part of town, perpetuates a pottery tradition dating back to Moorish times. The workshops produce pieces in emerald green on a white background—Úbeda's typical style. Push open a workshop door to see the artisans at work, and leave with a dish or jar as a souvenir. It's one of the few places in Spain where traditional pottery is still a living craft, not a tourist attraction.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: Sanctuary-fortress of Santa María of Ujué.
Viewpoints over middle Navarre.
Stone houses and vaulted passages.

activities.text: Circular hike around the village.
Tasting of migas from Ujué.
Nighttime stargazing.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre, Spain: Castles, Pyrenean Forests and the Camino de Santiago

visits.text: Drive east to enter Lower Aragón. First stop at Alcañiz (1h40 from Daroca), the second city of Teruel province. Climb to the Calatrava castle, a fortress of the military Order of Calatrava that has dominated the town since the 12th century - it now houses a Parador. The Gothic Lonja (market hall) from the 15th century, one of Aragón's finest, stands out with its three pointed arches opening onto the Plaza de España. The collegiate church of Santa María la Mayor and the medieval underground passages beneath the square complete the visit.

Continue 30 minutes east to reach Valderrobres, capital of the Matarraña comarca, nicknamed 'Aragonese Tuscany.' The arrival is spectacular: the medieval stone bridge spans the Río Matarraña and leads straight to the San Roque gate, one of seven gates in the old wall. At the village summit, the archbishop's castle-palace (14th-15th centuries) connects to the church of Santa María la Mayor, one of Aragón's finest examples of Levantine Gothic. Its sculpted facade and rose window are remarkably delicate.

If time permits, drive 15 minutes south to Calaceite, the other jewel of Matarraña. Classified as a Historic Site, this stone village with sloping streets and seigneurial houses in wrought iron has kept its Mediterranean character intact. The Plaza de España, the Renaissance town hall (1609), and the church of the Asunción form a harmonious ensemble. The Catalan influence - the border is just kilometers away - is felt in the architecture and gastronomy.

activities.text: In the evening, dine in Valderrobres. Matarraña's gastronomy is Mediterranean countryside cuisine: PDO Bajo Aragón olive oil, roast lamb, black truffle in season (Lower Aragón is one of Europe's top producers), goat cheeses, and Calanda peaches. Accompany your meal with a wine from the DO Bajo Aragón - powerful reds based on Garnacha.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

visits.text: City of Arts and Sciences — Calatrava's futuristic complex.
Central Market — dome and colorful stalls.
Old town — cathedral, Miguelete and silk exchange (Lonja, UNESCO).

activities.text: Bike ride in Turia park.
Traditional Valencian paella at noon.
Horchata and fartons aperitif at the Oceanogràfic.

days_plan.itin_title: Valencia's Art & Paella: City of Arts & Sciences & Albufera Wetlands

visits.text: Begin your exploration of Valladolid at the Museo Nacional de Escultura, one of Europe's most distinctive museums. Housed in the 15th-century College of Saint Gregory—an Isabelline-style masterpiece whose façade is a triumph of carved stonework—it holds over 6,000 pieces of polychrome wooden sculpture from the 15th to 18th centuries. You'll encounter works by the three great Castilian masters: Alonso Berruguete, pioneer of Spanish Mannerism who studied under Michelangelo in Florence; Juan de Juni, famed for his dramatic compositions; and Gregorio Fernández, whose Passion Christs are still carried in procession through Valladolid's streets every Holy Week. Don't miss Berruguete's Great Altarpiece of San Benito (16th century) or the 17th-century processional pasos. The museum spans three buildings—allow at least 2 hours, more if you're passionate about sacred art.

Stepping out of the museum, look up at the façade of San Pablo church, directly opposite. This Isabelline stone lacework, completed in the late 15th century, rivals that of the College of Saint Gregory. The interior is more austere but preserves an impressive Baroque altarpiece. This is where Philip II (1527) and Philip IV (1605) were baptized—Valladolid served as Spain's capital twice, under Charles V and briefly under Philip III between 1601 and 1606.

Walk down to the cathedral, designed by Juan de Herrera—the architect of El Escorial—in the late 16th century. The project was so ambitious it was never completed: only part of the nave and one tower were built. The interior, despite its unfinished appearance, houses a monumental altarpiece by Juan de Juni. Climb to the Diocesan Museum in the cloister for a fine collection of painting and sculpture.

Head next to the Plaza Mayor, one of Spain's first arcaded squares, laid out in the 16th century. This is where the Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella celebrated their wedding in 1469. Stroll beneath the porticoes, grab a coffee on a terrace, and observe the statue of Christopher Columbus at the center. The explorer died in Valladolid on May 20, 1506, convinced to the end that he had reached Asia. His remains have since traveled between Seville, Santo Domingo, Havana, and back to Seville—almost as much as the man himself during his lifetime.

Finish with a stroll through Campo Grande, Valladolid's great urban park—115,000 m² of greenery in the heart of the city. Peacocks roam freely, monumental fountains punctuate tree-lined paths shaded by century-old specimens. It's the perfect place to catch your breath before dinner.

activities.text: Valladolid is a city of tapas and wine. Head to the streets around Plaza Mayor and Calle Correos for a pinchos crawl—Castilian-style tapas. The local specialty is lechazo asado, milk-fed lamb roasted in wood-fired ovens, served in traditional asadores. Pair it with a glass of Ribera del Duero or Rueda, the province's two great appellations.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

visits.text: Valldemossa Charterhouse was built in 1309 as a royal residence for the asthmatic son of Jaume II. Converted to a monastery in 1399, it hosted Chopin and George Sand during the winter of 1838-1839.
Chopin and Sand's cells can be visited with the authentic Pleyel piano. The walls preserve memories of that winter which inspired the Preludes and 'A Winter in Majorca.'
The monastic pharmacy dates to 1722. Considered one of Europe's best-preserved, it displays jars, alembics, and medicinal plants once cultivated by the monks.
The church's turquoise bell tower is visible from afar. Its azulejo tiles depict Saint Catalina Tomàs, Majorca's patron saint, born in Valldemossa in 1531.
Cobbled lanes climb between stone houses with potted geraniums. Every facade displays a different azulejo illustrating the beata's life.
The Charterhouse gardens cascade in terraces. Orange, lemon, and almond trees offer panoramic views over the verdant valley.

activities.text: Serra de Tramuntana trails wind between centuries-old olive groves. The hike toward Deià crosses karst landscapes and dry-stone terraces.
Several olive oil estates offer tastings and tours. Majorcan olive oil has held protected designation status since 2002.
Coca de patata is dunked in thick hot chocolate. This local potato-based pastry is served in cafés around Plaça Cartuja.
The Chopin Festival runs each summer. Piano concerts resonate through the Charterhouse's Chopin Room from August through September.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

places.name: Vejer De La Frontera

visits.text: Vejer de la Frontera, declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble in 1976, reveals itself as you climb cobbled lanes to the 10th-11th century castle. Pass through its single horseshoe arch: two patios and ramparts offer plunging views over the Barbate valley and the distant sea.

Medieval ramparts snake 2 kilometers around the old town. Four gates survive: Arco de la Segur, Arco de Sancho IV, Arco de la Villa, and Puerta Cerrada. The latter, facing the Jewish quarter, was walled up for centuries to protect the town from North African pirates.

Plaza de España, the village heart, centers on a fountain adorned with multicolored Sevillian tiles. The 16th-century church of Divino Salvador, built atop the former mosque, retained the original minaret converted into its bell tower.

Head to Mirador de la Cobijada to discover the statue of this enigmatic figure: a woman wrapped in a black cloak revealing only one eye. This vestimentary tradition of Castilian origin, dating to the 16th century, has become Vejer's symbol. The viewpoint extends to Barbate's beaches and, on clear days, to Africa.

Vejer's seven windmills, built in the 1960s, stand on the windy ridge above the village. They've become the town's iconic image, offering panoramic views of the coast, El Palmar beach, and the rolling hills of La Janda.

Lose yourself in the old Jewish quarter, the judería, where vaulted passages and whitewashed houses press so close they nearly touch. The 18th-century Casa del Mayorazgo displays a patio overflowing with 450 flowerpots in a riot of color.

activities.text: The Barbate and Zahara de los Atunes region perpetuates bluefin tuna fishing by almadraba, a millennia-old technique inherited from the Phoenicians. From May to June, Vejer restaurants showcase tuna in every form: encebollado, tartare, tataki, grilled belly—celebrating this ancient harvest of the sea.

Descend to El Palmar beach, part of Vejer municipality, 8 kilometers from the village. This wild ribbon of sand pounded by Atlantic waves draws surfers and sunset lovers to one of the coast's most spectacular and unspoiled stretches.

Sample local gastronomy in a downtown restaurant: Vejer-style chicken pie, couscous with tuna and pumpkin, or retinta—meat from indigenous cattle raised free-range in the surrounding countryside, prized for its deep flavor and tenderness.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages

map_keywords: Castillo de Vejer de la Frontera
Plaza de España Vejer de la Frontera
Murallas de Vejer de la Frontera
Molinos de viento Vejer de la Frontera

places.name: Vigo

visits.text: The Casco Vello winds through cobbled lanes and traditional Galician townhouses. Plaza de la Constitución pulses at the heart of the old quarter.
Monte do Castro preserves the remains of a Celtic castro settlement from the 3rd century BC alongside 17th-century fortifications. Panoramic views sweep across the ría.
The Port of Vigo—Europe's largest fishing harbor—thrums with trawlers and cargo ships in constant motion along the working waterfront.
The Cíes Islands, reached by ferry from the port, unfurl white-sand beaches within the Atlantic Islands of Galicia National Park—once called "the Islands of the Gods" by the Romans.

activities.text: Kayak rental outfitters offer sea kayaks to paddle the protected ría. Calm waters and island vistas make for an easy, scenic paddle.
Calle de las Ostras in O Berbés lines up wooden tables piled with iced seafood. Slurp fresh estuary oysters with a squeeze of lemon.
Playa de Samil stretches nearly a mile of fine sand and shallow, calm water. Sunset paints the ría in molten gold and amber.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia Road Trip: Santiago de Compostela and the Coast of Death

map_keywords: Marché aux poissons O Berbés Vigo

visits.text: MACVAC occupies the Palau del Batlle, a 15th-century Valencian Gothic palace. Since 1972, it has exhibited over 700 works of contemporary art—including pieces by Miró—donated by the artists themselves.
The Roca Grossa dominates the village. This monumental pink limestone boulder supports several traditional houses and defines Vilafamés' visual identity.
Climb up to the castle perched at the summit. Conquered by James I in 1233, it blends Roman, Moorish, and Christian remains. The circular tower and cistern are its most striking features.
The lanes of Arab origin wind between ochre facades. The El Cuartijo quarter, enclosed by walls, preserves its medieval layout intact.
The Church of the Assumption dates from the late 16th century. Its baroque style is enhanced by frescoes by the Guilló family and a remarkable main altarpiece.
The Abric del Castell shelters UNESCO-listed schematic rock paintings. These panels with religious motifs bear witness to the prehistoric art of the Levant.

activities.text: Artistic cafés in the village attract painters and sculptors. Settle onto a terrace to watch visitors drift between galleries and workshops.
Every lane offers a photogenic scene. Pink stone houses, pots of geraniums, and vistas across the countryside compose natural tableaux.
Vilafamés produces wines of Castellón. Several village cellars offer tastings of Mediterranean grape varieties grown on the surrounding hillsides.
The Fuente de las Picas is a cascading fountain surrounded by ancient olive trees. The spot combines cool shade with panoramic views of the old town.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves

places.name: Villafranca Del Bierzo

visits.text: Villafranca del Bierzo owes its name to the French pilgrims who settled here in the 11th century at King Alfonso VI's invitation—'villa francorum' became Villafranca. The Cluniac monks the king brought from Occitania founded Santa María de Cluniaco and introduced viticulture to the valley. The Romanesque church of Santiago, built between the 12th and 13th centuries, is one of only two in the world—along with the Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela—to possess a Puerta del Perdón (Door of Pardon): pilgrims who could not physically continue received here the same indulgences as at Compostela. Pope Callixtus III granted this extraordinary privilege, acknowledging that the climb to the Puerto de Cebreiro was the Camino's most formidable stretch.

The castle of the Marquises of Villafranca, a 16th-century construction with massive lateral towers, dominates the town from above. Part is still inhabited—you can't visit inside, but the view from its surroundings over the confluence of the Burbia and Valcarce rivers is magnificent. In 1822, Villafranca briefly became capital of Bierzo province—a status that lasted only a decade.

Calle del Agua is the town's historic spine: an arcaded street lined with Renaissance and Baroque palaces, a 15th-century Mudéjar manor, and several convents. It's now an open-air heraldry museum—the coats of arms carved on façades tell five centuries of Bierzo noble families. The old town has been classified as a Cultural Heritage Site.

activities.text: Tasting Bierzo wines—the protected designation produces reds from mencía, a grape unique to this region, prized for its notes of red fruit and volcanic minerality. The vineyards cling to the valley's slate-covered slopes. Several producers offer tastings at their estates around Villafranca.

Dinner around Plaza Mayor to try botillo del Bierzo—a local butcher describes it as 'smoked pork ribs and backbone, stuffed into the stomach.' Served boiled with vegetables, it's the region's emblematic dish, recognized with protected geographical indication status.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

Collégiale Santa María de Cluniaco Villafranca del Bierzo
Calle del Agua Villafranca del Bierzo
Couvent San Francisco Villafranca del Bierzo

Medieval Quarter — houses with overhanging upper floors.
Anillo Verde parks.

activities.text: Bicycle loop on the green ring.
Creative vegetarian pintxos tasting.
Visit to a traditional cider house.

visits.text: Castle of Xàtiva — two fortresses on the ridge.
Old town — Via palace and collegiate church.
Fountain of 25 spouts — local symbol.

activities.text: Short hike to the castle viewpoints.
Tasting of dacsa shells.
Valencian ceramics workshop.

days_plan.itin_title: Valencia's Art & Paella: City of Arts & Sciences & Albufera Wetlands

places.name: Yaiza

visits.text: Timanfaya National Park is Lanzarote's volcanic heart—and one of Europe's most surreal landscapes. Between September 1, 1730 and April 16, 1736, dozens of volcanoes erupted successively for six years, burying 167 km² of the island, destroying 420 houses, and forcing half the population to emigrate. The 5,107-hectare park was created in 1974 to protect this intact lunar landscape. The visit begins with the Route of the Volcanoes: a 14 km bus circuit through craters, lava tunnels, and red ridges, with multilingual commentary. Arrive early—parking fills fast and the last Route of the Volcanoes entry is at 5 PM.

At Islote de Hilario, don't miss the geothermal demonstrations: staff thrust brushwood into a simple hole in the ground—it ignites within seconds. Then a bucket of water poured into a pipe erupts in a geyser of boiling steam, because the earth's crust is only two meters thick here and temperatures exceed 400°C below. At 27 meters depth, near the main crater, the thermometer reads 700°C. Since 2013, NASA astronauts have trained in this park—the basaltic soil geologically resembles Mars.

Restaurant El Diablo, designed by César Manrique above a natural volcanic chimney, is the world's only restaurant that cooks meat and fish using geothermal heat—at about 400°C, via a grill positioned over a five-meter pit. The volcanic stone and glass architecture blends into the landscape so seamlessly it's almost invisible from the road. Note that this restaurant doesn't take reservations.

activities.text: Dromedary ride at Echadero de Camellos, just outside the park gates. Camels were long essential to Lanzarote's agriculture, and a small museum on-site traces their history. The 20-minute ride takes you up the volcano flanks—an unexpected perspective on the sea of lava.

Detour to Los Hervideros in late afternoon: a stretch of volcanic coastline where Atlantic waves surge into caves and chimneys formed by the 1730-1736 lava flows. The effect is especially spectacular when the sea is rough. Just nearby, El Golfo's green lagoon—an emerald lake nestled in a former crater open to the sea—offers one of the island's strangest landscapes.

End the day in Yaiza, considered Lanzarote's prettiest village. Its flower-filled white houses and manicured streets contrast with the lava fields surrounding it. The Iglesia Nuestra Señora de los Remedios dates to the 17th century. It's also the best sunset viewpoint over the Montañas del Fuego—the red light on black lava is stunning.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

map_keywords: Parc national Timanfaya Montañas del Fuego
Iglesia Nuestra Señora de los Remedios Yaiza

places.name: Zafra

visits.text: Zafra is nicknamed "little Seville" for its white streets, flower-filled patios, and Andalusian atmosphere—but it's stayed far quieter than its big neighbor. Start with the Alcázar de los Duques de Feria, a 15th-century fortress flanked by nine crenellated towers, now converted to a Parador. Hernán Cortés stayed here before sailing off to conquer Mexico. Even if you're not spending the night, step inside to see the arcaded interior courtyard and coffered ceilings—Parador staff are used to curious visitors.

Zafra has two adjoining squares forming the town's heart. Plaza Grande, vast and airy, is lined with porticoes sheltering cafés and terraces. Plaza Chica, more intimate, was the medieval market site—the stone columns bordering it still bear the marks of standard measures carved by merchants to verify weights and lengths. The covered passage connecting the two squares is a charming detail.

The Colegiata de la Candelaria, from the 15th century, is Zafra's main church. Its main altarpiece is impressive and its position on Plaza Grande makes it a natural landmark. Just next door, the Convento de Santa Clara (15th century) shelters remarkable altarpieces and wrought-iron grilles.

Enter Zafra through the Puerta de Jerez, one of Extremadura's finest town gates. From there, wander the historic center's streets, declared a Bien de Interés Cultural—the whitewashed palaces, wrought-iron balconies, and patios glimpsed through half-open doors justify the town's nickname. In the evening, the atmosphere on Plaza Grande's terraces is delightful.

Dedicate the day to Olivenza, 85 km northwest of Zafra. It's one of Spain's most unusual places: a Portuguese town on Spanish soil. Founded by the Templars in the 13th century, it remained Portuguese for five centuries (1297–1801) before being annexed by Spain during the brief War of the Oranges. Portugal has never recognized this annexation—officially, Olivenza is still claimed by Lisbon. Result: black-and-white Portuguese-style cobblestones, Manueline architecture, and azulejos coexist with Spanish signage. Since 2010, street names are posted in both languages.

The Igreja de Santa María Magdalena is Olivenza's masterpiece and the finest example of Manueline architecture outside Portugal—after Lisbon's Jerónimos Monastery. Commissioned by King Manuel I in 1510 and completed in 1560, it has three naves resting on eight twisted granite columns typical of Manueline style. The walls are covered with 18th-century blue-and-white azulejos, and six baroque altarpieces complete the ensemble. In 2012, Spanish internet users voted it "Spain's most beautiful corner"—a delicious paradox for an utterly Portuguese monument.

Olivenza's castle, built by the Templars then reinforced under Portuguese kings, has a keep tower 37 meters high—the tallest on the entire Spanish-Portuguese border. Erected in 1488 on flat terrain, it had to compensate through height what the land didn't provide in natural defense. It's accessed by a ramp (no stairs)—so it's accessible to all. From the top, the panorama sweeps across the plain to the Guadiana and Portugal. The castle also houses the Museo Etnográfico González Santana, one of Extremadura's richest ethnographic museums.

Stop by the Santa Casa de la Misericordia, founded in 1501 by King Manuel I himself. The chapel's interior walls are covered with 18th-century Portuguese azulejos depicting Old and New Testament scenes—a remarkable collection. The town hall on Plaza de la Constitución preserves a Manueline doorway that's the town symbol.

activities.text: Zafra sits at the heart of Tierra de Barros, Extremadura's main wine region. Tour operators offer bodega visits in the area, especially toward Almendralejo (30 km), the region's "wine capital." Extremadura wines, long underrated, have improved tremendously—try reds from tempranillo and garnacha, and whites from pardina and cayetana.

If you prefer staying in town, Zafra hosts a lively flea market on Thursdays in Plaza Grande, a direct legacy of the medieval fairs that made the town prosperous. The Feria Internacional Ganadera (livestock fair) in September–October is one of Lower Extremadura's major events—if you're there then, don't miss it.

Olivenza is a gastronomic border post: you'll eat migas extremeñas alongside bacalhau Portuguese-style, and convent pastries blend traditions from both countries. Lunch in the historic center and taste both cuisines—it's the only place in Spain where that's possible in the same village.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas
Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

map_keywords: Alcázar de los Duques de Feria Zafra

places.name: Zamora

visits.text: Zamora's cathedral is nicknamed 'The Pearl of the Duero'—and once you see its scaled stone dome, you'll understand why. Built between 1151 and 1174, consecrated in 1175, it's the oldest cathedral in Castile and León. Its cimborrio (lantern tower) with sixteen double arches showing Byzantine, Islamic and French Romanesque influences is a unique creation in Spanish Romanesque art. Inside, don't miss the Cathedral Museum which houses one of the world's finest collections of 15th-century Flemish tapestries, from the workshops of Arras, Tournai and Brussels. Check opening times before coming, as the cathedral closes during certain exhibition periods.

Zamora has over twenty Romanesque churches in the old town—making it the densest concentration in Europe according to several art historians. Follow the Romanesque circuit from Plaza de Viriato: the church of La Magdalena, with its carved doorway and oriental sepulcher, then Santiago de los Caballeros, where tradition places El Cid's knighting by Ferdinand I. The Mirador del Troncoso, at the end of the ramparts, gives panoramic views over the Duero and the plains of Castile.

Zamora's ramparts extend over a kilometer along the rocky promontory overlooking the Duero. Built in several phases from the 9th century onward, they're among the best preserved in Spain. Look for the Portillo de la Traición—a small gate in the wall through which, according to legend, the assassin of King Sancho II was introduced during the siege of Zamora in 1072. This is the origin of the Spanish proverb 'Zamora wasn't taken in an hour.'

activities.text: Late afternoon, head down to Playa de Los Pelambres on the Duero's banks to view the silhouette of the cathedral and castle from the opposite shore. The medieval stone bridge, crossing point of the Via de la Plata—the Roman road that linked Mérida to Astorga—holds one of Spain's oldest sets of aceñas (water mills). This is the best angle for photographing the historic ensemble.

places.name: Zaragoza

visits.text: The Basilica del Pilar, Spain's largest baroque sanctuary, stretches 130 meters along the Ebro riverbank. Its eleven domes and four towers dominate the Plaza del Pilar. Inside, two cupolas bear frescoes by the young Goya, a native of the region: the Adoration of the Name of God and Regina Martyrum.

Take the elevator to the top of the Pilar tower, 62 meters high: the panorama sweeps across Zaragoza's rooftops, the ribbon of the Ebro, and distant mountain ranges. Below, the Holy Chapel houses the jasper column where the Virgin is said to have appeared to the apostle James in the year 40 AD.

La Seo del Salvador, Zaragoza's oldest cathedral, layers seven centuries of architecture on the site of the ancient Roman forum and later mosque. Its exterior Mudéjar wall, a UNESCO World Heritage site, is a tapestry of brick and glazed ceramics in geometric patterns. Inside, the Gothic polychrome alabaster altarpiece ranks among Europe's finest.

The La Seo Tapestry Museum preserves one of the world's finest collections of Flemish tapestries, woven between the 15th and 17th centuries. Biblical and mythological scenes unfold in remarkable condition.

The Aljafería Palace, built in the 11th century as the "Palace of Joy" by King Al-Muqtadir, is the northernmost Islamic palace in Europe. Its polylobed arches, the Patio de Santa Isabel with its orange trees, and the Golden Hall form—together with the Alhambra and Córdoba's Mosque—the triad of Hispano-Muslim architecture.

Cross the Puente de Piedra, a 15th-century stone bridge spanning the Ebro. From the opposite bank, the view of the Basilica del Pilar reflected in the river composes Zaragoza's most iconic image, especially striking at dusk.

activities.text: Dive into El Tubo, the labyrinth of narrow downtown alleys dedicated to tapas. Each bar has its specialty: borage in béchamel, Aragonese migas, ternasco lamb. The ritual: one glass of wine and one tapa per counter, then move on.

The Fountain of Hispanidad, on Plaza del Pilar, represents Latin America in the shape of an inverted map. In the evening, the square comes alive and the illuminated basilica reflects in the fountain's basin.

Every October 12th, the Fiesta del Pilar transforms Zaragoza into a week of processions, giant floral offerings to the Virgin, concerts, and fireworks. Off-season, the landscaped banks of the Ebro invite strolls among bridges and gardens.
